Summary of what was found: L’Oréal formally organizes its portfolio into Consumer Products, Luxe, Dermatological Beauty, and Professional Products, supporting premium/luxe and mass/consumer as distinct packaging-evaluation tracks.
Detailed description of what was found: The 2025 annual report lists Consumer Products and Luxe as separate divisions alongside Dermatological Beauty and Professional Products. This supports the report’s structure because one of the world’s largest beauty companies already uses organizational and financial segmentation that mirrors the proposed packaging framework. It supports making Premium/Luxe and Mass-Market/Consumer the primary material-evaluation tracks, while treating dermocosmetics and professional/salon as narrower secondary categories.
Source name: L’Oréal Annual Report 2025 — L’Oréal Finance — https://www.loreal-finance.com/en/annual-report-2025/

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Luxe defines itself around luxury beauty, elevated services, emotions, and desirability, supporting the view that premium packaging materials serve symbolic and sensory value beyond protection.
Detailed description of what was found: The Luxe Division describes itself as a leader in luxury beauty and emphasizes unique luxury experiences, elevated services, emotions, desirability, and refill as part of redefined beauty rituals. This supports the report’s argument that premium/luxe packs are evaluated partly on emotional, tactile, and experiential performance; therefore, lightweighting in this tier must protect premium cues rather than optimize only for grams or cost.
Source name: L’Oréal Groupe: Luxe Division — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/luxe-division/

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Consumer Products emphasizes value for money, broad reach, operational scale, and sustainable mass beauty, supporting a distinct mass-market material-selection logic.
Detailed description of what was found: The Consumer Products Division states that it creates beauty for everyone, everywhere, every day, reaches more than one billion consumers, and remains committed to delivering best value for money. It also highlights refill formats such as L’Oréal Paris Elvive refill packs with plastic-saving claims. This supports the mass-market track because it ties the segment to scale, affordability, execution, and material reduction—priorities that typically favor lighter, lower-cost, recyclable, and easier-to-manufacture packaging systems.
Source name: Consumer Products Division — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/consumer-products-division/

Summary of what was found: Coty separates Prestige and Consumer Beauty and explicitly contrasts prestige, mass fragrance, and mass cosmetics across a wide price range.
Detailed description of what was found: Coty’s press release discusses integrating Prestige Beauty and Mass Fragrance while separately reviewing Consumer Beauty, which includes mass cosmetics, mass skin, and personal care. Its financial reporting also separates Prestige and Consumer Beauty revenue pools and notes different pressures, including supply-chain cost issues particularly in Consumer Beauty. This supports the report because it shows that a major beauty player operates with explicit prestige-versus-consumer economics, channel logic, and portfolio expectations that should translate into different lightweight material criteria.
Source name: Coty Announces Plans to Bolster Its Leading Position in Fragrance and Launches a Strategic Review of Its Consumer Beauty Business; Coty Announces Third Quarter Fiscal Year 2026 Results — Coty — https://www.coty.com/news/coty-announces-plans-to-bolster-its-leading-position-in-fragrance-and-launches-a-strategic-review-of-its-consumer-beauty-business ; https://www.coty.com/news/coty-announces-third-quarter-fiscal-year-2026-results

Summary of what was found: Circana tracks prestige and mass beauty as separate retail markets with different scale and pricing profiles, validating brand tier as an industry-standard segmentation.
Detailed description of what was found: Circana reports U.S. prestige beauty and mass beauty as separate retail markets with different sales scale and average-selling-price dynamics. This validates the use of prestige/mass segmentation for packaging-material analysis. It also implies different pack economics: prestige can absorb more material cost and sensory investment, while mass-market operates at larger scale where small unit-level packaging changes can create larger cost and logistics consequences.
Source name: US Prestige & Mass Beauty Retail Performance 2025 — Circana — https://www.circana.com/post/us-prestige-and-mass-beauty-retail-deliver-a-positive-performance-in-2025-circana-reports

Summary of what was found: Circana describes mass beauty as driven by accessible price points and efficacy, while prestige is driven by innovation, elevated formats, and experiential appeal; it also identifies masstige as a bridge segment.
Detailed description of what was found: Circana’s analysis says mass beauty gains momentum as consumers seek high-performing products at accessible price points, while prestige is defined by innovation, elevated formats, and experiential appeal. It also states that masstige skincare brands offer premium benefits at mass prices. This source is useful because it directly frames the consumer logic behind packaging divergence: mass packs need to signal value and performance efficiently, whereas prestige packs rely more on format elevation and experience. It also supports mentioning masstige as a secondary category without displacing the premium-versus-mass core structure.
Source name: U.S. Beauty Market Trends: Consumers Seeking Affordable Alternatives — Circana — https://www.circana.com/post/us-beauty-consumers-are-driven-by-value-based-purchase-behavior

Summary of what was found: McKinsey provides a beauty price-tier ladder including mass, masstige, entry prestige, prestige, and luxury, supporting a two-track report with middle tiers acknowledged.
Detailed description of what was found: McKinsey states that mass beauty is the largest price tier and identifies masstige, entry prestige, prestige, and luxury as higher-price tiers. This is strong evidence for the segmentation logic because it provides an external, recognized tier framework. It supports keeping the report broad enough to mention masstige and entry prestige, but focused enough to consolidate the analysis into two main packaging-material tracks: Premium/Luxe and Mass-Market/Consumer.
Source name: The Beauty Industry Boom: Can Growth Be Maintained? — McKinsey & Company — https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/consumer-packaged-goods/our-insights/the-beauty-boom-and-beyond-can-the-industry-maintain-its-growth

Summary of what was found: McKinsey illustrates how premium packaging can become over-engineered through high-end boxes and nonrecyclable inserts before the consumer reaches the primary pack.
Detailed description of what was found: McKinsey’s packaging article uses a premium-packaging example involving a high-end box and disposable materials such as cellophane or foam before the consumer reaches the bottle. This supports the report’s argument that premium beauty often carries extra secondary-packaging and presentation layers, which can add weight and material complexity for experiential reasons. It explains why premium/luxe lightweighting must preserve unboxing and symbolic cues while reducing unnecessary material burden.
Source name: Packaging: A Key Performance and Value Driver — McKinsey & Company — https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights/packaging-the-underrated-performance-and-value-driver

Summary of what was found: Shiseido distinguishes Prestige from Premium and also lists Inner Beauty and Life Quality Beauty, supporting secondary category overlays around the premium-versus-mass split.
Detailed description of what was found: Shiseido defines Prestige as high-end, high-added-value cosmetics and fragrances sold mainly through department stores and specialized cosmetic stores, while Premium is defined as mid-priced cosmetics sold mainly in drugstores and general merchandise stores. It also lists Inner Beauty and Life Quality Beauty. This provides a clean source-backed taxonomy for the middle ground between prestige and mass and for adjacent categories that may need to be acknowledged in the report. It supports using prestige/luxe and consumer/mass as the primary tracks while treating premium/masstige-like and special-needs categories as secondary variants.
Source name: BRANDS — Shiseido Company — https://corp.shiseido.com/en/brands/

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Dermatological Beauty provides a source-backed example of dermocosmetics as a distinct but secondary category focused on skin health, efficacy, and professional trust.
Detailed description of what was found: L’Oréal says Dermatological Beauty sits at the crossroads of beauty and health, is a dermocosmetic market leader, and serves needs ranging from skin pathologies to aesthetics. It also mentions accessible refill solutions. This supports the report because dermocosmetics can have different formulation-protection and trust cues than mainstream beauty, but they do not need to displace the two main tracks. They can be treated as an overlay focused on efficacy, safety, and clinical credibility.
Source name: Dermatological Beauty Division — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/dermatological-beauty-division/

Summary of what was found: P&G states that packaging must protect products, delight consumers, reduce plastic, create transportation efficiencies, and scale circularity through recyclable or reusable formats.
Detailed description of what was found: P&G’s ESG waste page states that superior packaging protects, ships, and supports product use, while new packaging solutions should delight consumers, reduce plastic, create transportation efficiencies, and reuse materials. It also describes progress through recycled plastic, lightweight designs, replacement of plastic with renewable materials, and recyclable/reusable packaging. This is directly relevant to the mass-market packaging track because it connects material selection to manufacturability, logistics, at-scale recyclability, and system feasibility—not only aesthetics.
Source name: ESG — Environmental — Waste — Procter & Gamble Investor Relations — https://www.pginvestor.com/esg/environmental/waste/default.aspx

Summary of what was found: Unilever’s plastics strategy emphasizes lighter packaging, recycled content, rigid-versus-flexible recyclability realities, reusable/refill models, and alternatives that work in practice and at scale.
Detailed description of what was found: Unilever reports progress against reusable, recyclable, or compostable packaging goals and distinguishes rigid and flexible plastic recyclability realities. It says it is reducing virgin plastic by using recycled plastic, designing lighter packaging, and scaling alternative materials and models that work in practice and at scale. This strongly supports the mass/consumer packaging track because it shows how a scaled consumer-goods company evaluates materials through operational recyclability, infrastructure compatibility, and deployment economics, often ahead of prestige-style sensory cues.
Source name: How Unilever Is Tackling Plastic Waste — Unilever — https://www.unilever.com/sustainability/plastics/

Summary of what was found: Aptar links mono-material dispensing systems with recyclability, showing why mass and consumer packs often favor material simplification.
Detailed description of what was found: Aptar’s Future pump is described as a polyethylene-only dispensing system developed so that the pump aligns with common bottle material streams. Aptar states that traditional pumps can contain multiple materials, including metal, which complicates recycling, while mono-material design makes the complete packaging easier to recycle. This supports the report’s material-selection implication that efficiency-led packaging programs should simplify component materiality, align closures with bottle streams, and reduce separation burdens. This is highly relevant for mass-market packs and for lightweighting pathways that must preserve high throughput and recycling compatibility.
Source name: Aptar Beauty Introduces Future: a Fully Recyclable Mono-Material Pump — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/aptar-introduces-a-fully-recyclable-mono-material-pump

Summary of what was found: Bormioli Luigi shows that premium beauty still values decoration, refillability, and glass aesthetics, while combining those demands with lighter walls, improved weight-to-strength ratio, and PCR content.
Detailed description of what was found: Bormioli Luigi says leading beauty brands are embracing refillable bottles whose decorative elements underscore value and extended lifespan. It also says it is optimizing weight-to-strength ratio and glass composition, offering lighter, thinner-walled premium solutions while preserving design rituals and customization. This demonstrates that premium/luxe lightweighting is real, but differs from mass lightweighting: the solution must preserve prestige codes, decoration potential, formula compatibility, and long-life or refill value.
Source name: Bormioli Luigi Invests in Innovative Eco-Conscious Packaging — Bormioli Luigi — https://bormioliluigi.com/beauty/en/news/104/bormioli-luigi-invests-in-innovative-eco-conscious-packaging

Summary of what was found: Quadpack links tactile finishes, emotional connection, craftsmanship, luxury codes, and eco-design, supporting the role of material feel and storytelling in premium packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack’s BeautyWood 2026 release says wooden beauty packaging focuses on tactile finishes that create emotional connection, with forms aligned to luxury codes and eco-design principles. It also notes sustainably managed wood and waste-minimizing manufacturing methods. This source explains why premium packaging may use materials such as wood, glass-like plastics, decorative components, or metal accents: they communicate tactility, craftsmanship, and narrative value. It also shows how premium lightweighting may rely on premium-feel, lower-impact substitutions rather than straightforward downgauging.
Source name: Touch Flow Feel – the Sensory Appeal of Wood for Beauty — Quadpack — https://www.quadpack.com/assets/14383132/touch-flow-feel-the-sensory-appeal-of-wood-for-beauty/

Summary of what was found: Berlin Packaging’s resin guide shows why PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP form the mainstream rigid and squeezable-material baseline for beauty and personal care primary packs.
Detailed description of what was found: The source states that PET offers gloss, clarity, impact resistance, and is desirable for clear bottles and jars; LDPE is flexible and suitable for squeezable packaging such as shampoo, conditioners, and lotions; HDPE is widely used for extrusion-blown bottles because it is economical, impact resistant, and offers a moisture barrier; and PP provides moisture barrier and high-temperature stability. This supports using PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP as baseline material families for later lightweighting comparisons in bottles, jars, tubes, and closures.
Source name: About Plastic Resins for Packaging — Berlin Packaging — https://www.berlinpackaging.com/about-plastic-resins/

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging confirms the current beauty primary-packaging material set: PP, PE, PET, PETG/PCTA/PCTG, PMMA/acrylic, AS/SAN, ABS, aluminum, and glass.
Detailed description of what was found: This source maps materials directly to cosmetic packaging use. It explains PP as heat and chemical resistant; PE as ranging from soft and flexible to hard and sturdy; PET as clear and chemically resistant; PETG/PCTA/PCTG as offering above-average chemical resistance and clarity compared with clarified PP; PMMA/acrylic as lightweight, shatter-resistant plastic glass commonly used as an outer jar or bottle; AS/SAN as rigid, strong, tough, and transparent; ABS as high-gloss, impact resistant, and easy to decorate; aluminum as luxurious and protective; and glass as highly chemically resistant with strong barrier properties. It supports the report’s format-material baseline and premium-versus-mass distinction.
Source name: Packaging Materials For Cosmetic Containers — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/custom-services/materials/

Summary of what was found: Quadpack explains that PET injection molding enables greater wall thickness than blow molding to create rigid beauty packs with a luxurious look and feel.
Detailed description of what was found: This source connects PET to premium-style execution rather than only mass-market packaging. Quadpack states that blow molding is used for thin-walled products, while injection molding allows greater wall thicknesses to create products with a luxurious look and feel. It also notes PET compatibility with many formulations and describes constraints when moving to rPET due to shape and aesthetic factors. This makes heavy-wall PET a major premium primary-packaging baseline and a likely lightweighting target.
Source name: The art of PET injection moulding — Quadpack — https://www.quadpack.com/assets/13873460/the-art-of-pet-injection-moulding/

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Cloud Jars show that premium skincare jars still use heavy, thick-wall glass to deliver a high-end aesthetic.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack describes Cloud Jars as single-wall glass jars with a premium look, heavy glass construction, and thick-wall glass for a high-end finish. It also mentions compatibility with creams and moisturizers and multiple decoration options. This is strong evidence that heavy glass jars remain a current baseline in prestige skincare and represent a high-weight starting point for lightweight-material substitution analysis.
Source name: Cloud Jars — Quadpack — https://catalogue.quadpack.com/product/14128079/cloud-jars

Summary of what was found: Ball’s Infinity aluminum bottle line confirms that aluminum bottles are used in beauty and personal care and can combine lightweighting, monomateriality, customization, and a premium aesthetic.
Detailed description of what was found: Ball states that its impact-extruded aluminum bottle line is customizable for beauty and personal care products, monomaterial, and lightweight, saving transport energy. The product positioning also frames aluminum bottles as sophisticated and high-end. This supports including aluminum bottles in the primary-packaging baseline for selected beauty and personal care applications, especially as a lighter alternative to glass with premium design cues.
Source name: A Circular Alternative to Plastic Packaging — Ball Corporation — https://investors.ball.com/news-presentations/press-releases/detail/165/a-circular-alternative-to-plastic-packaging

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Trap PET Jar shows a common mixed-material jar architecture: PET jar, PP cap, and PE shive.
Detailed description of what was found: This source gives an explicit beauty jar bill of materials: jar in PET, cap in PP, and shive in PE. It also states that virgin PET is ideal for highlighting formula texture, while PCR PET is more convenient for solid-color applications such as supplements and nutricosmetics. This is strong evidence that many current jars are not mono-material and that PET/PP/PE combinations are common in beauty jars, which matters for both weight and recyclability trade-offs.
Source name: Trap PET Jar — Quadpack — https://catalogue.quadpack.com/product/13974131/trappet-jars

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Regula Airless shows that premium-looking airless packs often combine multiple rigid plastics, including SAN, PET or rPET, and PP, plus a pump system.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack describes Regula Airless as popular among beauty brands because of its premium look and high-quality performance. It specifies a double-wall architecture with the outer bottle and cap in SAN, PET, or rPET and the inner in PP, plus a metal-free pump. This supports using multi-resin airless packs as a current baseline format for premium skincare and highlights where lightweighting or material simplification efforts may focus.
Source name: Regula Airless — Quadpack — https://catalogue.quadpack.com/pack-types/airless/14020807/regula-airless

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s GSA Advance shows a recyclable next-generation lotion pump made as an all-polyolefin system using PP and PE for skin care, personal care, and color cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Aptar describes GSA Advance as an all-plastic lotion pump and all-polyolefin pump made from polypropylene and polyethylene, designed to maximize recyclability while maintaining performance and design options from premium to lifestyle. This clarifies dispenser material reality: many dispensers are multi-component systems, while the future direction is PP/PE-based mono-family construction aligned with bottle materials.
Source name: GSA Advance — Aptar Beauty — https://beauty.latamcatalog.aptar.com/en/product/gsaadvance/

Summary of what was found: Silgan Dispensing states that conventional dispensers contain non-recyclable metals and materials and contrasts them with 100% polyolefin, no-metal-spring systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Silgan says its LifeCycle technology removes non-recyclable metals and materials used in conventional dispensers, enabling packs to be made nearly completely from a single polyolefin. It also says ReVive 2cc has no metal spring and improves compatibility with PET, HDPE, and PP bottle recycling, while Pearl 2 uses a patented plastic spring and 100% polyolefin design. This supports treating pumps and dispenser mechanisms as a distinct hotspot in lightweighting and mono-material redesign.
Source name: CMPL Expo 2026 — Silgan Dispensing Systems — https://silgandispensing.com/cmpl-expo/

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer’s cosmetic glass dropper catalogue confirms glass as a current standard for serum/oil droppers and provides actual pack weights by capacity.
Detailed description of what was found: Gerresheimer lists cosmetic glass dropper bottles from 5 ml to 100 ml and shows weights rising from 20 g at 5 ml to 47 g at 30 ml, 55 g at 50 ml, and 100 g at 100 ml. This provides concrete evidence that glass droppers are weight-intensive primary packs and should be priority candidates for lightweighting analysis, especially in prestige skincare categories where glass droppers are common.
Source name: Dropper bottle for specialities made of moulded glass — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/catalogue/products/cosmetics-fragrance/glass-packaging/others/dropper-bottles.html

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging’s mono-material dropper shows that traditional droppers are typically multi-material and often use glass, while APC’s redesign uses enhanced PP for both bottle and pipette.
Detailed description of what was found: APC states that typical market droppers are multi-material and non-recyclable and that its redesign replaces the usual glass-based approach with an enhanced polypropylene bottle and pipette that can be recycled as one unit. This supports using glass bottle plus mixed-material dropper as the current baseline and all-PP dropper systems as a lightweight, recyclability-oriented future benchmark.
Source name: Mono-Material Dropper with Bottle - Recycle Code 5 — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/mono-material-dropper/

Summary of what was found: Berlin Packaging shows why squeeze tubes remain common in beauty and personal care: they are lightweight, durable, leak-resistant, portable, and easy to decorate.
Detailed description of what was found: Berlin says brands choose plastic squeeze tubes for durability, resistance to breakage and leakage, lightweighting, dispensing ease, product protection, shelf life, and customizable graphics. It also notes that recyclable mono-material toothpaste-style tubes use multiple layers of HDPE with EVOH rather than an aluminum foil layer, and that tube work can be done in mono-material PP or HDPE constructions. This supports PE/HDPE-based tubes as a mainstream lightweight route while flagging barrier-layer complexity as a design constraint.
Source name: The Latest Twists and Turns in Plastic Squeeze Tubes — Berlin Packaging — https://www.berlinpackaging.com/insights/product-categories/latest-twists-and-turns-in-plastic-squeeze-tubes

Summary of what was found: ALLTUB confirms that aluminum squeeze tubes remain a mainstream option for cosmetics needing high barrier and hygiene.
Detailed description of what was found: ALLTUB states that aluminum tubes have exceptional protective barrier properties, including airtightness, no ingress of light and UV rays, heat resistance, and corrosion resistance, and that they are widely used for pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and foods. The companion market page adds that aluminum and laminate tubes are well-suited to sensitive and demanding cosmetic formulas. This places aluminum tubes in the high-protection, higher-material-intensity, premium or specialist-use portion of the tube-material baseline.
Source name: Aluminum squeeze tubes manufacturer — ALLTUB — https://www.alltub.com/en-us/products/aluminum-squeeze-tubes/

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s Metamorphosis is described as a carton-based cosmetic tube where plastic is reduced and replaced with bio-based, certified paper-like material.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the paper-based primary-packaging baseline. Paper-based tubes remain a minority format compared with PE and laminate tubes, but Albéa’s product shows that paper-heavy cosmetic tubes are already commercialized as a current material pathway. It justifies including paper-based tubes as a secondary but relevant current baseline for later lightweighting and sustainability comparison, especially for brands exploring lower-plastic premium storytelling.
Source name: Metamorphosis Paper tube — Albéa — https://www.albea-group.com/products/metamorphosis-paper-tube

Summary of what was found: APC’s color-cosmetics compact page shows that compact cases are commonly built from aesthetic rigid plastics such as ABS and SAN.
Detailed description of what was found: APC lists the YYD3123 round compact as Compact ABS/SAN, confirming the use of these rigid plastics in compact shells. Combined with APC’s broader material guide, this supports using ABS-, SAN-, and PMMA-based compacts as the current baseline for face powders and similar formats in color cosmetics, with attention to decoration, clarity, and premium tactile feel rather than minimum weight.
Source name: YYD3123 | Round Compact — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/product/yyd3123-round-compact/

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s mono-material StandUpPouch confirms that PE-based refill pouches are already current solutions for liquid soap and detergent refills, with optional EVOH or PCR depending on application and barrier need.
Detailed description of what was found: Mondi states that its pouch is a PE-based mono-material solution, recyclable where facilities exist, and can include EVOH or PCR depending on the application. It also says the pouch replaces non-recyclable laminates and is used for liquid soap and detergent refills. This supports using mono-PE refill pouches as a realistic lightweight baseline while recognizing that barrier add-ons such as EVOH can complicate material purity and recyclability.
Source name: Mono-material StandUpPouch — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/flexible-packaging/mono-material-standuppouch/

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmPrima range shows that home and personal care refills are increasingly built in PE- and PP-format recycle-ready pouches that retain barrier, stiffness, seal strength, graphics performance, and high-speed runnability.
Detailed description of what was found: Amcor states that AmPrima is available in PE and PP formats, is designed to preserve the performance of traditional unrecyclable packaging, and can deliver up to 90% packaging weight reduction for refill products versus rigid containers. It also notes PCR-content options. This is strong evidence that flexible refill packs already form a current primary-packaging baseline in personal care and are central to material-based lightweighting.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/emea/homeandpersonalcare

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmFiber page confirms that paper-based primary sachets are already used for single-use home and personal care applications such as face masks, serums, shampoo, and conditioner.
Detailed description of what was found: Amcor describes AmFiber as a curbside-recyclable paper-based sachet for difficult-to-hold aqueous products and explicitly names face masks, serums, shampoo, and conditioner. It highlights barrier protection, machinery compatibility, shelf appeal, and premiumization. This makes it a relevant baseline reference for paper-led primary packs in sample, single-dose, and travel-size beauty applications.
Source name: Product Sheet: AmFiber Paper-Based Home and Personal Care Sachets - North America — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/insights/educational-resources/paperbased-hpc-sachets

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board shows that folding boxboard is a core secondary-packaging material in beautycare and is used for cartons as well as premium decorative effects.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports folding boxboard as a mainstream secondary-packaging substrate for cosmetics and personal care. It states that beautycare folding boxboards are suitable for multi-layer embossing, foil laminations, holograms, 3D special effects, high-quality printing on both sides, and sturdy packaging that creates an unboxing experience. For later lightweighting work, it helps distinguish standard lightweight paperboard use from premium paperboard use with added decorative treatments that can increase material intensity.
Source name: Beautycare — Paperboard end uses — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/metsaboard/products-and-services/paperboard-applications/beautycare/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso establishes folding boxboard as a mainstream material base for cosmetics and personal care while showing lighter-weight FBB as a market direction.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for secondary packs built from folding carton board. It identifies Performa as a premium folding-boxboard family and describes Performa Lumi as a GC1 folding boxboard designed for premium visual impact and efficient material use, while also noting that the platform is robust yet lightweight. It also shows broader folding-carton options including luxury-board and brown-fiber board families, which is useful for comparing mass-market cartons, premium cartons, and material-efficient luxury-board options.
Source name: Premium folding carton boards for packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso provides an explicit SBS baseline for luxe beauty packaging and ties SBS to demanding finishes and tactile premium effects.
Detailed description of what was found: This source identifies solid bleached sulfate as a luxury-board portfolio for high-end packaging, including cosmetics and perfumes. It says SBS boards are engineered for premium folding cartons and high-end packs, provides coated and uncoated SBS examples, and says these boards tolerate embossing, foil stamping, metallized foils, and other demanding finishing treatments while maintaining stiffness and sharp folds. This is strong evidence that premium/luxe beauty often uses high-spec SBS for secondary cartons when tactile feel, whiteness, print quality, and decorative finishing are central.
Source name: Coated and uncoated carton boards for luxury packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/carton-boards-for-luxury-packaging

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock shows that paperboard secondary packaging includes boxes, trays, and carton sleeves, not only standard tuck cartons.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports mapping paperboard-based secondary formats. It states that folding cartons are part of a broader paperboard packaging portfolio that includes carton sleeves and multipack solutions, and that folding-carton systems can be delivered in different styles such as boxes and trays. It also notes that folding cartons can carry varnishes, foil-type embellishments, textured effects, window patches, and a range of board grades, making it useful for mapping mainstream and upgraded secondary formats before lightweighting evaluation.
Source name: Folding Cartons — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/products/packaging/paperboard/folding-cartons

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock confirms coated recycled board as a current paperboard option for folding cartons and custom applications.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for recycled paperboard in secondary beauty and personal-care packaging. It states that coated recycled board is made with recycled fibers and has a smooth clay coating that supports printability for folding cartons and custom applications. This is useful as a mass-market and scale-oriented baseline because it is paper-based, scalable, and often more aligned with lower-cost secondary packaging than rigid-box constructions.
Source name: Coated Recycled Board — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/products/paper-and-board/paperboard/coated-recycled-board-crb

Summary of what was found: WestRock provides a coated natural kraft paperboard baseline for secondary packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: The source identifies coated natural kraft paperboard as a current board option and states that it delivers crisp graphics while retaining the natural-kraft substrate character. For baseline purposes, CNK is relevant where brands want a natural or rustic premium look without moving to much heavier rigid formats. It should be included as a paperboard alternative to SBS and CRB when appearance, strength, and material positioning differ.
Source name: Coated Natural Kraft Paperboard — WestRock — https://www.westrock.com/products/paperboard/customkote

Summary of what was found: GPA Global’s Sugarpill case shows a premium/luxe rigid-box construction using rigid greyboard, decorative outer wrap, film treatment, foil, magnets, and ribbon.
Detailed description of what was found: This is strong evidence for the premium/luxe secondary-packaging baseline. The case states that the pack used a rigid greyboard structure, wrapped exterior paper, soft-touch film coating, foils, spot UV, concealed magnets, and a ribbon lift. For later lightweighting work, this type of pack is a material-intensive target because the structural board, decorative wrap, closure hardware, and embellishments all layer material and weight onto a non-primary beauty package.
Source name: Sugarpill 10th Anniversary Collection — GPA Global — https://www.gpaglobal.net/packaging/case-studies/sugarpill-10th-anniversary-collection/

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock says rigid boxes are often used for luxury packaging and that choosing a lower board weight can reduce cost and material use.
Detailed description of what was found: This source directly supports the premium-versus-mass split. It states that paperboard comes in various weights and that choosing a lower weight reduces cost, while also noting that rigid boxes are often used for luxury packaging and that their quality brings a higher price point. This is useful evidence for why mass-market packs tend to favor lighter board and why later lightweighting work should treat board-weight optimization and rigid-to-folding-carton substitution as important material-based pathways.
Source name: Ways to adjust your luxury packaging to meet your business goals — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/blog/ways-to-adjust-your-luxury-packaging-to-meet-your-business-goals

Summary of what was found: Fedrigoni provides a direct material taxonomy for labels: paper or plastic-film facestock, adhesive layer, and release liner.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the label baseline for secondary and tertiary packaging where pressure-sensitive labels are used on cartons, sleeves, gift packs, shippers, and related formats. It states that self-adhesive labelstock has three main components and that the printed facestock can be paper or plastic film, including PP, PE, PET, and PVC. This is useful because labels can add mixed-material complexity and affect recyclability when filmic constructions or heavy laminations are used.
Source name: Self-adhesive material: what it is and how it is made — Fedrigoni — https://fedrigoni.com/en/news/self-adhesive-material-what-it-is-and-how-it-is-made/

Summary of what was found: CCL confirms shrink sleeves as a current beauty and personal-care packaging format and signals polyolefin sleeve development for recycling compatibility.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for sleeve materials in beauty packaging. The product page states that beauty and personal-care shrink sleeves provide 360-degree decoration, confirming their continued use as high-graphics sleeve formats. CCL’s beauty-market information also references floatable polyolefin shrink sleeves that support PET recycling, helping distinguish conventional plastic sleeve systems from improved sleeve-material options that may reduce recycling interference.
Source name: Beauty & Personal Care Shrink Sleeves — CCL Label — https://ccllabel.com/product/beauty-personal-care-shrink-sleeves/

Summary of what was found: GPA Global’s molded-pulp guide provides a replacement pathway for plastic trays and inserts in premium cosmetics: molded pulp made from recycled paper or natural fibers.
Detailed description of what was found: This source is central for replacing plastic inserts and trays. It states that molded pulp can be made from recycled paper or natural fibers, lists inputs such as bagasse, bamboo, wheat straw, old newsprint, OCC, and paper, and identifies cosmetics applications including interior cushioning, liner trays, and exterior/secondary packaging. It also addresses the premium/luxe challenge by noting advances in finish, color matching, and surface quality, making molded pulp viable beyond basic brown transit applications.
Source name: Our guide to moulded pulp packaging — GPA Global — https://www.gpaglobal.net/sustainability/our-guide-moulded-pulp-packaging-luxury-cosmetics/

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock identifies foam and plastic trays as historic defaults for delicate premium products and names corrugate and molded fiber as paper-based replacement routes.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports why premium packs often become material-intensive and what replacement pathways exist for plastic or foam inserts. It states that foam or plastic trays were historically go-to protective materials for delicate products, but corrugate and molded fiber inserts are increasingly preferred as brands push for sustainability. This identifies premium internal fitments as a high-priority target area for lightweighting and material substitution.
Source name: Ways to Make Your Luxury Packaging More Sustainable — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/blog/ways-to-make-your-luxury-packaging-more-sustainable

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock establishes corrugated paper-fiber shipping boxes as the mainstream tertiary baseline for cosmetics, skincare, haircare, and wellness.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the tertiary-packaging baseline for corrugated shippers used in direct-to-consumer and e-commerce channels. It states that health-and-beauty shipping boxes are made from paper fiber, are designed to be recyclable, protect fragile cosmetics and skincare in transit, and can be delivered flat-packed in strong yet lightweight designs. This is a key baseline combination for later comparison: corrugated paper-fiber shipper plus optional internal paper-based protection.
Source name: Health & Beauty Shipping Boxes — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/products/packaging/ecommerce/health-beauty-shipping-boxes

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock’s FaleLokiKoki case provides a cosmetics-specific example of replacing assorted fillers and plastic materials with a single corrugated insert.
Detailed description of what was found: This direct beauty-sector customer case explains that a Polish distributor of cosmetics and hairdressing products replaced bulk packaging with assorted fillers by adopting a single-piece corrugated insert that divided the shipper into multiple sections. It reports lower damage complaints, faster packing, and elimination of adhesive tape, foil, and plastic fillers. This is highly relevant proof that corrugated inserts are a practical tertiary-packaging replacement pathway in beauty e-commerce.
Source name: FALELOKIKOKI — Reducing damage complaints by 75% with a corrugated packaging insert — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/innovation/customer-stories/falelokikoki

Summary of what was found: Paptic provides a paper-like wood-fibre mailer option positioned for e-commerce and premium unboxing.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports current material options for mailers in tertiary packaging. It states that Paptic mailers are designed for e-commerce and emphasizes performance, functionality, and a soft premium feel for the unboxing experience. It sits between conventional corrugated boxes and traditional paper or plastic mailers, making it a relevant lightweighting alternative where brands want lower-bulk mailer formats without defaulting to plastic.
Source name: Sustainable E-Commerce Mailers — Paptic — https://paptic.com/applications/e-commerce/

Summary of what was found: Pregis establishes paper-based cushioned mailers as a current e-commerce protective-packaging format.
Detailed description of what was found: Pregis states that the EverTec mailer uses durable two-layer paper construction, resilient cushioning, double-walled kraft paper, and curbside recyclability. This documents a mailer format that combines protection and reduced pack size relative to a box. It is a useful baseline for lightweight, low-cube, paper-based tertiary packaging that can replace some box-plus-void-fill combinations for smaller cosmetic and personal-care orders.
Source name: EverTec Cushioned Mailer — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/us-solutions/by-product/shipping-mailers/cushioned-evertec/

Summary of what was found: Sealed Air confirms inflated plastic-film pillows as a mainstream void-fill material in e-commerce tertiary packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Sealed Air states that inflatable air pillows are used to prevent shifting, bruising, and breakage during transit, and that they function as efficient void-fill solutions shipped and stored flat. This identifies a material class that is lightweight in shipped mass but still fossil-film-based and should be evaluated against paper-based alternatives in replacement or recyclability workstreams.
Source name: Inflatable Air Pillows — Sealed Air — https://www.sealedair.com/ap/products/protective-packaging/inflatable-air-pillows

Summary of what was found: Ranpak shows paper-based cushioning and paper-based void fill as scalable current protective-packaging materials for shipping applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Ranpak describes PadPak paper as a cushioning material for fragile products and describes its void-fill solutions as paper-based dunnage used to fill empty space and immobilize products inside shipping boxes. These sources represent already-commercial, scalable, recyclable paper-based protection systems that can be benchmarked against bubble, foam, and air-pillow systems.
Source name: Paper Cushioning Solutions; Sustainable Void Fill Solutions — Ranpak — https://www.ranpak.com/cushioning/ ; https://www.ranpak.com/void-fill/

Summary of what was found: PET resin sits in a low-to-mid commodity packaging cost band versus engineering plastics, with June 2026 regional prices around USD 0.94–1.55/kg.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PET. Price/trend found: June 2026 PET prices were reported at Africa USD 0.94/kg, Northeast Asia USD 1.17/kg, Europe USD 1.55/kg, India USD 1.04/kg, Southeast Asia USD 1.11/kg, and North America USD 1.41/kg. Geography/date/period: multiple regions, June 2026. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because PET remains commercially practical and scalable for cosmetic bottles and jars compared with higher-cost PMMA, SAN, and ABS. Limitation: resin benchmarks do not include grams per pack, conversion, decoration, scrap, transport, or grade-specific requirements.
Source name: Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) Price Index & Chart 2026 — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/polyethylene-terephthalate-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: HDPE is a low-cost commodity packaging resin, with Q2 2026 prices around USD 0.99–1.33/kg across major regions.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: HDPE. Price/trend found: Q2 2026 prices reached USA USD 1,194/MT, China USD 1,023/MT, Germany USD 991/MT, United Kingdom USD 1,327/MT, and Brazil USD 1,042/MT. Geography/date/period: major global regions, Q2 2026 and Q1 2026. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because HDPE is clearly in the low-cost, scalable bucket and suitable as a baseline for mass-market personal care bottles and closures. Limitation: prices vary by grade, supplier contract, and region, and downstream conversion costs are excluded.
Source name: High Density Polyethylene (HDPE) Price Trend & Forecast 2026 — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/hdpe-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: LDPE sits in a low-cost commodity band, with Q4 2024 public benchmarks around USD 1.12–1.33/kg across key markets.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: LDPE. Price/trend found: Q4 2024 prices were United States USD 1,140/MT, Japan USD 1,125/MT, England USD 1,160/MT, Saudi Arabia USD 1,153/MT, and Brazil USD 1,330/MT. Geography/date/period: selected global markets, Q4 2024. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because LDPE is directionally low-cost and relevant for tubes, flexible components, and film applications. Limitation: source period is older than 2026 snapshots, so it should be used as directional benchmark only.
Source name: LDPE Prices, Chart, Index, Graph, Trend and Forecast — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/low-density-polyethylene-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: PP remained a commodity-to-lower-mid cost resin in June 2026, with prices around USD 1.28–2.34/kg depending on region.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PP. Price/trend found: June 2026 PP prices were Northeast Asia USD 1.42/kg, Europe USD 2.34/kg, India USD 1.28/kg, and North America USD 1.45/kg. Geography/date/period: multiple regions, June 2026. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because PP remains commercially practical for caps, closures, jars, and molded components where cost and broad processing compatibility matter. Limitation: Europe was materially higher than Asia and North America; pack economics still need weight-per-pack and molding-cost translation.
Source name: Polypropylene Prices, Index, Trend, Chart & Forecast 2026 — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/polypropylene-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: Recycled PET was broadly similar to or lower than virgin PET in several 2026 public benchmarks, but price depends heavily on feedstock quality and region.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: rPET / recycled PET. Price/trend found: Q2 2026 prices were USA USD 1,018/MT, China USD 749/MT, Germany USD 941/MT, India USD 763/MT, and Brazil USD 1,033/MT. Geography/date/period: Q2 2026. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because rPET can be commercially feasible and not automatically premium-priced versus virgin PET. Limitation: public benchmarks may not distinguish food-grade, cosmetic-grade, flake versus pellet, certification, color, odor, or quality premiums.
Source name: Recycled PET Prices, Chart, Trend, Index and Demand 2026 — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/recycled-pet-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: Recycled HDPE can be competitive with virgin HDPE in some markets but higher in others, showing PCR premiums and discounts are regional and quality-driven.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: rHDPE / recycled HDPE. Price/trend found: Q1 2026 prices were USA USD 1,180/MT, China USD 955/MT, Germany USD 1,281/MT, Saudi Arabia USD 802/MT, and India USD 930/MT. Geography/date/period: Q1 2026. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because rHDPE is viable for PCR tracks, but it cannot be assumed cheaper than virgin HDPE. Limitation: regulatory grade, odor, color, contamination, certification, and supply consistency can materially affect real procurement pricing.
Source name: Recycled High Density Polyethylene Prices, Chart, Demand — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/recycled-high-density-polyethylene-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: Recycled PP can carry a material premium over commodity PP in some markets, implying PCR PP may fail a strict mass-market cost filter unless recycled-content value is strategic.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: rPP / recycled PP. Price/trend found: March 2026 prices were Japan USD 1,028/MT, Germany USD 1,736/MT, and Saudi Arabia USD 1,020/MT. Geography/date/period: March 2026. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because rPP can be more expensive in certain regions, especially Europe, and may fit premium or sustainability-target-led programs better than pure cost-down briefs. Limitation: grades and purity are not fully specified and the data should be validated before procurement use.
Source name: Recycled Polypropylene Prices, Trend, Chart, News and Forecast — ChemAnalyst — https://www.chemanalyst.com/Pricing-data/recycled-polypropylene-1277

Summary of what was found: ABS prices materially exceed PET, HDPE, and PP, supporting its classification as a mid-to-high cost engineering resin for premium cosmetics components.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: ABS. Price/trend found: Q2 2025 prices were USA USD 1,824/MT, China USD 1,486/MT, Germany USD 2,132/MT, Brazil USD 1,721/MT, and India USD 1,668/MT. Geography/date/period: Q2 2025. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because ABS is more expensive than commodity packaging resins and should be treated as a premium-feature or performance/aesthetic material rather than a default lightweight substitute for mass-market formats. Limitation: 2025 data is older than some 2026 commodity snapshots, but directionally useful.
Source name: Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) Price Trend, Index and Forecast — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: SAN sits in a relatively high price band, especially in the U.S. and Europe, reinforcing that transparent engineering plastics can be premium-priced.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: SAN / styrene acrylonitrile. Price/trend found: Q1 2026 prices were USA USD 2,610/MT, China USD 1,414/MT, Germany USD 1,821/MT, Brazil USD 1,443/MT, and France USD 2,089/MT. Geography/date/period: Q1 2026. Unit: USD/MT. This supports price-first filtering because SAN is not a low-cost substitution candidate for mainstream cosmetic packaging and fits better where transparency and premium appearance justify higher costs. Limitation: real cosmetic-packaging economics also depend on molding yield, color/clarity specs, and decoration.
Source name: Styrene Acrylonitrile Resin Prices, Trend, Index and Forecast 2026 — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/styrene-acrylonitrile-resin-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: PMMA is one of the most expensive polymers in the baseline set, with June 2026 prices around USD 2.25–3.78/kg.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PMMA / acrylic. Price/trend found: June 2026 PMMA prices were Northeast Asia USD 2.25/kg, Europe USD 3.54/kg, Southeast Asia USD 3.10/kg, and North America USD 3.78/kg. Geography/date/period: multiple regions, June 2026. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because PMMA should generally be reserved for luxe or high-visibility components where aesthetics justify the premium and should fail most mass-market price-first filters. Limitation: decoration, polishing, rejects, and conversion costs can widen the final pack-cost gap further.
Source name: Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA) Prices, News and Demand — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/polymethyl-methacrylate-pricing-report

Summary of what was found: Primary aluminum is expensive relative to commodity plastics and paperboard, with World Bank monthly averages rising to USD 3,600/mt in April 2026.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: aluminum. Price/trend found: World Bank Pink Sheet lists aluminum at USD 3,193/mt in Jan 2026, USD 3,065/mt in Feb 2026, USD 3,373/mt in Mar 2026, and USD 3,600/mt in Apr 2026. Geography/date/period: global commodity benchmark, early 2026 monthly averages. Unit: USD/mt. This supports price-first filtering because aluminum belongs in a mid-to-high cost packaging-material bucket and should be screened against grams used, wall thickness, decoration, and freight benefits. Limitation: this is a primary metal benchmark, not a converted cosmetic-packaging component price; alloy, sheet/canstock processing, forming, and finishing add cost.
Source name: Commodity Markets Pink Sheet, May 2026 — World Bank — https://thedocs.worldbank.org/en/doc/74e8be41ceb20fa0da750cda2f6b9e4e-0050012026/related/CMO-Pink-Sheet-May-2026.pdf

Summary of what was found: Soda-lime glass appears around USD 0.8–1.7/kg, but its high density means low price/kg does not make it low-cost per pack.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: glass / soda-lime glass. Price/trend found: ScienceDirect lists soda-lime glass at USD 0.8–1.7/kg. Geography/date/period: general materials reference, not a dated market quote. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because glass can look affordable on a raw-material basis but can become costly per unit once grams per pack, freight, breakage, and filling-line handling are considered. Limitation: this is a generic material-property reference, not a regional 2026 commodity quote or converted bottle price.
Source name: Soda-Lime Glass — ScienceDirect Topics / Elsevier — https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/soda-lime-glass

Summary of what was found: Generic paperboard is the cheapest broad material family in the reviewed public benchmarks, at roughly USD 0.15–0.29/kg in June 2026.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: paperboard broadly; directional proxy for boxboard used in folding cartons and some SBS/FBB applications. Price/trend found: June 2026 prices were North America USD 0.15/kg, Northeast Asia USD 0.22/kg, Europe USD 0.23/kg, India USD 0.23/kg, and Southeast Asia USD 0.29/kg. Geography/date/period: June 2026. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because paperboard is clearly low-cost and scalable for secondary packaging lightweighting. Limitation: source is generic paperboard, not grade-specific SBS, FBB, CRB, or decorated luxury-board pricing; premium coatings, barriers, and finishes sit above this baseline.
Source name: Paper Board Prices 2026 | June Price Chart & Index — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/paper-board-price-trend

Summary of what was found: Unbleached kraft packaging paperboard had a current U.S. producer-price index of 217.087 in May 2026, useful as a directional baseline for kraft-board formats.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: unbleached kraft packaging and industrial converting paperboard. Price/trend found: May 2026 PPI was 217.087, with Apr 2026 at 215.471 and Jan 2026 at 220.339. Geography/date/period: United States, monthly producer-price index, May 2026. Unit: index. This supports price-first filtering by providing a credible public benchmark for kraft-board cost movement when evaluating decorated kraft cartons, sleeves, or mailer-style secondary packs. Limitation: this is an index, not USD/kg or USD/tonne, so it should be used for relative movement rather than direct RFQ substitution.
Source name: Producer Price Index by Commodity: Pulp, Paper, and Allied Products: Unbleached Kraft Packaging and Industrial Converting Paperboard — FRED / U.S. BLS — https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/WPU09141107

Summary of what was found: Recycled paperboard had a May 2026 U.S. producer-price index of 438.147, providing a directional cost baseline for CRB and recycled boxboard families.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: recycled paperboard; directional proxy for CRB/duplex/recycled-boxboard formats used in cartons and inserts. Price/trend found: May 2026 PPI was 438.147, versus 430.584 in Apr 2026 and 429.318 in Jan 2026. Geography/date/period: United States, monthly producer-price index, May 2026. Unit: index. This supports price-first filtering because recycled board remains important for cost-down and recyclability-led secondary-packaging options. Limitation: it is an index rather than transactional price and does not isolate cosmetic-grade print or surface-performance requirements.
Source name: Producer Price Index by Industry: Paperboard Mills: Recycled Paperboard — FRED / U.S. BLS — https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PCU3221303221307

Summary of what was found: Corrugated paper remains one of the lowest-cost transport-packaging materials, with June 2026 prices around USD 0.19–0.41/kg depending on region.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: corrugated paper / containerboard family. Price/trend found: June 2026 prices were Europe USD 0.19/kg, North America USD 0.27/kg, and Northeast Asia USD 0.41/kg. Geography/date/period: multiple regions, June 2026. Unit: USD/kg. This supports price-first filtering because corrugated remains a leading low-cost, scalable tertiary-packaging baseline for shippers, e-commerce boxes, and protective inserts. Limitation: converted corrugated-box economics depend on flute/profile, basis weight, board combination, converting, and print complexity.
Source name: Corrugated Paper Prices June 2026 | Trend & Forecast — IMARC Group — https://www.imarcgroup.com/corrugated-paper-price-trend

Summary of what was found: Molded pulp goods had a U.S. producer-price index of 248.590 in April 2026, giving a credible directional benchmark for plastic-insert replacement screens.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: molded pulp / molded fiber goods. Price/trend found: Apr 2026 PPI was 248.590, with Mar 2026 at 247.239 and Jan 2026 at 245.403. Geography/date/period: United States, monthly producer-price index, April 2026. Unit: index. This supports price-first filtering because molded fiber belongs in the commercially practical sustainable-material set for trays, inserts, and e-commerce protection and should be screened against thermoformed plastic and foam options. Limitation: this is an index for molded pulp goods broadly, not a cosmetic insert-specific converted-piece quote; tooling, wet-press quality, and surface finish can alter cost.
Source name: Producer Price Index by Commodity: Pulp, Paper, and Allied Products: Molded Pulp Goods, Including Egg Cartons, Florists Pots, Food Trays, etc. — FRED / U.S. BLS — https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/WPU09150814

Summary of what was found: Converted paperboard format costs show a premium ladder: setup rigid paperboard boxes are indexed far above folding paperboard boxes in May 2026, confirming that luxe secondary formats are materially more cost-intensive.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: setup rigid paperboard boxes and folding paperboard boxes. Price/trend found: in May 2026, FRED’s release table showed setup rigid paperboard boxes at 507.508 (Index Dec 1983=100) and folding paperboard boxes at 155.693 (Index Dec 2004=100). Geography/date/period: United States, May 2026. Unit: index. This supports price-first filtering because rigid/set-up luxury box formats should be treated as a separate premium cost track from mainstream folding-carton formats. Limitation: the indices use different base years, so this is directional rather than a direct absolute cost ratio.
Source name: May 2026, Goods: Pulp, Paper, and Allied Products — FRED / U.S. BLS — https://fred.stlouisfed.org/release/tables?eid=143284&rid=46

Summary of what was found: Petainer gives a practical density benchmark for PET and PP: PET about 1.38 g/cm³ and PP about 0.90 g/cm³, linking those values to packaging behavior.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PET and PP. Density data found: PET ~1.38 g/cm³ and PP ~0.90 g/cm³. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: useful for clear bottles, jars, and closures where PET is chosen for clarity/barrier and PP for lighter-weight rigid parts. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing why PP is intrinsically lighter than PET for the same solid volume, while PET remains attractive when clarity and barrier matter. Limitation: directional comparison from a packaging explainer rather than a full resin-family datasheet.
Source name: What is PET Plastic? Engineering and Safety Guide — Petainer — https://www.petainer.com/insights/materials-sustainability/what-is-pet-plastic

Summary of what was found: SpecialChem provides quantified density ranges for several packaging-relevant plastics: HDPE 0.940–0.970 g/cm³, LDPE 0.917–0.940 g/cm³, and ABS 1.020–1.210 g/cm³.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: HDPE, LDPE, ABS, plus general plastics density methodology. Density data found: HDPE 0.940–0.970 g/cm³; LDPE 0.917–0.940 g/cm³; ABS 1.020–1.210 g/cm³. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: HDPE/LDPE are core bottle, tube, closure, and film materials in personal care; ABS is relevant for prestige caps, overcaps, and decorative components. This supports lightweighting evaluation by identifying polyethylene grades as intrinsically low-density candidates and showing ABS as a denser, more premium/decorative plastic that can add mass when used in thick-wall caps or shells. Limitation: cross-industry rather than cosmetics-specific, so actual pack weight still depends on wall thickness, part geometry, and multi-material assemblies.
Source name: Density of Plastics Material: Technical Properties Table — SpecialChem — https://www.specialchem.com/plastics/guide/density

Summary of what was found: Eastman’s TDS for Eastar copolyester 6763 gives a PETG/copolyester density point of 1.27 g/cm³, placing PETG above PP/PE in density while remaining attractive for clarity and rigid packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PETG / copolyester. Density data found: 1.27 g/cm³; specific gravity 1.27. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: relevant for clear rigid trays, packs, and some premium cosmetic bottles or jars. This supports lightweighting evaluation by placing PETG below glass and aluminum on density but above PP/PE, explaining why PETG can be heavier than commodity polyolefins before wall-thickness effects are considered. Limitation: grade-specific Eastman data, not a whole-family range, and positioned for medical/rigid packaging rather than beauty-specific packs.
Source name: Eastar copolyester 6763 | TDS — Eastman — https://productcatalog.eastman.com/tds/ProdDatasheet.aspx?pn=Eastar+676&product=71040786

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging explains why beauty packaging still uses PETG, PMMA, AS/SAN, and ABS despite these not being the lightest options: clarity, gloss, rigidity, toughness, decoration performance, and glass-like aesthetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: PET, PE, PP, PETG/PCTA/PCTG, PMMA, AS/SAN, ABS, aluminum, and glass. Density/weight data found: no numeric density table, but strong cosmetics-specific evidence on material-selection logic. Packaging format relevance: prestige jars, outer bottles, overcaps, and decorative shells. This supports lightweighting evaluation by explaining why density alone is not enough: PMMA is used as plastic glass, PETG for superior clarity, SAN for rigidity/transparency, and ABS for high gloss, impact resistance, and decoration. These materials may survive a price/weight penalty in premium beauty because of sensory and branding value. Limitation: qualitative source that supports material-selection logic rather than numerical density benchmarking.
Source name: Packaging Materials For Cosmetic Containers — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/custom-services/materials/

Summary of what was found: MatWeb’s soda-lime glass entry gives a packaging-relevant density anchor of about 2.50 g/cm³, confirming glass is intrinsically far denser than commodity plastics and most paper-based materials.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: soda-lime silicate glass. Density data found: 2.50 g/cc at 25°C. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: cosmetic serum, fragrance, dropper, and molded-glass containers because container glass is typically soda-lime based. This supports lightweighting evaluation by quantifying why glass creates a high-mass baseline before decoration, thick bases, droppers, and overcaps are added. Limitation: material-property source rather than finished-pack source; practical pack mass depends on bottle design, wall thickness, and base thickness.
Source name: Schott AR-GLAS Soda Lime Silicate Glass — MatWeb — https://www.matweb.com/search/datasheet.aspx?matguid=c72a11187fb34d8cadb600a0706aba69&n=1

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer’s cosmetics and fragrance catalogue provides real glass pack-weight examples showing how quickly glass mass rises with capacity.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: cosmetic glass / moulded glass. Weight data found: 20 ml dropper 35 g; 30 ml dropper 47 g; 50 ml dropper 55 g; 100 ml dropper 100 g; 250 ml bottle 223 g. Unit: grams. Packaging format relevance: serum/dropper and premium cosmetic bottle formats. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing that glass may be manageable on a raw-material-price basis but still becomes heavy per pack because empty container mass is high relative to filled volume. Limitation: SKU-specific catalogue weights and excludes system weight from droppers, pumps, collars, caps, labels, and secondary packaging.
Source name: Glass Packaging - Cosmetics & Fragrance - Products — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/catalogue/products/cosmetics-fragrance/glass-packaging.html

Summary of what was found: MatWeb’s aluminum data gives a packaging-relevant density baseline of about 2.70 g/cm³, lower than glass but far above commodity plastics and paperboard on a solid-material basis.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: aluminum. Density data found: 2.7 g/cc. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: aluminum bottles, shells, caps, aerosols, and luxe trim. This supports lightweighting evaluation by explaining why aluminum can still be attractive despite higher density than plastics: it is lighter than glass for the same solid volume, yet dense enough that thick or decorative metal components can add noticeable pack mass. Limitation: pure-material property reference; practical cosmetic-pack weights depend strongly on gauge, forming method, and component design.
Source name: Aluminum, Al — MatWeb — https://www.matweb.com/search/datasheet.aspx?bassnum=AMEAL00

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board’s FBB specifications provide grammage and thickness ranges that infer an apparent-density range of roughly 0.58–0.81 g/cm³ for FBB used in beautycare and point-of-sale applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: folding boxboard (FBB). Data found: grammage 170–380 g/m² and thickness 210–650 µm. Unit: g/m² and µm; inferred apparent density ≈ 0.58–0.81 g/cm³ calculated from grammage divided by thickness. Packaging format relevance: folding cartons, sleeves, covers, cards, and display materials for beautycare and consumer applications. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing FBB achieves low apparent density because of its bulky multilayer structure, making it a strong lightweight secondary-pack option versus denser premium boards and rigid structures. Limitation: density is inferred from published specifications, not directly stated, and applies to board sheet structure rather than an assembled carton.
Source name: MetsäBoard Prime FBB Bright Product Sheet — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/contentassets/89d536cbb9ea42bea02aef4516212389/metsaboard-prime-fbb-bright-en-us.pdf

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s Ensocoat SBS specifications imply an apparent-density range of about 0.78–0.95 g/cm³ and position the board for luxury packaging, including cosmetics and perfume applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: SBS / solid bleached sulphate board. Data found: grammage 190–380 g/m² and thickness 200–485 µm. Unit: g/m² and µm; inferred apparent density ≈ 0.78–0.95 g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: folding cartons and premium cartons for cosmetics, perfume, displays, and luxury printed packs. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing SBS is denser than many FBB options and optimized for surface smoothness, whiteness, print quality, embossing, hot foil stamping, and luxury presentation. Limitation: inferred density from supplier specs; assembled pack weight can rise further with foils, coatings, inserts, and extra structure.
Source name: Ensocoat — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/-/media/documents/download-center/documents/product-specifications/paperboard-materials/ensocoat-en_n.pdf

Summary of what was found: WestRock’s CustomKote CNK guide provides grammage and thickness data implying an apparent-density range of roughly 0.73–0.82 g/cm³ and lists beauty and personal care among uses.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: coated natural kraft / CNK. Data found: thickness 356–762 µm and nominal basis weight 293–557 g/m². Unit: µm and g/m²; inferred apparent density ≈ 0.73–0.82 g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: cartons requiring strength, wet/cold performance, or natural-kraft aesthetics, including beauty and personal care. This supports lightweighting evaluation because CNK offers stiffness-to-weight routes at a given strength target, making it a candidate to replace heavier boards where high strength is still needed. Limitation: calculated density is board-sheet level only; high-barrier treatments or premium decoration can still raise total pack mass.
Source name: WestRock CustomKote Paperboard — WestRock — https://shop.igepa.be/media/19/0e/f9/1659914368/H-602-5361-1_CustomKoteProductGuiderevJan2020.pdf

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging identifies CRB as 100% recycled fiber and describes it as a lightweight sheet for folding-carton applications, even though no numeric density is published.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: coated recycled board / CRB. Data found: qualitative lightweight sheet made from 100% recycled fiber; no numeric density published. Packaging format relevance: consumer-product folding cartons and printed secondary packs. This supports lightweighting evaluation by positioning CRB as an already lightweight, scalable baseline for mass-market secondary packs where recycled content and printability matter more than luxury tactility. Limitation: no direct density or pack-weight data; later modeling should add supplier caliper/grammage data before final cross-material comparisons.
Source name: Conquest Recycled Paperboard — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/products/conquest-recycled-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board’s white kraftliner page gives grammage and thickness ranges implying a broad apparent-density range of roughly 0.78–0.96 g/cm³ for estimating corrugated-liner mass in tertiary packs.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: white kraftliner / kraft paperboard for corrugated applications. Data found: examples include 135–175 g/m² at 140–185 µm and 70–160 g/m² at 90–205 µm. Unit: g/m² and µm; inferred apparent density ≈ 0.78–0.96 g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: corrugated liner for e-commerce shippers, display-ready packs, and transit packaging. This supports lightweighting evaluation by giving a practical basis for comparing kraftliner choices in corrugated structures and showing that liner selection can change tertiary-pack weight without changing broad box geometry. Limitation: calculation is inferred from public specs and does not include fluting medium or flute geometry.
Source name: White kraftliners for high-quality corrugated packaging — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/metsaboard/products-and-services/products/white-kraftliners/

Summary of what was found: International Paper’s containerboard page gives basis-weight ranges for corrugated components: liners 100–469 gsm, white-top liners 126–361 gsm, and medium 112–220 gsm.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: corrugated liner and medium / containerboard. Data found: liner 100–469 gsm; white top 126–361 gsm; medium 112–220 gsm. Unit: g/m². Packaging format relevance: corrugated shippers, e-commerce boxes, display trays, and transport cases. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing why density alone is insufficient for tertiary packaging: corrugated weight depends on liner selection, white-top upgrades, and medium grade/basis weight. Limitation: source does not publish assembled board thickness or box weights, so later modeling should pair gsm ranges with flute profile and board construction.
Source name: Containerboard Products & Solutions — International Paper — https://www.internationalpaper.com/paper/containerboard

Summary of what was found: BioResources shows molded pulp can span a wide apparent-density range, including 0.28–0.69 g/cm³ in one cited study and a broader reported range of 0.2–1.0 g/cm³.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: molded pulp / molded fiber. Density data found: 0.28–0.69 g/cm³ in one cited study; broader 0.2–1.0 g/cm³ reported for molded pulp products; about 0.6 g/cm³ judged optimal in one cited case. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: inserts, trays, protective end-caps, cushioning, bracing, and replacements for plastic/EPS protective elements. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing molded fiber is not one fixed density class; cushioning-oriented pulp can be low density, while densification for strength or premium finish can raise mass. Limitation: review article synthesizes multiple studies and values reflect a broad process universe rather than one supplier grade.
Source name: Molded pulp products for sustainable packaging: Production rate challenges and product opportunities — BioResources — https://bioresources.cnr.ncsu.edu/resources/molded-pulp-products-for-sustainable-packaging-production-rate-challenges-and-product-opportunities/

Summary of what was found: Nissha gives a beauty-relevant specific gravity of approximately 1.2 g/cm³ for high-density pulp packaging, with applications including cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: high-density fiber-forming / dry formed pulp packaging. Density data found: specific gravity approx. 1.2 g/cm³. Unit: g/cm³. Packaging format relevance: molded trays, bottle covers, end caps, premium inserts, and protective secondary components for cosmetics. This supports lightweighting evaluation by warning that premium fiber replacements can approach plastic-like density if a smooth, dense, premium-finish molded part is specified. It helps separate paper-based from automatically lightweight. Limitation: supplier-specific technology page; density is approximate and may vary by design and coating.
Source name: Fiber-Forming: High-Performance Eco Packaging with High-Density Pulp — Nissha Europe — https://ecosense.nissha-europe.com/fiber-forming

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s paper-mailer range gives a tertiary-pack baseline: 2-ply open-flute paper protective mailers for fragile/personal-care items and solid-board mailers in about 350 gsm to 1000 grade range.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: paper mailers, solid-board mailers, and paper-based e-commerce protection. Data found: re/cycle Protective Mailer made from 2-ply open-flute paper; Solid Board Mailer offered from 350 gsm to 1000 grade range; designed to minimize material and applicable to personal care products. Unit: g/m² where provided. Packaging format relevance: e-commerce mailers, protective mailers, rigid flat mailers, and paper-based alternatives to heavier corrugated solutions. This supports lightweighting evaluation by providing paper-only tertiary formats that can sit below full corrugated boxes in mass for smaller and less fragile beauty orders. Limitation: product-family level page and does not publish finished mailer weights by size.
Source name: Paper mailers — Mondi — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/corrugated-solutions/paper-mailers/

Summary of what was found: CCL’s EcoFloat sleeve launch shows label/sleeve material matters for weight and recyclability: mainstream sleeves often use PVC or PET-G, while this alternative uses low-density polyolefin.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: shrink sleeves / label films. Data found: mainstream comparator materials are PVC and PET-G; EcoFloat uses low-density polyolefin and achieves up to 73% controlled TD shrinkage. Unit: shrink percentage and qualitative density class. Packaging format relevance: full-body shrink sleeves and decoration layers on bottles in beauty and personal care. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing label layers can be a hidden weight/material lever; switching from denser PET-G/PVC to low-density polyolefin can improve material intensity and recycling compatibility. Limitation: source does not publish exact film density or areal weight; best used directionally for sleeve-material screening.
Source name: Next generation high shrink EcoFloat sleeves — CCL Label — https://ccllabel.com/news/high-shrink-ecofloat-sleeves/

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s beauty-pump sources show why density alone is not enough at system level: pumps and dispensers can be redesigned as mono-material PE or all-plastic PP/PE systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Material covered: pumps, dispensers, and closures.
