Data found: Aptar’s Future pump is mono-material and fully recyclable when paired with PE or PET bottles; Aptar’s Advance pumps are made of PP and PE; other all-plastic designs are positioned for existing recycling streams. Unit: qualitative material-system evidence, not direct density. Packaging format relevance: lotion pumps, spray pumps, top closures, and major weight contributors in skincare and personal care packs. This supports lightweighting evaluation by showing pack weight is driven not only by bottle resin density but also closures, pumps, springs, actuators, and component count. Limitation: no gram weights on cited pages; use as system-design evidence rather than a numeric density source.
Source name: Future Dispensing Pump; Advance Pumps — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/products/future-fully-recyclable-dispensing-pump

Summary of what was found: EU cosmetics law requires cosmetic products to be safe under normal and reasonably foreseeable use; the Cosmetic Product Safety Report must include packaging-material purity and stability.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: overarching legal safety baseline for cosmetic packaging in the EU. Material/packaging relevance: any primary pack in direct contact with formula and container/packaging labeling. Product/formulation relevance: the CPSR must account for exposure scenarios, including foreseeable routes such as oral exposure for lip products and inhalation for sprays. Testing/compliance implication: lightweight materials cannot be recommended unless their purity, stability, and interaction profile can support CPSR sign-off. Use in screening: make CPSR-ready evidence on packaging purity/stability and foreseeable exposure a gate before material down-selection. Limitation: the regulation states obligations and information requirements but does not prescribe one universal compatibility test protocol.
Source name: Consolidated text: Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009 on cosmetic products — EUR-Lex — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A02009R1223-20250901

Summary of what was found: European Commission Annex I guidance states that formulation-packaging suitability studies should assess migration, storage-condition dependence, and sensitivity to light or air.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: migration from packaging to product, packaging suitability, and environmental sensitivity. Material/packaging relevance: all primary packaging in direct contact with cosmetics, especially materials with low-molecular-weight species, coatings, additives, recycled content, inks, adhesives, or barrier systems. Product/formulation relevance: formulas sensitive to light, oxygen, or air exposure need packaging that maintains safety and efficacy. Testing/compliance implication: compatibility programs should include migration-minded studies under relevant conditions. Use in screening: lightweight routes with coatings, recycled content, multilayers, inks, adhesives, or barriers should receive enhanced migration/suitability review. Limitation: guidance says there are no standard cosmetic-specific procedures, so product-specific judgment remains necessary.
Source name: Commission Implementing Decision 2013/674/EU on Guidelines on Annex I to Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009 — European Commission / EUR-Lex — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?qid=1782650439082&uri=CELEX%3A32013D0674

Summary of what was found: EU PPWR adds packaging-design constraints relevant to lightweighting: packaging must be minimized, recyclable at scale, meet recycled-content rules for plastic parts, and reduce empty space including in e-commerce packs.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: recyclability, minimization, recycled content, and empty-space rules. Material/packaging relevance: all packaging, especially plastic parts, e-commerce packs, transport packs, fillers, and non-recyclable composites. Product/formulation relevance: materials chosen for protection must still support product integrity while meeting recyclability and minimum-weight/volume rules. Testing/compliance implication: screening cannot consider only chemistry and performance; it must also assess recyclability at scale and whether fillers/secondary structures are necessary. Use in screening: add filters for minimum necessary weight/volume, design for recycling, recycled-content feasibility, and avoiding unnecessary empty space/fillers. Limitation: implementation details are being operationalized through methodologies and delegated/implementing acts, so company-specific compliance interpretation is still needed.
Source name: Regulation (EU) 2025/40 on packaging and packaging waste — EUR-Lex — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/40/oj/eng

Summary of what was found: FDA states cosmetics are adulterated if their container is made of a poisonous or deleterious substance that may render the contents injurious; packaging can also misbrand a cosmetic if misleading.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: packaging-related adulteration and misleading packaging under U.S. law. Material/packaging relevance: all cosmetic containers and closures sold in the U.S. Product/formulation relevance: any material that can leach harmful substances, contaminate product, or misrepresent contents creates regulatory exposure. Testing/compliance implication: U.S. screening should include toxicological review of packaging-contact materials and package integrity/fitness for purpose. Use in screening: treat any new lightweight resin, recycled-content route, decoration chemistry, or barrier system as a potential adulteration risk until safety is substantiated. Limitation: FDA sets legal responsibility on the company but does not prescribe one fixed premarket package-testing battery for cosmetics.
Source name: FDA Authority Over Cosmetics: How Cosmetics Are Not FDA-Approved, but Are FDA-Regulated — U.S. Food and Drug Administration — https://www.fda.gov/cosmetics/cosmetics-laws-regulations/fda-authority-over-cosmetics-how-cosmetics-are-not-fda-approved-are-fda-regulated

Summary of what was found: FDA’s GMP inspection checklist covers facilities, equipment, finished and unfinished materials, containers, and labeling, framing GMP as a practical way to reduce adulteration and misbranding risk.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: manufacturing-control expectations affecting packaging cleanliness and contamination risk. Material/packaging relevance: packaging components, contact surfaces, containers, and labeling handled in manufacture/storage. Product/formulation relevance: contamination-sensitive products, microbiologically vulnerable products, and packaging exposed to unsuitable storage are higher risk. Testing/compliance implication: packaging screens should include supplier GMP maturity, cleanliness controls, storage conditions, and component handling, not only material properties. Use in screening: disqualify lightweight routes relying on immature conversion or handling likely to increase contamination, variability, or label/closure failure risk. Limitation: GMP guidance is an operations-control filter, not a chemistry filter.
Source name: Good Manufacturing Practice Guidelines/Inspection Checklist for Cosmetics — U.S. Food and Drug Administration — https://www.fda.gov/cosmetics/cosmetics-guidance-documents/good-manufacturing-practice-gmp-guidelinesinspection-checklist-cosmetics

Summary of what was found: MoCRA requires FDA to establish cosmetic GMP regulations and requires responsible persons to maintain safety-substantiation records based on scientifically robust methods; FDA draft GMP guidance notes alignment work with ISO 22716.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: U.S. movement toward stronger GMP and documented safety substantiation. Material/packaging relevance: all packaging materials and dispensing systems used in U.S. cosmetics, especially where safety claims depend on packaging compatibility. Product/formulation relevance: eye-area products, long-wear systems, products with actives, solvents, or fragrances need stronger substantiation. Testing/compliance implication: companies should keep documentary evidence for compatibility, migration risk assessment, and pack performance as part of defensible safety substantiation. Use in screening: make documentable scientific substantiation mandatory before lightweight recommendations. Limitation: GMP rulemaking is evolving, so operational expectations may continue to develop.
Source name: Modernization of Cosmetics Regulation Act of 2022 / Draft Guidance for Industry: Cosmetic Good Manufacturing Practices — U.S. Food and Drug Administration — https://www.fda.gov/cosmetics/cosmetics-laws-regulations/modernization-cosmetics-regulation-act-2022-mocra ; https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/draft-guidance-industry-cosmetic-good-manufacturing-practices

Summary of what was found: Cosmetics Europe’s stability guideline says stability testing should assure physical integrity, chemical stability, microbiological stability, and compatibility between contents and container.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: cosmetic stability and package-compatibility testing design. Material/packaging relevance: all primary packaging, including plastics, glass, aluminum tubes, labels, and closures. Product/formulation relevance: fragrance variants, volatile systems, emulsions, powders, and products exposed to temperature cycling or light. Testing/compliance implication: compatibility work should cover product appearance, odor, pH, viscosity, changes in the container, weight change, microbial performance, orientation testing, climatic/transport stress, barrier properties, label loss, cracking, and corrosion of internal lacquers in aluminum tubes. Use in screening: turn this into the minimum baseline test matrix for proposed lightweight materials. Limitation: guideline is principle-based and flexible; it does not give one mandatory schedule for all products.
Source name: Guidelines on Stability Testing of Cosmetic Products — Cosmetics Europe / CTFA — https://cosmeticseurope.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Guidelines_on_Stability_Testing_of_Cosmetics_CE-CTFA_-_2004.pdf

Summary of what was found: CTPA states GMP is a legal requirement under UK/EU cosmetic rules, ISO 22716 provides a presumption of compliance, and changing to a different container can create compatibility and preservative-performance risk.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: GMP expectation, ISO 22716 role, and refill/container-change safety risks. Material/packaging relevance: all packaging systems, with special relevance to refills, alternate containers, and reuse models. Product/formulation relevance: products dependent on preservative efficacy and controlled headspace/contact conditions. Testing/compliance implication: if a lightweight route changes container, closure, headspace, or consumer refill behavior, compatibility and preservative performance should be revalidated rather than assumed transferable. Use in screening: include a same-formula-new-container-equals-new-compatibility-assessment rule, especially for refill and reuse concepts. Limitation: CTPA guidance does not replace full product-specific safety assessment.
Source name: Frequently Asked Questions — Cosmetic, Toiletry and Perfumery Association — https://www.ctpa.org.uk/faqs

Summary of what was found: Intertek states cosmetic packaging testing is essential because harmful chemicals can be released from packaging into product; it highlights migration testing and substances such as phthalates, PAAs, PAHs, bisphenols, heavy metals, and NIAS.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: migration/extractables-style risks and chemical screening of packaging. Material/packaging relevance: plastics, recycled plastics, printed/decorated packs, laminated structures, and packs with additives or contaminants. Product/formulation relevance: solvent-rich, long-shelf-life, or leave-on formulations are especially sensitive to migration risk. Testing/compliance implication: lightweight candidates using PCR, novel additives, inks, adhesives, or multilayer structures should undergo targeted chemical-risk studies, not just visual stability. Use in screening: apply enhanced chemical package safety review for recycled-content plastics and complex converting stacks. Limitation: Intertek describes available testing approaches; exact protocols need tailoring to formula, contact conditions, and region.
Source name: Cosmetics Packaging Testing — Intertek — https://www.intertek.com/beauty-personal-care/cosmetics-packaging-testing/

Summary of what was found: Intertek’s cosmetic stability-testing guidance says compatibility studies should use final packaging and may assess degradation, discoloration, leakage, loss of efficacy, microbiological integrity, package functionality, pump priming, dose delivery, locking resistance, opening force, adhesive strength, and drop tests.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: practical stability, leakage, dispensing, and pack-function testing for cosmetics. Material/packaging relevance: bottles, jars, pumps, airless packs, lipstick mechanisms, caps, and decorated/assembled packs. Product/formulation relevance: emulsions, alcohol-containing products, active formulas, pumps, airless systems, and transport-exposed packs. Testing/compliance implication: screening must include mechanical and user-function tests, not only chemistry; closures, pumps, and mechanisms can fail even when resin compatibility looks acceptable. Use in screening: define a separate performance gate for leakage, dispense accuracy, opening/actuation force, drop resistance, and distribution stress. Limitation: source describes a toolkit rather than pass/fail thresholds; brand-specific specs are required.
Source name: Cosmetic Stability Testing — Intertek — https://www.intertek.com/beauty-personal-care/cosmetic-stability-testing/

Summary of what was found: Intertek links EU cosmetic-packaging assessment to Regulation 1223/2009 and stresses toxicological consideration of migration from packaging, including recycled materials and NIAS.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: toxicological review of packaging migration in EU cosmetics, including recycled-content packaging. Material/packaging relevance: recycled plastics, conventional plastics, and packaging with unknown or non-intentionally added substances. Product/formulation relevance: leave-on products, products with longer residence time in pack, and formulas likely to extract migrants. Testing/compliance implication: any PCR/rPCR route should be screened for origin, decontamination, NIAS, and migration, not just recycled-content percentage. Use in screening: create a special caution flag for recycled-content plastics intended for direct formula contact, especially for prestige skincare, sunscreens, and sensitive formulations. Limitation: service-oriented source; supports the need for testing but does not provide universal migration limits for cosmetics.
Source name: Regulatory & Safety Assessment of Cosmetic Packaging in Europe, Including Recycled Materials — Intertek — https://www.intertek.com/assuris/cosmetics/safety/regulatory-and-safety-assessment-of-cosmetic-packaging-eu/

Summary of what was found: Berlin Packaging provides practical compatibility guidance for PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP, while emphasizing that compatibility testing is the final determinant.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: base-level material selection for PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP. Material/packaging relevance: rigid bottles, jars, tubes, and squeezable formats. Product/formulation relevance: shampoos, conditioners, lotions, acid/caustic systems, dried products, and products where solvent exposure matters. Testing/compliance implication: PET, PE, and PP are good lightweight candidates, but HDPE/LDPE should be screened against solvents, PP against cold-impact conditions, and PET against relevant handling caveats. Use in screening: treat commodity polyolefins and PET as baseline lightweight candidates, then eliminate based on solvent, temperature, and barrier needs. Limitation: Berlin gives relative guidance only and says compatibility testing remains decisive.
Source name: Plastic Resin & Quality Facts / About Plastic Resins for Packaging — Berlin Packaging — https://www.berlinpackaging.com/insights/plastic-resin-quality-facts/ ; https://www.berlinpackaging.com/about-plastic-resins/

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging describes cosmetics-relevant materials and explains where PET, PETG/copolyesters, PMMA, SAN/AS, ABS, glass, and aluminum are typically used.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: beauty-packaging material-selection differences across premium and standard resin systems. Material/packaging relevance: PET, PETG/copolyesters, PMMA, SAN/AS, ABS, glass, and aluminum. Product/formulation relevance: clear packs, outer cosmetic shells, rigid luxury components, and applications where gloss/clarity or premium appearance matters. Testing/compliance implication: lightweight recommendations should distinguish between material in contact with formula and outer aesthetic shell, because PMMA and decorative premium materials may be external while another resin provides formula compatibility. Use in screening: separate structural/inner-contact material choice from premium outer-shell/aesthetic material choice. Limitation: high-level supplier guidance still needing formula-specific validation.
Source name: Packaging Materials for Cosmetic Containers — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/custom-services/materials/

Summary of what was found: APC technical notes caution that PMMA is susceptible to strong acids, alcohols, and ethers despite its clarity and UV/weather resistance; ABS can discolor under UV unless protected.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: material-specific limitations that can undermine attractive lightweight premium plastics. Material/packaging relevance: PMMA/acrylic and ABS used in luxe caps, jars, overpacks, and cosmetic components. Product/formulation relevance: alcohol-containing formulas, acidic systems, outdoor/light-sensitive use cases, and packs with UV exposure. Testing/compliance implication: PMMA should be treated cautiously for alcohol-rich or chemically aggressive formulations, while ABS needs UV/color protection and should not be assumed inert in decorative applications. Use in screening: flag PMMA and ABS as premium-use-with-targeted-compatibility/aging-review rather than general drop-in lightweight substitutes. Limitation: guidance notes rather than statutory standards.
Source name: Types of Plastic to Use in Packaging Products / Polymethyl Methacrylate — APC Packaging Tech Center — https://techcenter.apcpackaging.com/lets-talk-plastic ; https://techcenter.apcpackaging.com/lets-talk-pmma

Summary of what was found: Aptar shows dispensing systems need their own compatibility review: the GSA pump uses a metal-free pathway for compatibility across water-based and high-viscosity formulas and can be qualified for e-commerce/ISTA-6 with specific clips.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: pump-pathway chemistry, dispensing performance, and transport robustness. Material/packaging relevance: pumps, closures, mixed-material dispensers, PCR PP components, and e-commerce configurations. Product/formulation relevance: lotions, soaps, skincare, color cosmetics, and water-based or viscous formulas where metal contact can be problematic. Testing/compliance implication: lightweighting the bottle alone is insufficient; closure/pump pathway must be compatible, leak-resistant, and transit-capable. Use in screening: require separate assessment for dispensers, springs, seals, and contact-pathway materials, especially when switching to mono-material or PCR-heavy designs. Limitation: performance varies by container shape, size, and formula, which Aptar notes.
Source name: GSA Dispensing Pump with Metal-Free Pathway — Aptar Beauty — https://aptar.com/en-us/products/gsa-dispensing-pump-with-metal-free-pathway

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Derma Airless example shows special formulas may require specific inner-contact materials: the refillable system uses a PP inner layer for optimal compatibility and medium- to high-viscosity formulations.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: inner-layer material selection in airless/refillable systems. Material/packaging relevance: airless packs, refill systems, and multi-component packs with distinct inner and outer layers. Product/formulation relevance: higher-viscosity skincare and dermocosmetic-style formulas requiring protected dispensing. Testing/compliance implication: compatibility must be assessed at the internal contact layer, not inferred from the outer pack material. Use in screening: in luxe and refillable concepts, document which layer contacts formula and screen that layer first; outer materials are secondary. Limitation: product-specific example rather than universal rule, but strong evidence that contact layer and show layer may differ materially.
Source name: Derma Airless — Quadpack — https://catalogue.quadpack.com/product/14174916/derma-airless

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s functional barrier paper range shows paper solutions can be engineered for moisture, water-vapor, grease, and oxygen-barrier needs, but those functions depend on coatings and tailored structures rather than plain paper.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: barrier dependence of paper-based flexible packaging. Material/packaging relevance: barrier-coated paper, sealed papers, and paper structures proposed as substitutes for multilayer plastics or aluminum-based structures. Product/formulation relevance: non-food and personal-care applications needing moisture, grease, oxygen, or vapor protection. Testing/compliance implication: paper-based lightweight routes should not be recommended for wet, oily, aromatic, or oxygen-sensitive formulas unless barrier chemistry, sealability, and line performance are validated. Use in screening: classify plain paper/fiber as non-contact or dry-product friendly by default and only advance coated/barrier-engineered paper for demanding formulations. Limitation: supplier product-family information; suitability depends on specific barrier grade and construction.
Source name: Functional Barrier Papers — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/flexible-packaging/functional-barrier-papers/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso reinforces that fiber-based packs need barrier technologies for demanding contents, including humidity control, sealing, airtightness, light protection, oxygen/aroma barrier, heat resistance, and grease proofing.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: dependence of paperboard on barrier coatings for demanding product protection. Material/packaging relevance: barrier-coated paperboards, paper-based trays, tubs, and fiber formats considered for cosmetics or personal care. Product/formulation relevance: products vulnerable to moisture, oxygen, aroma loss, grease penetration, or leakage. Testing/compliance implication: barrier-coated paperboard must be screened for protection performance and coating/recycling implications; switching from plastic to fiber is not chemistry-neutral. Use in screening: fiber-based lightweight routes require an explicit barrier map covering moisture, oxygen, grease, light, seal, and recyclability trade-offs. Limitation: source is oriented to pack performance rather than cosmetic toxicology, so chemical compatibility with specific formulations needs separate review.
Source name: Barrier Boards — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/barrier-boards

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki shows both opportunity and caution for molded fiber: it offers rigidity, cushioning, shock absorbency, and recyclability, but molded fiber is moisture absorbent and higher-performance smooth molded fiber may need stronger barriers.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: performance envelope and limitation of molded fiber. Material/packaging relevance: molded pulp/fiber inserts, trays, protectors, and potentially smooth molded fiber components. Product/formulation relevance: secondary/tertiary packaging, fragile packs, and transit protection; not inherently suited for direct contact with wet/oily formulas without barrier design. Testing/compliance implication: molded fiber is a strong replacement for plastic inserts and protective components, but direct-contact or wet-use proposals need barrier validation and contamination/odor review. Use in screening: treat molded fiber as preferred for non-contact inserts and protective parts but caution for direct-contact, humid, or oil-exposed use. Limitation: supplier examples are strongest for transport/protective use.
Source name: Why choose molded fiber? / Huhtamaki Fiber Solutions – The Future Redesigned — Huhtamaki — https://www.huhtamaki.com/en-za/fiber-packaging/sustainability/why-choose-molded-fiber/ ; https://www.huhtamaki.com/en/highlights/sustainability/the-future-is-fiber/

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s home and personal care portfolio shows mono-material PE/PP and selected paper solutions can be engineered for categories such as liquid soap, detergent, wipes, and hygiene products, with barrier options, sealing windows, PCR options, and recyclable design claims.
Detailed description of what was found: Compatibility or regulatory issue covered: practical use of mono-material PE/PP and paper-based flexible packaging in personal-care/home-care applications. Material/packaging relevance: mono PE, mono PP, kraft-paper solutions, spouted pouches, stand-up pouches, and wet-wipe packs. Product/formulation relevance: liquid soap, detergents, wipes, and dry tabs as analogs for screening mono-material flexible formats in personal-care categories. Testing/compliance implication: mono-material flexible packaging can work, but suitability depends on barrier level, sealing performance, shelf-life needs, and production compatibility. Use in screening: use mono-PE/PP flexibles as candidate lightweight routes for sachets/refills where barrier demand is manageable and recycling design can be preserved; do not treat them as universal replacements for all high-barrier multilayers. Limitation: examples are mostly home/personal care rather than prestige cosmetics, so migration and sensory compatibility should be confirmed for fragrance-heavy or active-rich beauty formulas.
Source name: Sustainable Home & Personal Care Solutions — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/flexible-packaging/sustainable-home-and-personal-care-solutions/

Summary of what was found: IMPORTANT STUDY ASSUMPTION: For this report, logistics cost will be treated as directly proportional to packaging weight; effects of package size, shape, cube utilization, dimensional weight, and space optimization are outside the analytical scope.
Detailed description of what was found: This is a project-defined modeling assumption, not an external market source. The report will assume logistics cost changes in direct proportion to packaging weight reduction because the study scope is limited to material-based lightweighting and intentionally excludes size, shape, and space-utilization effects. Evidence rows S07-001 to S07-003 explain real-world dimensional/volumetric billing, but for this report those effects should be treated as limitations and not modeled. This note must be carried into the final report methodology and limitations section so that recommendations stay aligned with the client-defined scope.
Source name: Project scope assumption defined by user/client; no external source

Summary of what was found: FedEx states parcel charges are based on the greater of dimensional weight or actual weight, so material weight reduction alone does not always reduce parcel cost.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: actual weight versus dimensional weight in parcel shipping. Packaging/material relevance: any beauty shipper, mailer, carton, or e-commerce outer pack. Actual versus dimensional relevance: FedEx defines dimensional weight as the space a package occupies relative to actual weight and says billing is based on whichever is greater. Cost/emissions/damage implication: a lighter package may not reduce parcel spend if the pack still bills on dimensional weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: apply this as a screening rule before claiming freight savings from lighter materials; determine whether each SKU is actual-weight-billed or dimensional-weight-billed. Limitation: carrier-specific parcel logic, not a universal all-mode freight rule.
Source name: What is Dimensional Weight? — FedEx — https://www.fedex.com/en-us/shipping/packaging/what-is-dimensional-weight.html

Summary of what was found: DHL eCommerce says parcel chargeable weight is based on the greater of total actual weight and total dimensional weight.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: parcel/e-commerce chargeable weight. Packaging/material relevance: cosmetics and personal care mailers, cartons, and tertiary packaging used in D2C shipping. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL instructs shippers to calculate dimensional weight from package dimensions and compare it to actual weight; invoice charges are based on the greater value. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter materials can reduce shipping cost only when actual weight remains the billing driver or when material changes affect pack density enough to change chargeable weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: treat parcel SKUs as separate from pallet freight and avoid assuming every gram saved converts into carrier savings. Limitation: formula and divisor are carrier-program-specific.
Source name: Chargeable Shipping Weight and Cost — DHL eCommerce — https://www.dhl.com/us-en/home/ecommerce/business-help-center/chargeable-weight.html

Summary of what was found: DHL Global Forwarding shows chargeable weight differs by mode: air uses volumetric conversion, ocean LCL uses volume, rail uses another density ratio, and road varies by carrier/location.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: multimodal freight charging logic. Packaging/material relevance: international cosmetics supply chains moving components, finished goods, and e-commerce replenishment by air, ocean, rail, or road. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL lists volumetric-weight formulas for air, ocean LCL, and rail, and notes road can vary case by case. Cost/emissions/damage implication: material-based lightweighting has the clearest shipping-cost benefit where freight is weight-constrained; where freight is cube/volume-priced, logistics benefits may depend more on density/loading efficiency than grams alone. Use in lightweighting evaluation: test each packaging route against its real transport mode and billing basis before monetizing savings. Limitation: formulas are directional education guidance, not negotiated customer tariffs.
Source name: Calculating Chargeable Weight by Air, Ocean, Road and Rail — DHL Global Forwarding — https://www.dhl.com/in-en/home/global-forwarding/freight-forwarding-education-center/calculating-chargeable-weights.html

Summary of what was found: ISTA guidance says sustainable packaging should aim for optimum protection with minimum packaging and describes a reduce-to-damage approach.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: trade-off between reducing packaging and preserving distribution performance. Packaging/material relevance: primary, secondary, and tertiary packs for fragile beauty items such as glass bottles, droppers, and premium sets. Actual versus dimensional relevance: not a billing source, but essential for interpreting whether weight reduction is economically safe. Cost/emissions/damage implication: reducing packaging without validating protection can raise damage, returns, and replacement freight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use this as a methodological principle for material-downgauge or material-substitution recommendations. Limitation: testing philosophy source, not product-category-specific pass/fail thresholds for cosmetics.
Source name: Guidelines for Selecting and Using ISTA Test Procedures — International Safe Transit Association — https://ista.org/docs/ISTA_2017_Guidelines.pdf

Summary of what was found: ISTA says 3-Series and retailer/carrier-specific 6-Series tests simulate transport hazards across parcel, LTL, truckload, retail, and e-commerce systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: protective-packaging performance under real distribution hazards. Packaging/material relevance: lighter corrugated shippers, inserts, trays, paper void fill, air pillows, and alternative protective formats for beauty products. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; testing determines whether lighter material solutions remain viable through parcel and retail distribution even if they reduce weight. Cost/emissions/damage implication: logistics economics must include hazard exposure and damage probability, not only freight weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: require transport-test evidence for fragile or premium cosmetic packs, especially those moving through parcel or e-commerce channels. Limitation: ISTA describes test families and use cases, but detailed protocols may require separate access.
Source name: Test Procedures — International Safe Transit Association — https://ista.org/test_procedures.php

Summary of what was found: Amazon’s Ships in Product Packaging program can reduce added packaging and fulfillment fees but requires product testing; fragile, liquid, or sharp items may need ISTA 6 lab testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: e-commerce fulfillment cost, packaging minimization, and protection validation. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce products including liquids, breakables, and premium presentation packs. Actual versus dimensional relevance: not a carrier-rate page, but links packaging design to fulfillment cost and truck reduction. Cost/emissions/damage implication: eliminating added packaging can reduce fulfillment costs and carbon footprint, but testing is mandatory for higher-risk products. Use in lightweighting evaluation: e-commerce-ready packs must pass channel-specific performance tests before logistics savings are counted. Limitation: Amazon fulfillment program, not a universal retail standard.
Source name: Ships in Product Packaging — Amazon — https://sell.amazon.com/programs/custom-packaging

Summary of what was found: Pregis states damaged products can cost retailers millions, and a single return can incur hundreds of dollars when all functions are counted.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: hidden cost of damage and returns. Packaging/material relevance: fragile cosmetics, glass bottles, gift sets, and e-commerce parcel shipments where under-protection can negate material savings. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; freight savings cannot be evaluated separately from damage economics. Cost/emissions/damage implication: if lighter materials reduce protective performance, replacement shipments, reverse logistics, labor, and lost sales can overwhelm packaging-weight savings. Use in lightweighting evaluation: assess lightweighting options on total delivered-cost risk, not only pack material cost or freight cost. Limitation: supplier-published directional figures rather than category-specific cosmetics benchmarks.
Source name: How Damaged Product Impacts Your Profitability — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/globalassets/white-papers-and-case-studies/2019---updated/white-paper-6-part-analysis-damaged-product-impact-2019.pdf

Summary of what was found: NRF and Appriss reported $743 billion of merchandise returned in 2023, providing context for the scale of returns as a retail cost burden.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: retail returns as a cost burden. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce because packaging-related damage contributes to the broader returns cost environment. Actual versus dimensional relevance: none directly; contextual evidence only. Cost/emissions/damage implication: returns create additional handling, processing, transport, and loss costs. Use in lightweighting evaluation: background evidence for why avoiding avoidable damage-related returns matters; pair with damage-specific sources rather than using alone. Limitation: not all returns are packaging damage and the article focuses partly on return fraud.
Source name: NRF and Appriss Retail Report: $743 Billion in Merchandise Returned in 2023 — National Retail Federation — https://nrf.com/media-center/press-releases/nrf-and-appriss-retail-report-743-billion-merchandise-returned-2023

Summary of what was found: DHL says lightweight materials can lower shipping costs and sustainable packaging can reduce product damage, warehouse clutter, shipping weights, and long-term logistics costs.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: business case linking packaging choices to shipping cost, space use, and damage. Packaging/material relevance: paper, recycled-content, reusable, and minimalist tertiary/e-commerce formats. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL notes lighter materials reduce shipping costs while also linking savings to shipping volume and operational efficiency, reinforcing that weight is only one part of transport economics. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter materials can reduce fees and emissions, but protection and scalability still need validation. Use in lightweighting evaluation: general logistics-sector support that packaging-weight reductions can matter commercially. Limitation: high-level guidance rather than SKU-level cost modeling.
Source name: Sustainable Packaging: 8 Examples & Solutions for You — DHL — https://www.dhl.com/discover/en-global/logistics-advice/sustainability-and-green-logistics/sustainable-packaging-in-logistics

Summary of what was found: Mondi states lighter, well-protected e-commerce packaging using recycled and fresh paper can reduce waste and last-mile carbon emissions.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: last-mile transport efficiency and emissions. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce shippers, paper mailers, corrugated packs, and fresh-fibre/recycled-fibre paper solutions. Actual versus dimensional relevance: the source mixes lighter and more compact packs; for this report only the material-weight portion should be carried forward. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter paper-based transport packaging can reduce emissions, but Mondi ties this to adequate strength and protection rather than weight reduction alone. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use for paper-based tertiary packaging routes while separating valid material-downgauge options from out-of-scope size redesign. Limitation: statement mixes weight and compactness.
Source name: The last mile in eCommerce — Mondi — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2024/the-last-mile-unpacked-driving-ecommerce-with-sustainable-packaging/

Summary of what was found: International Paper says corrugated inserts and specialty packaging can replace plastic foam and loose-fill for damage-prone liquid-filled plastic and glass bottles.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: protective-packaging substitution for fragile bottles in transit. Packaging/material relevance: cosmetics and personal care because the source specifically names liquid-filled plastic and glass bottles and identifies corrugated inserts as alternatives to foam and peanuts. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; replacing heavier or more material-intensive protective formats may improve recyclability and potentially reduce overall shipper material use. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports paper-based protective inserts for fragile liquid products, but protection must remain adequate. Use in lightweighting evaluation: evidence that corrugated inserts are a credible tertiary/secondary protective route for bottle-based beauty SKUs. Limitation: no quantified cost or weight delta.
Source name: Paper-based Recyclable Packaging Fuels Design Innovations — International Paper — https://www.internationalpaper.com/resources/recycling/article/paper-based-recyclable-packaging-fuels-design-innovations

Summary of what was found: Pregis says its air-cushion system is more than 99% air and can reduce packaging material, transport costs, storage space, and return-related impacts.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: lightweight protective packaging for parcels. Packaging/material relevance: tertiary packaging choices such as air pillows for void fill and cushioning in beauty e-commerce shipments. Actual versus dimensional relevance: actual-weight advantage is strong because the protective medium is mostly air; dimensional billing may still limit savings for oversized cartons. Cost/emissions/damage implication: air pillows are attractive where lightweight protection matters and where storage-space reductions matter. Use in lightweighting evaluation: treat air cushions as a baseline lightweight protective option against which paper void fill, molded fibre, and corrugated inserts can be compared. Limitation: recyclability and curbside acceptance depend on local systems and film type; source is supplier-authored.
Source name: Eco Friendly Air Cushions AirSpeed — Pregis — https://uk.pregis.com/globalassets/resources/eu/sustainability/sustai~1.pdf

Summary of what was found: Sealed Air says kraft paper mailers weigh less and generate less solid waste than corrugated boxes, giving postage savings, and are made with 100% recycled paper fibers.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: tertiary-pack choice between mailers and boxes. Packaging/material relevance: lightweight, non-fragile beauty items and flat/soft goods where a paper mailer can replace a corrugated box. Actual versus dimensional relevance: supports actual-weight and postage savings; strongest where the item can safely ship in a mailer rather than a box. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter tertiary material can reduce postage and waste, but only for SKUs that do not need rigid protection. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use as a material route for mass-market and refill/accessory subcategories, not for fragile glass or luxe presentation-dependent packs unless validated. Limitation: package-format suitability is product-dependent and not a universal corrugated substitute.
Source name: Kraft Paper Mailers — Sealed Air — https://www.sealedair.com/uk/products/protective-packaging/mailers/paper-mailers

Summary of what was found: Smurfit says honeycomb cardboard is lightweight, fills gaps/voids, prevents movement, offers stacking strength up to 800 kPa, and can lower shipping costs without compromising stability.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: paper-based protective inserts and void-fill/stabilization. Packaging/material relevance: tertiary protective packaging and replacement of heavier protective components for premium gift sets, fragile glass, or multi-item beauty bundles. Actual versus dimensional relevance: primarily actual-weight and stability related. Cost/emissions/damage implication: honeycomb paper is a lighter protective option that still provides compression and stacking performance. Use in lightweighting evaluation: include honeycomb paperboard as a candidate to replace foam blocks, denser inserts, or multi-material protective components where compression and movement control matter. Limitation: no direct beauty-specific case or cost comparison.
Source name: Hexacomb Packaging — Smurfit Kappa — https://www.smurfitkappa.com/products-and-services/packaging/hexacomb-packaging

Summary of what was found: Smurfit says corrugated cardboard pallets are 70% lighter than wood pallets and can reduce shipping costs, carbon emissions, and manual-handling strain.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: pallet-level tertiary packaging and handling efficiency. Packaging/material relevance: palletized bulk shipments of finished beauty goods, components, and secondary-pack assemblies. Actual versus dimensional relevance: pure actual-weight benefit at pallet/load level; pallet tare directly affects truck and air-freight payload utilization. Cost/emissions/damage implication: cutting pallet tare can reduce load weight and potentially improve manual handling and safety. Use in lightweighting evaluation: useful for tertiary-pack screening at distribution-center/export level where wood pallets are avoidable. Limitation: performance depends on load profile, moisture exposure, and export handling conditions.
Source name: Cardboard Pallets — Smurfit Kappa — https://www.smurfitkappa.com/uk/products-and-services/packaging/corrugated-pallets

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer states that in luxury cosmetics, glass weight is often perceived as a sign of high value, even though lighter glass design improves sustainability.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: premium/luxe packaging’s intentional use of high-weight primary packs. Packaging/material relevance: fragrance, skincare, and luxury beauty packs using heavy glass. Actual versus dimensional relevance: mostly actual-weight issue; heavy glass increases pack tare and transport/handling burden. Cost/emissions/damage implication: premium/luxe brands may accept higher logistics cost because weight contributes to consumer-perceived value, but lightweight glass design is increasingly considered. Use in lightweighting evaluation: supports splitting recommendations into luxe and mass-market tracks because premium packaging has a different value equation and cannot be screened on logistics cost alone. Limitation: perception/value statements are segment-specific and should not be generalized across all beauty products.
Source name: Lightweight Packaging Design for Less — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/en/customer/products-solutions/cosmetics/sustainable-packaging-design/design-for-less

Summary of what was found: Berry/Amcor says plastic’s lighter weight can reduce the number of vehicles needed, allow more bottles per truck within pack-out limits, and had a cited 15% carbon-emission advantage for a plastic soap bottle versus glass.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: primary-pack material substitution and transport efficiency. Packaging/material relevance: personal care bottles and cosmetic body-care or hair-care formats where plastic can replace glass. Actual versus dimensional relevance: clear actual-weight transport case because lighter bottles can increase payload efficiency and lower indirect transport emissions. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports glass-to-plastic substitution where brand, compatibility, and recyclability allow; also implies lower breakage risk versus glass. Use in lightweighting evaluation: evidence for evaluating glass-to-PET/HDPE/PP transitions in mass-market SKUs and selected premium-adjacent categories. Limitation: aesthetics, compatibility, and premium-brand equity may limit applicability in luxe beauty.
Source name: Plastic or Glass? Why Choose Plastic Instead of Glass — Berry / Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/plastic-or-glass-how-do-you-decide

Summary of what was found: The Flexible Packaging Association says flexible packaging usually has lower fossil-fuel use, GHG emissions, and water use because of very light weight, efficient roll/flat shipping, and high product-to-package ratio.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: system-level transport and resource efficiency of flexible formats. Packaging/material relevance: refill pouches, sachets, flexible mailers, and lightweight films used in primary or tertiary beauty packaging. Actual versus dimensional relevance: strong actual-weight advantage because the format is very light and inbound materials ship flat or on rolls. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports flexible refill pouches and film-based routes as logistics-weight reduction candidates where breakage resistance and high product-to-package ratio matter. Use in lightweighting evaluation: directional basis for comparing refill/flexible routes against rigid bottles or jars in mass-market and e-commerce scenarios. Limitation: end-of-life and recyclability constraints for flexible formats need separate screening and should not be assumed favorable in every market.
Source name: Sustainable Packaging — Flexible Packaging Association — https://www.flexpack.org/sustainable-packaging

Summary of what was found: Sidel’s X-LITE Still shows industrialized ultra-lightweight PET in food and beverage: a 500 ml bottle weighing 6.5 g, with resin and energy savings versus a 12 g bottle.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: ultra-lightweight PET bottle design for non-pressurized beverages. Food/beverage use case: 500 ml still-water bottle. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: mass-market low-viscosity formats such as travel-size shampoo, body wash, micellar water, or refillable liquid-care bottles where PET is acceptable. Weight/material reduction relevance: 6.5 g versus 12 g directly evidences resin reduction potential. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market/value channels, not luxe. Compatibility/limitation notes: beverage-specific and does not prove suitability for oils, high alcohol, fragrances, pumps, or aggressive actives. Use in screening: keep as a top benchmark for rigid lightweight PET where cosmetic compatibility and handling requirements do not force thicker walls or complex dispensing systems.
Source name: X-LITE Packaging Solution — Sidel — https://www.sidel.com/en/services/x-lite-still/

Summary of what was found: ALPLA states plastic packaging weight has been reduced by an average of 25% since 1991 in Western Europe and reports a 1.5 L PET bottle reduced by 38% to 25 g.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight PET bottles at scale. Food/beverage use case: beverage bottles including a 1.5 L PET bottle reduced to 25 g. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shampoo, conditioner, body wash, and personal-care bottles where PET clarity and blow-molding infrastructure already exist. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantified large-scale bottle-weight reduction and long-term industry trend. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily mass-market; selected premium clear bottles possible where PET aesthetics are acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not address pumps, droppers, thick formulas, alcohol, or fragrance loss. Use in screening: use as evidence that lightweight PET is mature and scalable but requires formula, top-load, and closure-system screening.
Source name: Beverage Packaging Solutions End-to-End — ALPLA — https://www.alpla.com/en/markets/beverages

Summary of what was found: ALPLA reports Coca-Cola India launched a packaged drinking water bottle made from 100% recycled PET, supporting rPET as a mature route with circularity relevance.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: 100% rPET bottle. Food/beverage use case: packaged drinking water in India. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: clear or lightly tinted personal-care bottles where recycled content and PET’s lower mass versus glass are desirable. Weight/material reduction relevance: does not give bottle grams but confirms full-rPET food-contact bottle commercialization. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market, with some premium use if appearance requirements are met. Compatibility/limitation notes: evidence of recycled-content readiness, not direct cosmetics compatibility or luxury appearance; beauty use needs color, odor, clarity, and scuff-resistance checks. Use in screening: keep rPET as a serious candidate wherever virgin PET is acceptable, especially mass-market personal care.
Source name: ALPLA supports Coca-Cola in launching India’s first 100% recycled PET Bottle for packaged drinking water — ALPLA Blog — https://blog.alpla.com/en/blog/products-innovation/alpla-supports-coca-cola-launching-indias-first-100-recycled-pet-bottle

Summary of what was found: Berry’s thin-wall dairy cups are injection molded in 100% PP and described as lightweight and durable, proving thin-wall PP as a food lightweighting route.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: thin-wall polypropylene containers. Food/beverage use case: dairy cups for cottage cheese, fresh mozzarella, and yogurt. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: cream jars, balms, mask tubs, refill pods, and lightweight tubs for mass beauty/personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: thin-wall format reduces material through downgauging, aligned with material-based lightweighting scope. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market skincare and personal-care tubs; limited luxe use unless decoration or overpack elevates feel. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not prove performance with fragrant, oily, solvent-bearing, or active-rich formulas. Use in screening: keep thin-wall PP as a priority route for commodity jars/tubs and refill inserts where premium heft is not required.
Source name: Berry offers the latest in PP cups — Berry Global via Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/berry-offers-the-latest-in-pp-cups

Summary of what was found: Berry reports a tethered-cap collaboration with Coca-Cola Bottling Company United achieved a 10% closure weight reduction and made the overall pack around 20% lighter than the previous finish.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight beverage closure. Food/beverage use case: tethered cap for beverage bottles. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: lightweight disc-tops, flip-tops, screw caps, and non-dispensing closures for personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantifies closure-level and full-pack reduction, important because closures are often overlooked in beauty lightweighting. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market high-volume bottles where small gram savings scale quickly. Compatibility/limitation notes: beverage closures are mechanically simpler than pumps, droppers, airless systems, or prestige actuators. Use in screening: create a standalone closure-lightweighting workstream rather than focusing only on container bodies.
Source name: Tethered Cap for Coca Cola Bottling Company United — Berry Global via Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/14276-berry-cocacola-collaborate-to-implement-tethered-c

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmPrima PE Plus coffee pack is a mono-material PE structure with high-barrier performance, line speeds comparable to non-recyclable materials, and up to 38% lower carbon footprint than standard packs.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material PE high-barrier flexible pouch. Food/beverage use case: coffee packaging. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: refill pouches, sachets, and low-profile flexible packs for shampoos, body wash, soaps, or powdered beauty products. Weight/material reduction relevance: replaces heavier rigid formats or harder-to-recycle multi-material laminates with a lighter mono-material structure. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market refills; selective premium use where soft-pack convenience is acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: coffee aroma/moisture barrier does not automatically prove suitability for oils, alcohols, fragrances, or active skincare. Use in screening: advance mono-PE as a leading lightweight route but segment cosmetics by formulation aggressiveness before extrapolating food-pack performance.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle Ready Packaging Solutions for coffee — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/afla/coffee

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s Stonyfield pouch moved refrigerated yogurt from a multi-laminate structure to an all-PE film with a spout while removing metallized/foil layers and retaining heat resistance, barrier, hermetic seals, and premium graphics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: all-PE spouted pouch. Food/beverage use case: refrigerated yogurt pouch. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shampoo, baby care, body wash, cleanser, and liquid-soap refills or direct-use spouted packs. Weight/material reduction relevance: replacing multi-laminate structures with all-PE preserves pouch-level lightweighting and improves recyclability directionally. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and family-size refills; some premium opportunity for modern refill systems. Compatibility/limitation notes: proves barrier, sealing, and production validation for yogurt, not direct compatibility with essential oils, alcohol-rich products, or aggressive surfactants. Use in screening: place all-PE spouted pouches near the top of the shortlist for refill-led beauty routes, subject to formula and fitment testing.
Source name: Amcor collaborates to launch first all-PE spouted pouch — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/media/news/amcor-stonyfield-cpna-spouted-pouch

Summary of what was found: Mondi says BarrierPack Recyclable is a fully recyclable all-PE high-barrier laminate that is lighter than PET/PE laminates of the same thickness, runs on existing lines, and is offered for food, pet food, home and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: all-PE high-barrier flexible laminate/pouch. Food/beverage use case: confectionery and food. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: refill pouches, stand-up pouches, shaped pouches, and flexible packs for home/personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: source states the structure is lighter than PET/PE laminates of the same thickness. Premium versus mass-market fit: attractive for mass-market refills and scalable FMCG formats; limited premium use where soft packs are acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: barrier and line compatibility are supported, but beauty formulas still require chemical compatibility, odor, and stress-testing work. Use in screening: treat as one of the strongest transferable routes because the supplier explicitly spans food through home/personal care.
Source name: Mondi’s BarrierPack Recyclable helps consumer goods brands advance their circular economy goals — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2020/mondis-barrierpack-recyclable-helps-consumer-goods-brands-advance-their-circular-economy-goals/

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s home and personal care page shows the AmPrima PE Plus family already positioned for personal care, with up to 54% lower carbon footprint than standard packs plus high clarity/transparency for shelf appeal.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE recycle-ready flexible pouch for home and personal care. Food/beverage use case: cross-over proof from food-style mono-PE flexible platforms into personal care. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: body wash, shampoo, soap, personal-care refills, and flexible refill formats. Weight/material reduction relevance: characterizes pouches as lightweight and easy to carry/store. Premium versus mass-market fit: clearest for mass-market; selected premium brands could use it where refill systems matter more than rigid heft. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not prove suitability for all beauty categories such as prestige skincare with high sensory expectations or solvent-sensitive formulas. Use in screening: evidence that mono-PE flexible routes have already moved from food-style technology platforms into personal care at scale.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/afla/home-and-personal-care

Summary of what was found: Tetra Pak says a paper-based barrier can replace the aluminum-foil layer in aseptic cartons while maintaining comparable food protection and shelf life; one example reached 87% renewable content and 26% package carbon-footprint reduction.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper-based barrier in carton packaging replacing aluminum foil. Food/beverage use case: aseptic soy-milk carton on high-speed filling equipment. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: niche opportunities in refill cartons, low-viscosity liquid refills, or paper-barrier workstreams. Weight/material reduction relevance: simplifies material structure directionally and cuts package footprint in the cited example. Premium versus mass-market fit: more realistic for mass-market refill concepts than premium main packs. Compatibility/limitation notes: complex barrier structure built for aseptic liquid food and specialized filling systems; not a near-term mainstream replacement for bottles, jars, or pump packs. Use in screening: keep on the longlist for refill/carton concepts, but filter out early for most mainstream cosmetic primary packaging.
Source name: Tetra Pak extends paper-based barrier packaging to high-speed lines in Asia — Tetra Pak — https://www.tetrapak.com/en-in/about-tetra-pak/news-and-events/newsarchive/tetra-pak-extends-paper-based-barrier-to-high-speed-lines

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmFiber Performance Paper is used for dry-food formats such as confectionery, bars, cookies, cereals, dry beverages, and dry mixes, and runs on existing flexible-packaging equipment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper-based high-barrier flexible packaging. Food/beverage use case: dry-food formats such as bars, cereals, cookies, and dry-mix sachets. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: powder products, dry sample sachets, sheet-mask overwraps, or selective secondary/overwrap applications. Weight/material reduction relevance: materially light flexible paper structures can replace some plastic-heavy small-format wraps. Premium versus mass-market fit: mostly mass-market or niche eco-positioned brands; less suited to luxe unless used as secondary component with strong decoration. Compatibility/limitation notes: evidence should not be stretched to oily, wet, alcohol-rich, or fragrance-loaded cosmetics without separate validation. Use in screening: advance only for dry-product beauty formats and selective paper-overwrap formats, not mainstream wet primary packs.
Source name: High-Barrier Performance Paper Packaging Launches in North America — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/media/news/paper-packaging-launch-north-america

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso says FiberLight Tec creates lighter boards with strength and stiffness using less raw material; Performa Light folding carton is more than 5% lighter than without the treatment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight folding-carton paperboard. Food/beverage use case: premium food folding carton. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: secondary packaging such as folding cartons, sleeves, gift cartons, and premium paperboard outer packs. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantified board weight reduction while maintaining strength and printability. Premium versus mass-market fit: suitable for both, especially premium/luxe beauty where tactile quality, stiffness, and print surface matter. Compatibility/limitation notes: secondary-pack transfer route, not a primary wet-contact container solution. Use in screening: include as a key secondary-pack route because it addresses material-based lightweighting without changing pack shape or footprint.
Source name: Light-weight materials, efficient production – How technological innovation shapes the future of food packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/newsroom/news/2024/7/technological-innovation-shapes-the-future-of-food-packaging

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki’s blueloop portfolio offers mono-material packaging in paper, PE, PP, and PET across food, pet food, beverages, home and personal care, and healthcare while aiming to minimize material use and improve recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material flexible packaging platforms in paper, PE, PP, and PET. Food/beverage use case: food and beverage flexible packs. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: home and personal care flexible packs, sachets, pouches, and refill structures. Weight/material reduction relevance: source frames solutions as minimizing material use while advancing recyclability. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market FMCG, with selected premium refill applications possible. Compatibility/limitation notes: portfolio-level source proves platform breadth rather than a single detailed specification. Use in screening: justify including mono-material PE/PP/PET/paper flexible structures in the longlist for refill and sachet workstreams.
Source name: Flexible Packaging — Huhtamaki — https://www.huhtamaki.com/en-in/flexible-packaging/

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki’s foodservice range shows molded fiber, fluted fiber packaging, and folding cartons as lightweight fiber routes, with fluted packaging also listed for cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: molded fiber, fluted fiber packaging, and folding cartons. Food/beverage use case: foodservice plates, bowls, trays, boxes, and beverage-related lids/containers. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: molded fiber inserts, trays, lids, fluted protective structures, and lightweight fiber-based secondary packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: fiber structures can replace heavier plastic inserts or some mixed-material secondary components. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest in mass-market and eco-positioned beauty; selective premium use possible for molded inserts or high-design fiber packs. Compatibility/limitation notes: molded fiber should still be treated mainly as secondary/tertiary or dry-contact packaging unless coating and formulation testing are added. Use in screening: bring forward molded fiber, fluted fiber, and folding-carton routes for secondary/tertiary packaging, not mainstream wet-fill primary containers.
Source name: Foodservice packaging products — Huhtamaki — https://www.huhtamaki.com/en-gb/foodservice-uk/products/

Summary of what was found: International Paper’s wine-shipping page says corrugated cushioning, molded fiber inserts, and die-cut dividers protect bottles while engineers aim to minimize total package weight.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: corrugated cushioning, molded fiber inserts, and die-cut dividers. Food/beverage use case: wine bottle shipping protection. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: tertiary and e-commerce packaging for glass perfume bottles, serum bottles, gift sets, and premium skincare kits. Weight/material reduction relevance: explicitly links protective insert design with minimizing total package weight. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to both, especially premium/luxe glass-heavy SKUs. Compatibility/limitation notes: transit protection source, not shelf packaging or direct product contact. Use in screening: prioritize molded-fiber and corrugated-insert routes to replace heavier foam/plastic protection in beauty e-commerce.
Source name: Wine Packaging Solutions | Recyclable Wine Shipping Boxes — International Paper — https://www.internationalpaper.com/packaging/corrugated-packaging/wine-packaging

Summary of what was found: Pregis’ EverTec cushioned mailer is a two-layer paper mailer, lists cosmetics among ideal applications, and is positioned as a lightweight small-box replacement with typical weights from 0.54 oz to 1.98 oz.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper cushioned mailer for parcel/e-commerce. Food/beverage use case: functionally analogous to light e-commerce shipping of smaller consumer goods. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shipping cosmetics, accessories, samples, and selected low-fragility beauty products. Weight/material reduction relevance: provides actual mailer weights and positions the format as a box replacement that saves freight and labor. Premium versus mass-market fit: useful for both, especially direct-to-consumer channels. Compatibility/limitation notes: not appropriate for products too heavy or fragile for mailers; glass-heavy prestige sets may still need corrugated shipper plus inserts. Use in screening: evaluate paper cushioned mailers as a tertiary lightweighting route for smaller or less-breakable beauty items.
Source name: EverTec Cushioned Mailer | Durable & Recyclable Protection — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/us-solutions/by-product/shipping-mailers/cushioned-evertec/

Summary of what was found: Ball’s Alumi-Tek beverage bottle is described as a lightweight, recyclable aluminum package for a wide variety of beverages, supporting aluminum as a premium/differentiation-led transfer route.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight aluminum bottle. Food/beverage use case: reclosable aluminum beverage bottle. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: premium shampoo, body care, deodorant, haircare, fragrance-adjacent, or refillable liquid formats where metal look-and-feel adds value. Weight/material reduction relevance: source calls the package lightweight and aluminum offers a non-glass premium route. Premium versus mass-market fit: better for premium/luxe or hero SKUs than broad mass-market due to converting, lining, and decoration complexity. Compatibility/limitation notes: transfer by inference from beverage use; does not prove all cosmetic formulas are compatible, so lacquer/lining, denting, closure fit, and sourcing economics need separate screening. Use in screening: keep aluminum as a premium-track route where brands want to replace heavier glass while preserving prestige cues.
Source name: Ball to Manufacture Reclosable Alumi-Tek Aluminum Beverage Bottles — Ball — https://www.ball.com/newswire/article/123701/ball-to-manufacture-reclosable-alumi-tek-aluminum-beverage-bottles

Summary of what was found: PETCORE Europe says perforated sleeves on household and personal care PET bottles are only compatible with recycling if the bottle remains recognizable as PET and the sleeve/ink system separates correctly without ink bleed.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: sleeve/label design constraints on PET bottle recyclability. Food/beverage use case: PET-bottle recycling logic transferable from beverage-style PET systems. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: lightweight PET/rPET cosmetic bottles with body sleeves or high-decoration label systems. Weight/material reduction relevance: lightweighting gains can be undermined if label/sleeve architecture blocks sorting and recycling, so the full material system matters. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to both, especially decorated premium packs relying on full-body sleeves. Compatibility/limitation notes: recyclability rather than grams-saved source; value is as a screening constraint. Use in screening: treat sleeve material, inks, and wash-off/separation performance as part of the transferability checklist for PET/rPET routes.
Source name: Recyclability of Household and Personal Care PET bottles - Perforated Sleeves Approved — PETCORE Europe — https://www.petcore-europe.org/news-events/218-recyclability-of-household-and-personal-care-pet-bottles-perforated-sleeves-approved.html

Summary of what was found: West Pharmaceutical explains why cyclic olefin polymer can replace glass in high-sensitivity drug packaging: lower interaction risk, lower delamination risk, fewer leachables, better fracture resistance, and better container-closure integrity at cryogenic temperatures.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: COP polymer vials/syringes as a glass alternative. Pharma/healthcare use case: parenteral drug products, especially sensitive biologics and cryogenic applications. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: premium dermocosmetics, actives-heavy skincare, single-use boosters, or sensitive formulations where low interaction and break resistance matter more than lowest cost. Weight/material reduction relevance: moving from glass to COP can materially cut pack weight and breakage exposure while preserving transparency. Premium versus mass-market fit: more plausible for premium/luxe, clinical skincare, or sensitive-formula categories than mass-market due to likely higher material/system cost. Compatibility/regulatory/cost limitation notes: the source emphasizes compatibility, oxygen/water resistance, reduced extractables/leachables risk, and container-closure integrity; formulation testing is still required. Use in screening: treat COP as a high-performance lightweight substitute for glass where fragility, interaction, and purity are critical; do not assume it is economical for mainstream beauty.
Source name: Daikyo Crystal Zenith Cyclic Olefin Polymer for Drug Product Packaging — West Pharmaceutical Services — https://www.westpharma.com/blog/2020/may/the-right-polymer-for-drug-product-packaging

Summary of what was found: SCHOTT Pharma states its TOPPAC prefillable polymer syringes are made from COC and positioned as break-resistant, lightweight packaging for long-term storage, biologics, aesthetic treatments, and large-volume applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: COC prefillable syringe platform. Pharma/healthcare use case: long-term drug storage and administration, especially biologics. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: premium injectable-adjacent aesthetics, clinic-dispensed dermocosmetics, concentrated serums, or expensive treatment products where visual clarity and low breakage are valued. Weight/material reduction relevance: explicit lightweight design versus glass. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for luxe, med-spa, aesthetic, and dermocosmetic formats; weak fit for mainstream cosmetics because pharma-grade syringe platforms are likely over-specified. Compatibility/regulatory/cost limitation notes: supports COC as a high-performance transfer route only where long-term storage, purity, or handling safety justify cost and complexity. Use in screening: place COC/COP in a premium/sensitive-formula bucket rather than a broad mass-beauty bucket.
Source name: SCHOTT TOPPAC polymer syringes — SCHOTT Pharma — https://www.schott-pharma.com/en/products/syringes/schott-toppac

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer’s ClearJect COP syringe source adds transfer-relevant details: COP is transparent like glass, break-resistant, low in extractables/leachables, low in oxygen permeation versus other plastics, does not release metal ions, and avoids tungsten/adhesive issues in the cited design.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: COP syringe system. Pharma/healthcare use case: demanding medications including oncology and biotech products. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: high-value serums, precision-dose treatments, physician-dispensed skin products, and prestige packs needing a clinical precision format without glass fragility. Weight/material reduction relevance: COP offers a lighter non-glass route while retaining optical clarity. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe or professional only; likely too expensive and specialized for everyday mass cosmetics. Compatibility/regulatory/cost limitation notes: stresses pH tolerance, low interaction, low extractables/leachables, and no metal-ion release, supporting sensitive-formula transfer but narrowing use cases. Use in screening: use COP where formulation sensitivity or breakage risk outweighs cost; filter it out for commodity skincare, haircare, or bodycare.
Source name: Gerresheimer presents new COP syringes produced in Europe: Gx RTF ClearJect — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/en/customer/company/news/detail/gerresheimer-presents-new-cop-syringes-produced-in-europe-gx-rtf-clearject-1

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer’s plastics catalog shows a more commercially realistic route: pharma plastic packaging already uses PET bottles, COP vials, eye droppers, nasal sprays, nebulizers, and accessories; its liquid-packaging plastics range also includes PET, rPET, and bio-PET and serves both pharma and beauty.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: PET bottles and related pharma plastic-packaging systems; broader plastics range including PET, rPET, and bio-PET. Pharma/healthcare use case: liquid dosage, ophthalmic and nasal products, and plastic vials. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: mainstream bottles for skincare, oral beauty supplements, mists, sprays, and personal care liquids; some prestige clear-bottle applications with pharmaceutically informed quality logic. Weight/material reduction relevance: PET/rPET is one of the most realistic lightweight alternatives to glass for both mass and premium packs. Premium versus mass-market fit: very strong for mass-market and workable for premium where clear appearance is acceptable. Compatibility/regulatory/cost limitation notes: PET is broadly transferable, while COP is reserved for more demanding contents. Use in screening: rank PET/rPET as a top-transfer healthcare material; use COP only where formula risk demands more than PET can provide.
Source name: Pharmaceutical Primary Packaging: Plastic Packaging Solutions for Liquid Formulations / Ophthalmic, Nasal and Parenteral Applications — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/fileadmin/user_upload/user_upload/primary-packaging/download/bottles-containers/Gerresheimer_Plastic_Packaging_PET_Solid_and_Liquid_EN.pdf ; https://www.gerresheimer.com/fileadmin/user_upload/user_upload/primary-packaging/download/bottles-containers/Gerresheimer_Plastic_Packaging_Ophthalmic_Nasal_and_Parenteral_EN.pdf
