Summary of what was found: Gneuss says physically foamed PET sheet can be made from virgin PET and/or recycled PET, including 100% post-consumer material, with over 50% weight reduction depending on process while maintaining consistent foam structure and mechanical properties; target uses include thermoformed trays, cups, and pots.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed PET sheet extrusion and thermoforming. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic trays, inserts, thermoformed secondary components, sample pods, and protective secondary parts rather than clear premium bottles. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 50% weight reduction in sheet compared with rigid solid PET sheet. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: attractive for lightweight tray/insert routes where rPET content and reduced sheet mass are valuable. Premium versus mass-market fit: stronger for mass-market secondary packaging than luxe primary packs because foam sheet is not a transparent glass-like aesthetic route. Limitations/caution: opacity and thermoformed-sheet format limit use for visible premium jars/bottles.
Source name: From Rigid to Foam Sheet on the Fly: Fast and Flexible Production Changes with Gneuss Sheet Lines — Gneuss Kunststofftechnik GmbH — https://www.gneuss.com/en/from-rigid-to-foam-sheet-on-the-fly-fast-and-flexible-production-changes-with-gneuss-sheet-lines/

Summary of what was found: SML says premium foamed PP sheet can reach a density of 0.55 g/cm³ in an A/B/A structure, corresponding to 40% material reduction versus regular PP sheet; it is fully recyclable mono-material, fat/oil resistant, and suitable for thermoforming.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: physically foamed PP sheet for thermoforming. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic trays, refill trays, secondary inserts, opaque tub/case components, and some tertiary protective parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: 40% reduction versus regular PP sheet, with a foamed B-layer made using inert gas and nucleating agent. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: good candidate for non-product-contact thermoformed components where the company wants lighter plastic while staying in PP. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market or hidden components; premium visible use requires aesthetic validation. Limitations/caution: food-packaging oriented, so formula-contact and beauty-decoration performance need verification.
Source name: High-performance PP sheet lines now equipped to manufacture premium foamed sheet — SML Maschinengesellschaft mbH — https://www.sml.at/news/high-performance-pp-sheet-lines-now-equipped-manufacture-premium-foamed-sheet

Summary of what was found: Bockatech says EcoCore’s skin-foam-skin PP packaging can reduce plastic density by about 70%, create recyclable mono-material PP packs, and deliver lighter, stronger, insulated packaging; the same solution can be used for caps/closures and not just cups.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: skin-foam-skin injected PP packaging using EcoCore. Packaging/component relevance: thicker rigid beauty parts such as refillable outer shells, robust overcaps, aerosol caps, and selected secondary parts because the source extends the concept to caps/closures and healthcare. Weight/material reduction evidence: around 70% density reduction in foam walls, lighter-than-some-paper-cup performance, and short cycle time. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and durable/reusable packs; also relevant to premium refill systems where tactile stiffness is needed. Compatibility/limitation notes: cited product is food-safe, but cosmetic direct-contact compatibility requires separate validation. Use in screening: keep skin-foam-skin PP in the longlist for overcaps, refill housings, and durable cases.
Source name: EcoCore food pots — Bockatech — https://www.bockatech.com/ecocore-food-pots

Summary of what was found: Arburg reports Bockatech EcoCore cups made with PP and combined chemical/physical foaming are up to 50% lighter than non-foamed alternatives and can be produced in 5.5 seconds cycle time.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: combined physical and chemical foaming in PP with skin-foam-skin structure. Packaging/component relevance: high-speed evidence for commercially realistic production of lightweight rigid packaging, relevant to overcaps, refill cups/pods, cases, and other thick-walled beauty parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 50% lighter than non-foamed alternatives; high-speed cycle supports scalability. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and durable/reusable packaging, but could extend to premium outer shells if surface and decoration targets are feasible. Limitations/caution: case is a cup, not a cosmetic pack; meaningful because it proves foamed rigid packaging can run fast and stably at industrial scale.
Source name: Gamechanger Bockatech — ARBURG — https://www.arburg.com/en/gb/smart-production/success-stories/detail/gamechanger-bockatech/

Summary of what was found: Milacron describes structural foam as producing parts with a solid skin and cellular foamed core, generating a high strength-to-weight ratio and enabling thicker wall sections at much lower pressure than conventional molding.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: structural foam injection molding for thick rigid plastic parts. Packaging/component relevance: cosmetic secondary/tertiary parts and thick non-precision components such as merchandising trays, shipping inserts, refill caddies, rigid transport aids, or large outer shells. Weight/material reduction relevance: source frames the technology as a high-strength-to-weight solution for thicker parts but does not give a percentage on the webpage. Premium versus mass-market fit: more relevant to hidden or large-format components than small glossy closures or tight-tolerance dispensing parts. Limitations/caution: less attractive for precision threads, glossy overcaps, and small Class-A beauty components because optimized for thicker, larger parts.
Source name: L-Series: Low-Pressure Injection Molding Machines — Milacron — https://www.milacron.com/product/l-series/

Summary of what was found: World Aerosols reports Bockatech’s EcoCore technology can cut plastic use in overclosures by up to 44% while improving performance and reducing cost; the overclosures are made entirely from PP and can use recycled PP.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed PP overclosures/overcaps. Packaging/component relevance: direct beauty-adjacent source because aerosols and personal-care overclosures are close analogs to cosmetic overcaps. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 44% plastic reduction in overclosures. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong shortlist candidate for mass-market deodorant/body-care/aerosol caps and possibly fragrance or haircare overcaps; premium use conditional because flawless gloss, metallization, or luxury tactility may be challenging. Use in screening: keep foamed PP overcaps in the beauty shortlist rather than limiting foaming to trays and hidden parts. Limitations/caution: article is partially gated and summarizes company claims; useful as sector-specific directional evidence.
Source name: Bockatech’s EcoCore technology reshapes sustainable aerosol packaging — World Aerosols — https://worldaerosols.com/news/bockatechs-ecocore-technology-reshapes-sustainable-aerosol-packaging/

Summary of what was found: Amcor positions AmPrima for home and personal care as a recycle-ready mono-material flexible-packaging platform that can run on commercial converting and filling equipment at the same speeds as non-recyclable materials and can deliver up to 90% packaging-weight reduction versus rigid refill formats.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material recycle-ready flexible pouches for home and personal care, aimed at refill use cases. Packaging format relevance: refill pouches for rigid bottles/containers. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 90% packaging weight reduction. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to shampoo, body wash, hand wash, and similar liquid personal-care categories where the rigid hero pack can remain and the refill does the lightweighting. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market/high-volume refill systems; technically possible as a consumer-facing refill but not inherently suited to replacing a premium hero pack on shelf. Performance/recyclability/filling-line limitation notes: Amcor ties the route to commercial-speed converting/filling and recycle-ready design rather than aesthetic luxury cues. Use in screening: treat mono-material refill pouches as a core shortlist for mass personal care when the product can be decanted into a reusable rigid bottle and local flexible-pack recycling pathways exist.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/emea/homeandpersonalcare

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s mono-material stand-up pouch for liquids is a PE-based duplex structure offered for liquid soap and detergent refills; the HFFS version can include aroma barrier and weight-loss avoidance, has a wide sealing window, and can be specified with glossy or matte finishing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: PE-based mono-material refill pouch. Packaging format relevance: stand-up refill pouch, pre-made or HFFS, sized from 0.5 L to 3 L. Weight/material reduction relevance: no numeric weight-saving figure, but supports mono-PE as a lighter refill route versus traditional rigid refill bottles by showing a purpose-built flexible replacement for liquid formats. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant to shampoo, conditioner, body wash, hand wash, and similar liquid beauty/personal-care refills. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily mass-market; glossy/matte finish option shows flexible mono-material refill packs can support elevated shelf appearance where needed. Performance/barrier/filling limitation notes: aroma barrier, weight-loss avoidance, and a wide sealing window indicate mono-material PE can be engineered for fragranced liquid products and production robustness. Use in screening: classify PE mono-material refill pouches as a high-priority route for liquid personal care, especially where fragrance retention and filling-line seal robustness are critical.
Source name: Mono-material StandUpPouch — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/flexible-packaging/mono-material-standuppouch/

Summary of what was found: Mondi and Henkel launched a recyclable mono-PE refill pouch for Pril that reduced plastic use by 70%, worked with a reusable rPET pump bottle, and was described as leak-proof and durable enough for home deliveries.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE refill pouch used in a reuse-plus-refill system. Packaging format relevance: refill pouch feeding a durable rigid dispenser bottle. Weight/material reduction evidence: 70% plastic reduction versus the prior format/system as described by the partners. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: dishwashing liquid example is transferable to liquid personal care because dispensing logic, refill behavior, leak risk, and home-delivery stress are similar to shampoo, body wash, and hand soap refills. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market and mainstream refill systems; less relevant as a standalone premium primary package. Performance/barrier/e-commerce limitation notes: the pouch is described as leak-proof and highly durable for home deliveries, valuable for e-commerce and transport screening. Use in screening: justify mono-PE refill pouches as realistic and scaled when paired with a reusable rigid dispenser and validated for transport integrity.
Source name: Mondi & Henkel partner to launch fully recyclable mono-material refill pouch for Pril — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2022/mondi--henkel-partner-to-launch-fully-recyclable-mono-material-refill-pouch-for-pril/

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s Berlingot refill format is a mono-material PE solution that reduces plastic usage by more than 75% compared with traditional rigid plastic bottles.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: ultra-light mono-PE refill pack. Packaging format relevance: compact refill/reserve pack style flexible format. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 75% plastic reduction versus rigid bottles. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: relevant to concentrated liquid top-ups, reserve refills, travel refills, or applications where extreme material reduction matters more than shelf rigidity. Premium versus mass-market fit: much stronger for mass-market and cost-sensitive refill systems than premium/luxe hero packs. Performance/consumer-handling limitation notes: strong on material reduction, but does not claim suitability for highly sensitive formulas or prestige presentation. Use in screening: treat very-light refill sachet/pouch formats as a high-impact route for products that do not require high-rigidity packaging at point of use.
Source name: Sustainable Berlingot packaging — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/products-and-solutions/flexible-packaging/sustainable-berlingot-packaging/

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal states that Elvive refillable pouches reduce plastic use by 60% across several haircare lines, while the same page shows luxury refills are being scaled in fragrance and skincare rather than only mass-market haircare.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: refill systems in beauty, including flexible pouches in consumer haircare and refill systems in luxury fragrance/skincare. Packaging format relevance: refill pouches for haircare and refill solutions across fragrance, skincare, hygiene, and makeup. Weight/material reduction evidence: Elvive refillable pouches reduce plastic by 60%; Prada Paradoxe refilling saves 44% glass, 67% plastic, 100% metals, and 61% cardboard versus repurchase of the full pack. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct beauty-sector evidence from a major global player. Premium versus mass-market fit: supports two-track logic: flexible refill pouches are strong in mass-market, while premium/luxe can retain the premium outer shell and move material reduction into the refill system. Performance/brand limitation notes: source frames refills as compatible with desirability and performance, but does not imply that a standalone flexible pouch replaces the premium shelf pack. Use in screening: support separate mass-market and premium refill tracks, strongest where the consumer keeps a durable outer pack.
Source name: Pioneering the refill revolution — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/articles/commitments/pioneering-the-refill-revolution/

Summary of what was found: Kenvue reports that Le Petit Marseillais shower-product doypack refills cut plastic by at least 50%, removed more than 80 metric tons of virgin plastic annually, and sold more than 1.5 million units in Europe since 2025.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: flexible doypack refill pouches for personal care. Packaging format relevance: shower-gel refill pouches, later expanded into larger hand-wash refill formats. Weight/material reduction evidence: at least 50% less plastic than a standard bottle; more than 80 metric tons of virgin plastic removed annually. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct current evidence from a personal-care brand showing commercial-scale adoption rather than a pilot concept. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong mass-market route, particularly for rinse-off liquid categories. Performance/consumer limitation notes: strong on scale and material reduction but does not provide high-barrier or premium-perception claims; use as evidence for operationally practical mainstream refills. Use in screening: place doypack refill pouches in the mass-market shortlist for shower, hair, and hand-care categories where consumer reuse of the original bottle is feasible.
Source name: How Le Petit Marseillais reduced plastic packaging by 50% with doypack refills — Kenvue — https://www.kenvue.com/our-commitments/how-le-petit-marseillais-reduced-plastic-packaging-by-50-with-doypack-refills

Summary of what was found: Glenroy says personal-care flexible packaging must be selected around formulation aggressiveness and fragrance retention: active ingredients, acids, and other compounds can require chemically resistant, high-barrier structures, and product, shelf-life, distribution, and equipment conditions all influence film choice.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: flexible films and laminations for personal care/cosmetics. Packaging format relevance: pouches, flow wraps, sachets, and other flexible formats for personal-care products. Weight/material reduction relevance: no weight number; explains why not every light flexible structure is acceptable for cosmetics because barrier and chemical resistance can dominate film selection. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to shampoos, sunscreen, blemish treatments, anti-aging creams, toothpaste, and flavored gels. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both; premium/luxe may need stronger sensory, fragrance, and appearance retention, while mass-market needs scalable robust films. Performance/barrier/compatibility limitation notes: flags aggressive formulations and fragrance/flavor retention as reasons common films may fail; also points to product/package compatibility testing and barrier optimization. Use in screening: filter out lightweight pouch routes that cannot handle actives, acids, fragrance retention, or required shelf life.
Source name: Personal Care Flexible Packaging — Glenroy — https://www.glenroy.com/markets/personal-care-packaging/

Summary of what was found: Glenroy’s pouch and sachet page shows flexible sachets and related laminations are used for shampoo, conditioner, sunscreen, lotion, serums, and cosmetic samples, and successful structures depend on barrier, puncture resistance, seal compatibility, and compatibility testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: pouch, sachet, and related film laminations, including recyclable and PCR options. Packaging format relevance: sachets, pouches, sample packs, and stick-pack-adjacent formats for personal care and cosmetics. Weight/material reduction evidence: sachets use less packaging per unit than rigid containers and can lower material cost while improving portability and sampling efficiency. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant for samples, travel sizes, low-price sachets, and small dose packs. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market, travel, and sample distribution; weak as a premium hero-pack replacement. Performance/barrier/filling-line limitation notes: oxygen/moisture/light barriers, chemical resistance, puncture and tear resistance, heat-seal compatibility, and formulation compatibility testing are highlighted. Use in screening: shortlist sachets and small pouches for samples/trials/low-cost dose delivery, but treat cautiously where high-barrier multilayers or aggressive formulations are required.
Source name: Pouch & Sachet Film Laminations — Glenroy — https://www.glenroy.com/flexible-packaging/flexible-packaging-films/pouch-film-laminations/

Summary of what was found: APR’s PE flexible-packaging guide sets practical design-for-recycling rules: EVOH can be used in PE structures within defined limits, PE closures are preferred, rigid or non-PE attachments need testing, metal can make the pack non-recyclable, and inks/adhesives must be minimized or tested.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE and PE-rich flexible packaging design rules for recycling. Packaging format relevance: bags, pouches, sachets, wraps, and flexible PE packaging with closures, spouts, labels, inks, and adhesives. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a weight-saving source; shows constraints required so lightweight PE films remain viable in future recycling systems. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to mono-PE refill pouches and reserve packs for liquid personal care and cosmetics. Premium versus mass-market fit: highly relevant to mass-market scaling because decoration-heavy packs, rigid attachments, and metalized elements can undermine recyclability; also relevant to premium packs that often over-decorate. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: APR prefers EVOH within defined limits and PE closures; rigid/non-PE attachments need testing; metal is non-recyclable in this stream; inks/adhesives can cause gels and specks. Use in screening: apply as a hard filter for mono-PE pouch design, especially for barrier content, fitments, inks, adhesives, and decorative elements.
Source name: PE Flexible — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/apr-design-hub/apr-design-guide/pe-flexible/

Summary of what was found: APR’s PP flexible guidance shows mono-PP routes are possible but sensitive to barrier levels and decoration: EVOH within defined limits is acceptable, while metalized layers, paper labels, nitrocellulose/untested inks, heavy adhesives, and certain coatings can impair recyclability or require testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PP and PP-rich flexible packaging recycling design criteria. Packaging format relevance: PP flexible films and pouches, including labels, inks, adhesives, closures, and metalized decoration. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a quantitative weight source; explains what a commercially realistic recyclable mono-PP route can and cannot include. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: important for wet wipes, some flow-wraps, and certain pouch formats where PP is the chosen single-polymer route. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market wet-wipe and hygiene-style formats; caution for premium packs relying on metalized decoration, paper labels, or heavy wet-chemistry coatings. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: EVOH ≤6 wt% with PP-g-MAH tie layers is acceptable; metalized content and paper labels can be detrimental; printing, adhesives, and curable coatings can create gels/specks and degrade recyclate quality. Use in screening: apply as the main design-for-recycling screen for mono-PP beauty/wipe packs, especially when PCR or PPWR-readiness is considered.
Source name: PP Flexible — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/apr-design-hub/apr-design-guide/pp-flexible-guidance/

Summary of what was found: RecyClass reports that low levels of PP contamination in PE film can already hurt recycled-film quality: at 2.5% PP concentration in the test sample, tear resistance and elongation dropped while gels/specks increased; EVOH above 5% of PE-film weight also harms PE-film recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recyclability interaction of PE films with PP, PA, and EVOH. Packaging format relevance: PE-targeted mono-material pouches that nevertheless contain PP layers/components or barrier layers. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a lightweighting claim; recyclability risk source because many light flexible packs are near-mono rather than truly mono-material. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant to refill pouches with PP fitments, PP labels, or excessive barrier content. Premium versus mass-market fit: crucial for both; especially mass-market where recyclability claims must survive at scale and premium-decorated packs may add incompatible materials. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: quantified recyclate-quality deterioration from PP contamination and confirms EVOH above 5% harms PE recyclability. Use in screening: keep PP content and EVOH content tightly controlled in PE-targeted refill routes or the lightweight pouch may become a poor recycling design.
Source name: Recyclability of Flexible Polyethylene with PP & PA: Novel Findings — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/news/recyclability-of-flexible-polyethylene-with-pp--pa-novel-findings/

Summary of what was found: CEFLEX’s barrier-packaging guidance says pouches with fitments or spouts require case-specific testing to prove they can be sorted into the flexible-packaging stream, and structures outside the recommended polyolefin combinations need further testing to qualify for recycling.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: design-for-recyclability rules for plastic-based flexible barrier packaging. Packaging format relevance: barrier pouches, including spouted or fitment-bearing structures. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a weight claim; system-level recyclability constraint for lightweight barrier packs. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant because many liquid beauty/personal-care refill pouches need spouts, zippers, sliders, or higher barriers. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both; especially important in premium packs where extra components and decorative layers are more common. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: spouts/fitments need specific testing; non-PE/non-PP combinations and out-of-window constructions need additional evidence; guidance is cautious rather than permissive. Use in screening: treat recyclability claims for spouted beauty refills as conditional until the whole structure, not just the film, is validated.
Source name: Pioneer Project Barrier Summary Table — CEFLEX — https://ceflex.eu/public_downloads/Worksheet_Project_Barrier_final_Nov_2020_a.pdf

Summary of what was found: Menshen explains that mono-material spouted pouches are hard to produce on legacy HFFS equipment because conventional spouts and machines were built for mixed-material laminates; its LoTUS spout was developed to improve sealing, avoid low-melting additives, avoid speed loss, and even raised line speed by 5 pouches per minute.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material spout/fitment engineering for spouted pouches. Packaging format relevance: spouted pouches on HFFS equipment. Weight/material reduction relevance: indirect; lightweight mono-material pouches can fail operationally if the fitment system is not redesigned. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: very relevant to shampoo, liquid soap, lotion, and refill pouches that depend on spouts for clean pouring and leakage control. Premium versus mass-market fit: more important for mass-market scaling because line speed and machine conversion cost matter heavily; premium use possible at lower volumes. Performance/filling-line limitation notes: sealing quality, machine speed, and spout/laminate heat behavior are core constraints. Use in screening: classify spouted mono-material pouches as a separate, higher-validation-risk route than simple non-spouted reserve pouches.
Source name: Game Changer in Production of Sustainable Mono-material Spouted Pouches — MENSHEN — https://menshen.com/innovation-en/game-changer-in-production-of-sustainable-mono-material-spouted-pouches/

Summary of what was found: Gualapack lists cosmetics and beauty among key spouted-pouch markets and notes that pouches may use PE- or PP-based, alu-free, alu-based, mono-material, mixed-polyolefin, or bio-based structures depending on application; recyclability depends on material combination and local recycling streams, and barrier performance declines once opened.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: pre-made and spouted pouches across multiple material systems. Packaging format relevance: pre-made pouches, reclosable spouted pouches, no-spill formats, and low-viscosity liquid applications. Weight/material reduction evidence: spouted pouches typically require less material than rigid containers and reduce transport volume; pre-made spouted pouches significantly reduce material usage versus some form-fill-seal alternatives. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct because cosmetics and beauty are listed among served markets. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for functional refill and portable liquid formats; less suitable as full premium hero-pack substitute without a durable outer system. Performance/barrier/handling limitation notes: spout diameter must match viscosity; hot-fill suitability is material-dependent; once opened, original oxygen/moisture barrier is no longer fully intact; recyclability is conditional on structure and geography. Use in screening: frame spouted pouches as versatile but not universal, especially for sensitive formulas or markets without flexible-pack recycling.
Source name: Pre-made Pouches — Gualapack — https://gualapack.com/pre-made-pouches

Summary of what was found: ExxonMobil highlights two relevant points for lightweight beauty flexibles: a mono-material HFFS pouch case with 60% material reduction versus rigid thermoformed trays, and a non-laminated MDO PE pouch route with enhanced mechanical properties and significant downgauging potential.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: MDO PE and mono-material downgauged flexible films/pouches. Packaging format relevance: HFFS pouches and mono-material PE laminates or non-laminated structures. Weight/material reduction evidence: 60% material reduction for one mono-material pouch case; significant downgauging potential from MDO PE. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: useful for beauty/personal-care refill pouches, reserve packs, and certain sachet formats where better stiffness, toughness, and optics are needed from a PE route. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market because downgauging and operational efficiency are the stated value proposition; can support more premium-looking flexibles if optics and printability are sufficient. Performance/barrier/filling-line limitation notes: focuses on mechanical-property improvement and process efficiency rather than formula barrier needs, so barrier-sensitive beauty products still require validation. Use in screening: shortlist MDO-PE and similar film-engineering routes as enablers of thinner mono-material films where barrier requirements stay manageable.
Source name: Interpack 2026 — ExxonMobil Product Solutions — https://www.exxonmobilchemical.com/en/products/signature-polymers/events/interpack-2026

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s re/loop FlowWrap for wet wipes is a mono-PP, form-fill-seal structure for baby care, cosmetics, and household wipes that contains 35% PCR, is compatible with PP mechanical recycling per CEFLEX guidelines, and showed sealing, barrier, machinability, and line-speed performance comparable to virgin alternatives.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PP recyclable flexible structure with PCR content. Packaging format relevance: flow-wrap and sachet-like wet-wipe packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: lightweight flexible replacement for harder-to-recycle laminate structures rather than a rigid-vs-flex comparison; key evidence is that mono-PP route can retain performance while being designed for recycling and incorporating PCR. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to cosmetic wet wipes, beauty wipes, and adjacent hygiene formats. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and high-volume wipe categories; less directly relevant to prestige skincare or fragrance. Performance/PCR/filling-line limitation notes: internal tests and customer trials showed comparable performance to virgin materials; compatible with existing lines and strong runnability at very high line speeds. Use in screening: put mono-PP/PCR wipe and wrap structures on the shortlist for wipe-format beauty and personal-care categories.
Source name: Mondi introduces re/loop FlowWrap with 35% post-consumer recycled content for wet wipes packaging — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2026/mondi-introduces-reloop-flowwrap-with-35-percent-post-consumer-recycled-content-for-wet-wipes-packaging/

Summary of what was found: BOBST’s oneBARRIER PrimeCycle is a transparent AlOx-based full-PE mono-material substrate with a recyclability certification level of 98%; BOBST says it was developed to deliver barrier performance equivalent to non-recyclable mixed-material packaging, especially metallized polyester structures.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: high-barrier recyclable mono-PE film using AlOx barrier technology. Packaging format relevance: high-barrier pouches and flexible structures where conventional mixed-material laminates would otherwise be used. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a direct rigid-vs-flex comparison, but relevant because barrier-rich pouches need not default to heavier or non-recyclable mixed-material structures. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: especially relevant for fragranced, oxygen-sensitive, or moisture/light-sensitive beauty refills that exceed basic mono-PE capability. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe or sensitive-formula conditional shortlist first; likely cost/complexity above entry-level mass-market films. Performance/barrier/recyclability limitation notes: positioned as alternative to metallized polyester-type structures, indicating strong barrier capability with mono-PE outcome. Use in screening: include advanced coated mono-PE barrier solutions in the premium/sensitive-formula shortlist, but screen after basic mono-PE/mono-PP routes on cost and availability.
Source name: BOBST oneBARRIER PrimeCycle — BOBST — https://www.bobst.com/in/en/sustainability/bobst-onebarrier-primecycle

Summary of what was found: BOBST’s FibreCycle paper-based barrier solution shows why paper-flex routes are conditional rather than default: paper is hygroscopic, rough, brittle, and inherently poor in barrier, so the structure needs primer coating, metallization, and a heat-sealable topcoat to achieve high barrier.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: barrier-coated/metallized paper-fibre flexible packaging. Packaging format relevance: paper-based high-barrier flexible packs intended as alternatives to plastic-based flexible packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: useful as lightweight and high-paper-share route in some cases, but most valuable because it explains the technical burden required to make fibre-based flex perform. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: potentially relevant to dry or lower-demand refill applications; much less suitable as default route for wet, oily, alcohol-rich, or highly fragranced formulas. Premium versus mass-market fit: visually/premium interesting, but technically conditional and likely better for niche dry products or secondary refill systems than mainstream liquid cosmetics. Performance/barrier limitation notes: paper’s moisture sensitivity, roughness, brittleness, and poor native barrier must be engineered around. Use in screening: treat barrier-coated paper/fibre flex as conditional or early-caution route, behind mono-PE/PP in the liquid beauty and personal-care lightweighting shortlist.
Source name: BOBST oneBARRIER FibreCycle — BOBST — https://www.bobst.com/cn/en/sustainability/bobst-onebarrier-fibrecycle

Summary of what was found: ETMA’s tube overview provides the baseline of current European tube formats: aluminum, plastic, and laminate tubes, and explains where each material system is typically used. It also lists key European tube test standards for airtightness, weld strength, print adhesion, flip-top orientation, and dimensional tolerances.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: baseline tube formats and tube performance standards. Tube format relevance: aluminum tubes, plastic tubes, ABL laminate tubes, PBL/CBL laminate tubes. Weight/material reduction relevance: establishes the current tube material families that lightweighting options must compare against. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: plastic tubes are identified as highly important for cosmetics, while laminate tubes are used where barrier and print quality matter. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both. Barrier, compatibility, decoration, recyclability, filling-line, or consumer-experience limitation notes: lists relevant EN tests including airtightness of closures, head-weld strength, side-seam strength, print adhesion, flip-top orientation, and dimensional tolerances. Use in screening: use as the taxonomy and performance-test anchor for tube routes. Limitations/caution: European tube-market framing, not a global share estimate.
Source name: Always the optimal material for a good idea — ETMA — https://www.etma-online.org/tubetypes.html

Summary of what was found: ETMA’s recyclability position paper argues plastic tubes are lightweight and have a favorable packaging-weight-to-fill-volume ratio versus bottles, while recyclability depends on consistent design-for-recycling and post-consumer sorting/recycling.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: plastic tube lightweight/recyclability framing. Tube format relevance: extruded and laminate plastic tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: plastic tubes are described as resource-efficient and lightweight with better packaging-weight-to-filling-volume ratio than bottles. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: supports tubes as an inherently attractive lightweight format for beauty and personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, with mass-market strongest by scale. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: recyclability depends on design-for-recycling plus efficient collection, sorting, and recycling systems. Use in screening: use to justify tubes as weight-efficient but not automatically recycling-ready. Limitations/caution: position paper from a tube association, so use with design-for-recycling guidance.
Source name: Position Paper on the Recyclability of Plastic Tubes — ETMA — https://linhardt.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Etma_position_paper_recyclability_of_plastic_tubes_revision_2024_final.pdf

Summary of what was found: APR’s Design Guide describes common HDPE and PP tube construction, typical squeeze-tube wall thicknesses, and frequent use of aluminum foil or EVOH barrier layers, pigments, fillers, printing, and inserts.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: HDPE and PP tube construction and design-for-recycling baseline. Tube format relevance: extruded tubes and laminated sheet/film tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: typical squeeze-tube wall thickness ranges from 160 to 600 microns, useful for benchmarking downgauging. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: directly relevant to cosmetic and personal-care tubes using HDPE/PP and barrier layers. Premium versus mass-market fit: both. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: barrier layers may use aluminum foil or EVOH; pigments, fillers, labels, printing, and inserts contribute to material system complexity. Use in screening: benchmark tube wall thickness and recognize sleeves, barriers, inserts, shoulders, and closures as mass contributors. Limitations/caution: design-for-recycling source, not a quantified lightweighting case.
Source name: The APR Design Guide for Plastics Recyclability — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/documents/res-o-01/

Summary of what was found: APR’s tube design resource makes closure choice a major recyclability issue: caps can be a significant share of the package, PP is a contaminant in the HDPE stream, and tube size/sortability thresholds matter.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: tube closure and sortability design-for-recycling guidance. Tube format relevance: HDPE and PP squeeze tubes with caps/closures. Weight/material reduction evidence: caps/closures can be a significant part of the package, so lightweighting and material matching should include closure systems. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant because beauty tubes often use PP flip-top or screw caps on PE/HDPE tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: both; high-volume personal care especially. Recyclability limitation notes: PP is a contaminant in the HDPE stream, preferred designs keep closure value in the recycling stream, and tube size/sortation matters. Use in screening: do not evaluate sleeves alone; cap material and sorting performance can make or break a recyclable lightweight tube route. Limitations/caution: recyclability guidance rather than tube weight case study.
Source name: Plastic Squeeze Tubes Design Resource Document HDPE and PP Tubes — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://www.plasticsmarkets.org/jsfcontent/APRTubesDesign_jsf_1.pdf

Summary of what was found: RecyClass reports that Albéa’s HDPE tube with HDPE flip-top cap was approved, marking a notable milestone because the low-melt-flow HDPE flip-top cap technology tested successfully.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: mono-material HDPE tube-plus-cap system. Tube format relevance: HDPE tubes with HDPE flip-top caps. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a direct weight-saving case, but supports mono-material redesign without losing closure functionality. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for recyclable beauty and personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if appearance/feel passes. Recyclability limitation notes: RecyClass approval gives European direction for improving recyclability of rigid HDPE packaging. Use in screening: move mono-HDPE tube/closure combinations into detailed evaluation and avoid default PP caps where HDPE recycling compatibility is the objective. Limitations/caution: approval is for a tested technology and should not be generalized to all HDPE caps without validation.
Source name: Albéa’s HDPE tube with HDPE flip-top cap approved by RecyClass — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/news/albeas-hdpe-tube-with-hdpe-flip-top-cap-approved-by-recyclass/

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s Lightweight Tube quantifies a downgauging route: tube body wall thickness reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm, cutting virgin material by up to 30% while maintaining a similar haptic experience.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: downgauged PE/coex tube sleeves. Tube format relevance: Mono and Coex tubes for skincare, hair care, make-up, toothpaste, and dentifrice. Weight/material reduction evidence: wall thickness reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm; up to 30% virgin material reduction; for one million D50/210 mm sleeve-only tubes, up to 4.6 tons HDPE eliminated and about 1.5 tons CO2 reduced. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: direct cosmetic and personal-care tube lightweighting evidence. Premium versus mass-market fit: core mass-market route; premium/luxe may require haptic validation because feel is described as similar, not identical. Barrier/filling limitation notes: optional EVOH and HDPE/rHDPE shoulders with HDPE or PP low-profile closures. Use in screening: place downgauged PE/coex tubes into later evaluation. Limitations/caution: not a full formula/filling-line matrix.
Source name: Lightweight Tube — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/tubes/lightweight-tube

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s Mono PE Tube PDF explains the recyclability problem with PE tubes using PP caps and positions HDPE caps as functionally equivalent and visually similar, enabling mono-material PE tubes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: mono-PE tube with HDPE cap/closure. Tube format relevance: PE tubes, flip-top caps, screw caps, PE shoulders. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a numeric weight reduction source; relevant because closure material choice affects both material system and recyclability. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to recyclable cosmetic and personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market, masstige, and premium where HDPE cap appearance meets expectations. Recyclability/consumer limitation notes: PP caps seal well and look good but create mixed-material tubes; HDPE caps are positioned as high quality and well sealing. Use in screening: prioritize PE sleeves with HDPE caps for recyclable mono-material tube systems when seal testing confirms performance. Limitations/caution: supplier PDF; brand-specific closure feel and torque validation still needed.
Source name: Mono PE Tube — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/pdf?id=9813

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s recycled tube platform shows PCR-rich tube bodies can coexist with cosmetic relevance, thin walls, recyclable rHDPE closures, optional EVOH barrier, and decoration options.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: PCR PE/rPE tubes with rHDPE shoulder/closure options. Tube format relevance: recycled-content tubes with 0.35/0.40/0.50 mm wall thickness, optional EVOH, decoration options. Weight/material reduction evidence: supports PCR plus thin-wall options rather than treating recycled content separately from lightweighting. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: references cosmetic/skincare suitability and quality for cosmetic products. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass and masstige skincare/personal care; premium if appearance consistency and decoration pass. Barrier/decoration limitation notes: appearance consistency, hot-foil/print quality, and barrier needs should be checked case by case. Use in screening: include PCR PE tubes as mainstream candidates with performance validation. Limitations/caution: not a full independent performance dataset.
Source name: RECYCLED (PCR) tube — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/tubes/recycled-tube

Summary of what was found: EPL’s Platina shows recyclable laminated tubes can retain barrier performance: the laminate uses less than 5% barrier resin, meets stringent APR HDPE guidance, and is positioned as avoiding the usual trade-off between recyclability and shelf life.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: recyclable HDPE laminate tube with low barrier-resin content. Tube format relevance: laminate tubes for personal care, toiletries, and cosmetics. Weight/material reduction evidence: source-reduction and recyclable laminate route rather than sleeve downgauging; uses less than 5% barrier resin. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for applications needing barrier plus recyclability. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if aesthetics and haptics pass. Barrier/recyclability limitation notes: designed to preserve barrier properties, product stability, and shelf life while remaining APR-recognized in HDPE streams. Use in screening: shortlist recyclable HDPE laminate tubes where simple mono-layer PE is insufficient. Limitations/caution: supplier platform, brand-specific product stability still required.
Source name: Platina — EPL — https://www.eplglobal.com/product/platina/

Summary of what was found: EPL’s r-Platina extends the laminated-tube route by combining recyclability with higher PCR content and thinner laminates: 250μ and 300μ options with up to 50% PCR and 60% PCR respectively, plus RecyClass A certification and premium-performance positioning.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: recyclable PCR laminate tubes. Tube format relevance: 250 micron and 300 micron laminate tube constructions. Weight/material reduction evidence: thinner laminate options and high PCR content; up to 50% PCR in 250μ and up to 60% PCR in 300μ. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: applicable to cosmetics, oral care, baby care, and household products. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass and premium where PCR laminate aesthetics and performance satisfy brand needs. Barrier/decoration limitation notes: supplier claims premium performance and aesthetics; validate decoration, squeeze feel, and formula compatibility. Use in screening: screen as recyclable laminate plus PCR plus downgauging route. Limitations/caution: supplier claim and product-specific validation needed.
Source name: r-Platina — EPL — https://www.eplglobal.com/product/r-platina/

Summary of what was found: Berry’s Slimline closure launch confirms closure lightweighting is a distinct active route: the range is described as one of the lightest on the market, available in PE/PP or food-safe rPE/rPP, with mono-material pairing possible.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight low-profile tube closures. Tube format relevance: closures for skincare, body wash, hair care, and pharma tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: directional lightweight design and GHG savings versus traditional caps, but no gram delta. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong because cap reductions scale in high-volume personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; premium if closure feel and visual quality pass. Recyclability limitation notes: virgin or recycled PE/PP options allow material matching with tube materials for mono-material packs. Use in screening: treat closure lightweighting as a stand-alone workstream. Limitations/caution: no quantified cap weight reduction.
Source name: Berry Launches New Lightweight Tube Closure Solution — Berry Global — https://www.berryglobal.com/en/news/articles/berry-launches-new-lightweight-tube-closure-solution

Summary of what was found: Aptar positions Purity Lite as the first low-profile tube top in North America and says it becomes a lightweight, recyclable mono-material solution when paired with HDPE tubes; Aptar emphasizes reducing weight and minimizing extra materials.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: low-profile tube top/closure system. Tube format relevance: HDPE tubes and closures/tops. Weight/material reduction evidence: positioned as lightweight and material-minimizing but not quantified. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: closure determines consumer experience and recycling compatibility in personal-care tubes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market, masstige, and selected premium if top design is acceptable. Recyclability limitation notes: mono-material when paired with HDPE tubes; also reduces extra materials such as liners and shrink wrappers. Use in screening: include low-profile mono-material closure systems in shortlist and follow with mass, torque, seal, and consumer-use validation. Limitations/caution: strategic source rather than highly quantitative.
Source name: Enabling Brands to Achieve Sustainability Targets and Unlock Growth Opportunities — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/enabling-brands-to-achieve-sustainability-targets-and-unlock-growth-opportunities

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top is a quantified tube-system lightweighting example: by merging head and cap into one part, it claims over 80% weight reduction versus standard head+cap and 55% reduction for the full tube, while remaining HDPE mono-material and ISTA-6 e-commerce ready.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: integrated tube head/cap lightweight system. Tube format relevance: D50/200 ml style cosmetic/personal-care tubes with thin-wall sleeve plus integrated top. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 80% weight reduction versus standard head+cap and 55% full-tube reduction versus standard tube in cited example. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: shows tube lightweighting can come from closure/shoulder architecture, not sleeve alone. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market and selected premium where visible closure redesign is acceptable. Barrier/filling/e-commerce limitation notes: HDPE mono-material and ISTA-6 e-commerce readiness are noted; classic cap aesthetics may be a barrier. Use in screening: evaluate tubes by full system: sleeve plus shoulder/head/cap. Limitations/caution: proprietary design and visible pack architecture change.
Source name: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top range - Less plastic for a reduced carbon footprint — Cosmetics Business — https://cosmeticsbusiness.com/alb-a-s-ecofusion-top-range-less-plastic-for-a-reduced-carbon-footprint-209313

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal’s announcement with Albéa provides major-beauty-company proof for paper-based tube development, stating that the first cosmetic tube integrating certified paper-based material was designed to replace the majority of plastic currently used in such tubes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: paper-based cosmetic tube development. Tube format relevance: face and body care tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: designed to replace the majority of plastic currently used in such tubes, but no precise gram reduction. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strategic beauty-industry proof that paper/plastic hybrid tubes are being developed. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe, natural-positioned beauty, and selected personal care where paper tactility adds value. Barrier/filling limitation notes: announcement lacks technical details on barrier, filling-line behavior, or scale economics. Use in screening: keep paper/plastic hybrid tubes on longlist but not default mainstream shortlist. Limitations/caution: strategic announcement, not a detailed performance case.
Source name: L’Oréal and Albéa launch the first cosmetic tube integrating certified paper-based material — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/news/commitments/loreal-and-albea-launch-the-first-paper-based-cosmetic-tube/

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s PaperX gives a quantified paper-hybrid example for cosmetics: 80% FSC paper, 46% less plastic than standard COEX tubes, 24% lower CO2, 300-micron wall, EVOH/coating barrier, and compatibility with standard sealing equipment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: paper-hybrid cosmetic tube with barrier layers. Tube format relevance: 300-micron fiber-based laminate tubes for cosmetics/personal care. Weight/material reduction evidence: 46% less plastic than standard COEX tubes and 24% lower CO2. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: meaningful for premium/natural beauty and selected personal-care products where tactile differentiation matters. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/natural-positioned conditional route; mass-market possible only if cost and line compatibility work. Barrier/filling limitation notes: uses EVOH plus coating barrier, PP shoulder/closure, and standard sealing equipment; paper-content claims should not be confused with full paper-stream simplicity. Use in screening: evaluate carefully as a differentiated lightweight route. Limitations/caution: still relies on plastic layers and plastic shoulder/cap in this version.
Source name: PaperX Tube — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/tubes/paperx

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s PaperX FiberTop pushes paper further by moving fiber into body, shoulder, and cap, with about 85% fiber overall, up to 87% less plastic, EVOH in body/shoulder, and standard filling/end-sealing compatibility, but it is still presented as an emerging pilot path.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: fiber-forward tube with paper body, shoulder, and closure. Tube format relevance: paper-based tubes for cosmetics/personal care. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 87% less plastic and up to 65% lower emissions versus standard Polyfoil or COEX tubes; technical highlights include around 80% paper body, 85% fiber shoulder, and 95% fiber cap. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: high-interest innovation track for brands seeking paper tactility and major plastic reduction. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium innovation/future-route first, not default mass-market rollout. Barrier/filling limitation notes: includes thin EVOH barriers and is described as compatible with standard filling and end-sealing, but is in pilot/industrialization stage. Use in screening: place in future-route/premium innovation bucket. Limitations/caution: not yet safest near-term high-volume route.
Source name: PaperX FiberTop — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/tubes/paperx-fibertop

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s Timeless PCR PBL is an example of a lighter laminate tube with recycled content: around 30% recycled material in total, 60% recycled content in the sleeve, 17% lower carbon footprint versus other recycled tubes, and suitability for shower, shampoo, and sun-care formulas due to light/moisture barrier.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: PCR plastic-barrier laminate tubes. Tube format relevance: laminate tubes for shower, shampoo, and sun-care products. Weight/material reduction evidence: uses less material and has 17% lower carbon footprint versus other recycled tubes; around 30% recycled material overall and 60% recycled sleeve content. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: strong for personal care and skincare subcategories that need better product protection than a basic mono-layer tube. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige transitional route. Barrier limitation notes: offers high light barrier and superior moisture barrier; pure mono-material or paper-heavy options may not satisfy same protection needs. Use in screening: treat as practical transitional format where high barrier and recycled content matter. Limitations/caution: not necessarily the final optimum for recyclability.
Source name: Timeless: PCR Laminate Tubes for Cosmetics by Albéa — Global Cosmetic Industry — https://www.gcimagazine.com/packaging/news/21863240/albea-timeless-pcr-post-consumer-recycled-laminate-tubes-for-cosmetics-by-albea

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s aluminum-barrier discussion is a necessary caution source: ABLs are difficult to recycle in current systems, but mono-material alternatives will not eliminate ABL entirely because aluminum barriers are still needed for the most stringent barrier applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: aluminum-barrier laminate tubes and substitution limits. Tube format relevance: ABL tubes and mono-material barrier alternatives. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a weight-saving source; informs caution around composite-heavy structures. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: relevant to products needing volatile retention, stringent barrier, or regulated ointment/cream-like performance. Premium versus mass-market fit: ABL should be flagged as heavier/harder-to-recycle but not automatically rejected when barrier needs are critical. Recyclability/barrier limitation notes: ABL generally cannot be industrially separated/recycled in current facilities, but may remain necessary for the most stringent barrier applications. Use in screening: treat ABL as substitution candidate where feasible, but retain it in special barrier-required cases. Limitations/caution: supplier perspective and barrier need should be validated product by product.
Source name: Closing the loop with aluminum barrier tubes — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.neopac.com/en/news-events/closing-the-loop-with-aluminum-barrier-tubes

Summary of what was found: PPC’s grade overview gives a useful baseline for beauty cartons: SBS is premium white board for cosmetics and high-end retail packs; CUK/kraft is strength-led; CRB is recycled and value-oriented; chipboard is used for rigid/setup luxury goods boxes including cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: SBS, CUK, CRB, and chipboard/setup box board. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and rigid boxes. Weight/material reduction evidence: no quantified reduction, but it identifies rigid/setup boxes made from thick chipboard as a heavier format risk area and frames folding-carton grades as alternatives. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: SBS is identified for cosmetics; rigid boxes are identified for small luxury goods including cosmetics. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe aligns with SBS and rigid chipboard boxes; mainstream aligns with CRB and CUK depending on strength and appearance. Limitation/caution: classification source rather than quantified downgauging case; use as baseline taxonomy.
Source name: 4 Types of Paperboard — Paperboard Packaging Council — https://paperbox.org/4-types-of-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging separates Consumer Beauty from Luxury Beauty: mass-market beauty focuses on shelf impact, while luxury beauty emphasizes premium finishes, structural design, anti-counterfeit embellishments, gift boxes, inserts, windows, and special effects.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: beauty folding cartons and gift-box systems using paperboard plus finishing layers. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: standard folding cartons, gift boxes, inserts, windows, metallic/tactile finishes. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a grammage source, but explains why premium cartons often accumulate extra material through structure and finishes, while consumer beauty uses scalable carton formats. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, fragrances, skincare, cosmetics, and hair color are directly covered. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong evidence for splitting luxury and consumer tracks. Limitation/caution: solutions page, not technical board-spec page; pair with grade and recyclability sources.
Source name: Beauty Packaging — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/markets/beauty/

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging confirms SBS/bleached paperboard as a bright-white, high-print-performance board for high-end beauty and personal care cartons, with strong converting performance for die-cutting, foil stamping, embossing, folding, and gluing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: solid bleached sulfate/bleached paperboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: high-end folding cartons and branded outer packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a downgauging case; establishes why SBS remains common in premium beauty due to white shade, graphics, advanced finishing, and converting performance. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty and personal care are explicit applications. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe incumbent board baseline against which lighter FBB alternatives should be tested. Limitation/caution: supports appearance/finish need, not automatic lightweighting suitability.
Source name: Bleached Paperboard — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/packaging-materials/paperboard/bleached-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: Holmen’s Invercote G shows how premium SBB/SBS boards are engineered for prestige cartons: ultra-smooth, triple-coated, high whiteness, and suitable for foil, film lamination, embossing, and aroma-sensitive products.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: premium SBB/SBS cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: prestige folding cartons for luxury cosmetics, fragrance, and high-end packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: used as heavier-feature baseline rather than lightweight route; shows why premium beauty often remains with coated SBB/SBS because the substrate supports sophisticated imagery, foil/film lamination, and sensory quality. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: prestige packaging and aroma-sensitive products are explicit. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: film lamination and luxury finishes can raise material intensity and recyclability complexity if overused.
Source name: Invercote G Technical Data Sheet — Holmen Board & Paper — https://www.holmen.com/en/board-and-paper/products/paperboard/invercote/invercote-g/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s luxury-carton page reinforces that premium beauty and perfume cartons often prioritize tactile feel and demanding finishes, including velvet-like uncoated SBS, coated SBS, embossing, foil stamping, metallized foils, and high-end converting.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: luxury SBS boards, coated and uncoated. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: premium folding cartons for beauty and fragrances. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a downgauging source; explains why lightweighting luxury cartons can be difficult because premium value is often built through white boards, coatings, velvet-touch surfaces, embossing, foils, and two-sided coatings. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, personal care, and perfumes are explicit. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: use as filter for routes that may compromise premium haptics or decoration quality if downgauged aggressively.
Source name: Coated and uncoated carton boards for luxury packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/carton-boards-for-luxury-packaging

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board explains FBB lightweighting through a three-layer structure: chemical pulp outer layers plus a BCTMP/high-yield middle layer that delivers high caliper, bulk, and stiffness at lower weight; beautycare is listed as a core end use.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight fresh-fibre folding boxboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: cosmetic folding cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: source states lightweight folding boxboards consume less raw material, reduce transport weight, and produce less waste; lower weight is tied to three-layer structure and high-yield pulp middle layer. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beautycare is named. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for both, especially where brands want premium printability without defaulting to heavier SBS. Limitation/caution: barrier-coated grades should only be used where barrier performance is actually required.
Source name: Folding boxboard — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/metsaboard/explore-paperboard/paperboard-as-packaging-material/folding-boxboard/

Summary of what was found: Holmen’s Incada Exel describes a fresh-fibre FBB with lightweight construction, high stiffness, low density, high-quality printing, varnishing, and high-speed conversion, making it a candidate premium-but-lighter alternative to heavier bleached boards.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight FBB. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: quality folding cartons for cosmetics and high-end applications. Weight/material reduction evidence: low density plus high stiffness; described as lightweight and optimized for visual impact and conversion. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: Holmen’s Incada family is positioned for cosmetics, and Exel is its premium FBB member. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong where premium appearance is needed but full SBS/SBB may be over-specified. Limitation/caution: still a premium-grade board; check whiteness, finishing depth, and fragrance-carton expectations before substitution.
Source name: Incada Exel Technical Data Sheet — Holmen Board & Paper — https://www.holmen.com/en/board-and-paper/products/paperboard/incada/incada-exel/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s Performa family is positioned for premium folding cartons in beauty and personal care, with lightweight GC1/GC2 options; Performa Light is its lightest-weight GC2 grade, while Performa White and Nova offer high stiffness and reduced weight for fragrances, cosmetics, and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: high-bulk/high-stiffness lightweight FBB for premium cartons. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: premium beauty, fragrance, and personal-care folding cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: multilevel board structures provide higher strength and stiffness at lower weight; Performa Nova is described as engineered to carry more with less. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, personal care, fragrances, and cosmetics are named uses. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium route for preserving print and surface quality while reducing reliance on heavier SBS specifications. Limitation/caution: selection must be linked to actual decoration needs; heavy lamination or deep foil/embossing may limit one-for-one interchangeability.
Source name: Premium folding carton boards for packaging / Performa by Stora Enso — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons ; https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/performa

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s Tambrite is described as a packaging workhorse for everyday necessities, with high stiffness, material efficiency, and an ideal stiffness-to-weight ratio; a customer example reports a 33% basis-weight reduction when switching from recycled board to Tambrite.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: GC2 folding boxboard optimized for efficiency and runnability. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: scalable cartons where cost, line performance, and adequate appearance matter more than prestige extremes. Weight/material reduction evidence: 33% lower basis weight versus a recycled-board baseline while improving visual characteristics and packing-line efficiency. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: direct beauty references are limited, but it is a strong analog for personal-care and mass-market secondary cartons. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market/consumer beauty and personal care. Limitation/caution: transfer to cosmetics should still check whiteness, tactile expectations, and premium visual standards.
Source name: Tambrite - Folding Cartons — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/tambrite

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging’s recycled paperboard page positions CRB as a lightweight sheet for most consumer-product folding cartons, with 100% recycled fiber, double-clay-coated print surfaces, and grades whose brightness/whiteness can compete with bleached paperboard.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: CRB/WLC recycled paperboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: mass-market folding cartons and substitution where premium boards are over-specified. Weight/material reduction evidence: PaceSetter and Conquest CRB grades are described as lightweight sheets with print/performance benefits. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: wide consumer folding-carton use is relevant to personal care and accessible beauty. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market; selected grades may be useful where recycled content is desired without losing too much brightness/whiteness. Limitation/caution: check fragrance-carton purity, premium whiteness, embossing, and foil requirements before substituting premium board.
Source name: Recycled Paperboard — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/packaging-materials/paperboard/recycled-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: MM’s WLC page confirms that coated recycled board/white-lined chipboard is a mainstream recycled-cartonboard option and that MM markets it into cosmetics and personal care, not only household categories.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: WLC/CRB recycled cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons for cosmetics and personal care plus other consumer categories. Weight/material reduction evidence: source demonstrates availability and suitability rather than quantified downgauging. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: cosmetics and personal care are explicitly named. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and selected mid-tier products; useful in cost-led and recycled-content tracks. Limitation/caution: pair with appearance and printability qualification for brands needing high whiteness or luxury finishes.
Source name: Recycled Cartonboard (WLC Board) — MM Board & Paper — https://mm.group/board-paper/products/cartonboard-wlc/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s CKB page shows coated unbleached kraft as a strong, lightweight, material-efficient route and documents a customer case reaching lower basis weight and 15% packaging-weight savings; CKB Nude adds a natural brown premium look without conventional plastic barriers.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: CUK/CNK-style kraft-back cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: strength-led folding cartons and natural-look premium cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: virgin kraft-fiber strength is linked to material efficiency and low weight; customer case quotes 15% packaging-weight saving from lower basis-weight solution. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: not cosmetic-specific, but natural brown reverse and premium natural-look positioning fit clean beauty, apothecary, and eco-premium brands. Premium versus mass-market fit: both; mass-market where strength is needed at lower basis weight and premium where natural kraft aesthetics are acceptable. Limitation/caution: unsuitable where ultra-white luxury surface is required inside and out.
Source name: CKB - Folding Cartons — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/ckb

Summary of what was found: Walki’s barrier-board page shows a route for reducing plastic lamination complexity in cartons: fibre-based boards with tailored coatings that preserve printability/converting and can reduce or eliminate inner bags or liners while maintaining paper-stream recyclability in many cases.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: barrier-coated fibre-based folding-carton boards. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons needing moisture, grease, oxygen, aroma, or light protection, and beauty cartons needing limited resistance to staining or migration from oily products. Weight/material reduction evidence: structures are designed to reduce or eliminate additional inner bags or plastic liners and simplify packaging structures while maintaining performance. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: mostly food/household oriented, but material logic transfers to secondary beauty packs relying on laminated or complex substrates. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and premium where barrier performance is needed but full plastic lamination is undesirable. Limitation/caution: film, foil, and metallized laminations should not automatically be treated as preferred lightweight/recyclable routes.
Source name: Barrier Boards for Folding Cartons | Recyclable Fibre-Based Packaging | WalkiPack — Walki — https://www.walki.com/products/walki-pack-barrier-boards-folding-cartons/

Summary of what was found: AF&PA’s recyclability guidance shows that water-soluble inks/adhesives, clay coatings, and varnish coatings are generally easier for mills, while hot-melt adhesives, laminated foils, stamped foils, metallized foils, and wet-strength resins are more likely to create recycling challenges.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: inks, adhesives, coatings, foils, windows, and non-fiber elements on paperboard cartons. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: bleached paperboard cartons, recycled/unbleached boxboard cartons, and carrier stock cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a basis-weight source; critical for screening because lighter cartons can become less circular if they rely on problematic non-fibre elements. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to beauty cartons using foils, windows, and decorative effects. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium cartons are the main exposure area due to specialty finishes; mass-market can usually stay closer to easier-to-recycle coating and adhesive systems. Limitation/caution: recycling challenge does not automatically mean non-recyclable; mill capability varies.
Source name: Design Guidance for Recyclability for Paper-based Packaging — American Forest & Paper Association — https://www.afandpa.org/sites/default/files/2021-08/AFPADesignGuidanceforRecyclability_FINAL_031621.pdf

Summary of what was found: 4evergreen’s guideline gives carton-design guidance: avoid full-surface metallization; PP/PET metallized laminates and PET metallized films can disrupt NIR sorting; strongly attached windows should be avoided; detachable, thin, lightweight windows are preferred. It links design to PPWR weight-minimization and recyclability expectations.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: decorative metallization, metallized laminates, films, and windows on fibre-based packaging. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and fibre-based composite packaging. Weight/material reduction evidence: most useful as a filter for decorative/material choices that add non-fibre layers and impair sorting even if fibre content remains dominant; recommends thin lightweight detachable windows where needed. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to fragrance and premium skincare cartons using metallic effects and windows. Premium versus mass-market fit: main risk area is premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: recommendations are based on expert opinion and recyclability behavior depends on chemistry/formulation, so case-specific testing remains necessary.
Source name: Circularity by Design Guideline for Paper-based Packaging — 4evergreen — https://4evergreenforum.eu/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/4evergreen-Circularity-by-Design-Version-4-February-2026.pdf

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s packaging-performance page makes a key screening point: carton lightweighting is not simply lower gsm; it means achieving required stiffness and structural performance at lower grammage, and final packaging performance must be validated through methods such as box-compression testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: stiffness-to-weight optimization and packaging performance testing logic. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and boxes across the value chain. Weight/material reduction evidence: lightweighting is defined as lower grammage with maintained stiffness/structural performance; thickness, bulk, and grammage alone do not show finished package behavior. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: directly transferable to cosmetic cartons where downgauging risks crushed corners, poor shelf appearance, or weak stacking. Premium versus mass-market fit: both.
