Packaging format relevance: sachets, stand-up pouches, bundle packs, and refill-type flexibles. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: flexible PE pouches are inherently lower-material formats than rigid bottles/jars, and PCR content further reduces virgin polymer demand. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: page references lotions, scrubs, body washes, and deodorant wet wipes. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong mass-market and refill/reserve-pack route; conditional premium relevance if used as refill-only or back-of-house reserve pack. Limitation notes: source does not quantify PCR percentage or barrier trade-offs; sealing, fragrance retention, and leak performance must still be screened.
Source name: Beauty Packaging — Huhtamaki — https://www.huhtamaki.com/en/flexible-packaging/market-segments/home-and-personal-care/beauty/

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s Color Code PCR fragrance pump contains up to 67% mechanical PCR and is positioned as the first fragrance pump with PCR content that still preserves a strong customization/aesthetics offer.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: PCR dispensing component. Packaging format relevance: fragrance pumps and dispensing systems. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: supports virgin-resin reduction in a high-complexity component that is normally difficult to convert; not a direct lightweighting claim, but relevant because pumps often constrain sustainable material adoption. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: direct fragrance-packaging example. Premium versus mass-market fit: especially important for premium/luxe because fragrance pumps are appearance-sensitive and customization-sensitive. Limitation notes: verify color consistency, actuator performance, and end-of-life recyclability at full-pack level.
Source name: Aptar Beauty Introduces the First Fragrance Pump Available with PCR Content — Aptar Beauty — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/aptar-beauty-introduces-first-pcr-fragrance-pump

Summary of what was found: Berry’s CleanStream process is designed to produce contact-sensitive mechanically recycled PP suitable for cosmetics/personal care, with testing for odor, taint, and low migration, opening opportunities in Europe for cosmetics and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mechanically recycled PP / PCR PP for sensitive applications. Packaging format relevance: PP jars, tubs, closures, and other contact-sensitive beauty packs. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: virgin-material-reduction enabler for packs already using lightweight PP; important where recycled PP quality has historically constrained use. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: source explicitly discusses contact-sensitive cosmetics and personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market jars/tubs/closures; premium fit depends on color/clarity expectations. Limitation notes: improves circularity, but application-specific appearance, odor, and processing validation are needed; high-purity PCR PP requires substantial sorting/decontamination infrastructure.
Source name: CleanStream – The Future for Contact-Sensitive Mechanically Recycled Plastic — Berry Global / Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/cleanstream-the-future-for-contact-sensitive-mechanically-recycled-plastic

Summary of what was found: L’OCCITANE reports a pilot in which its Almond Shower Oil bottle used 100% recycled PET made through Loop’s depolymerization process; the brand says the new bottles look identical to the standard ones.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: chemically recycled / depolymerized PET. Packaging format relevance: PET shower-oil bottle. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: replaces virgin PET with recycled, virgin-quality PET in an existing lightweight bottle format. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: direct beauty example, not food/beverage. Premium versus mass-market fit: important for premium or appearance-sensitive packs because bottles are described as identical to standard bottles. Limitation notes: pilot / mini-series example rather than category-wide scale proof; does not claim lower bottle weight than standard PET.
Source name: L’OCCITANE en Provence and Loop Industries Pilot Project: A new bottle from 100% recycled PET using groundbreaking technology — Groupe L’OCCITANE — https://group.loccitane.com/group/news/loccitane-en-provence-and-loop-industries-pilot-project-new-bottle-100-recycled-pet

Summary of what was found: Braskem states that I’m green bio-based polyethylene is a drop-in substitute for fossil PE, with HDPE, LLDPE, and LDPE grades for rigid and flexible applications without new machinery.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: bio-based PE / bio-PE. Packaging format relevance: bottles, tubes, caps, films, and pouches using PE-family resins. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: no lighter-weight claim versus fossil PE; supports bio-PE as lower-fossil/renewable substitution that can be combined with already-light routes such as flexible pouches, lightweight tubes, or PE closures. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: relevant to hygiene/cleaning and transferable to beauty/personal-care PE formats. Premium versus mass-market fit: broad fit because drop-in conversion avoids pack redesign and preserves processing. Limitation notes: not evidence of automatic lightweighting; move forward only when paired with a lightweight format or downgauged design.
Source name: Enabling Brands in Their Sustainability Journey — Braskem — https://www.braskem.com/portal/imgreen/newimgreen/img/portifolio-pdf-en.pdf

Summary of what was found: SABIC’s Lumene case study shows a skincare jar and self-adhesive label made with certified renewable PP, reaching 97% certified renewable / mass-balance bio-based content while maintaining recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: bio-attributed / mass-balance PP. Packaging format relevance: skincare jars and labels. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: replaces fossil PP with renewable-content PP in an existing jar system; no lower weight claim, but shows renewable-content PP can be integrated without moving to heavier pack formats. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: direct cosmetics packaging application. Premium versus mass-market fit: suitable for premium skincare jars where brands need a renewable-content story without giving up PP processing/recyclability. Limitation notes: renewable-content substitution route, not weight reduction itself; screen as a credible lower-impact substitute or combine with lightweight jar architectures.
Source name: Cosmetic Containers with Self-Adhesive Label – Lumene Nordic Skincare and UPM — SABIC — https://www.sabic.com/en/Images/SABIC_LUMENE_PACKAGING_CASE_STORY_tcm1010-42874.pdf

Summary of what was found: European Bioplastics states that drop-in bio-based plastics such as bio-PE and bio-PET are chemically identical to fossil equivalents and can be mechanically recycled in the same streams without adapting existing infrastructure.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: drop-in bio-based PET and PE. Packaging format relevance: PET or PE cosmetic bottles, films, or closure systems. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: no weight claim; supports the rule that drop-in bio-based materials can accompany lightweight formats without disrupting recycling logic. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: directly relevant to beauty bottles, pouches, and tubes that already use PET/PE. Premium versus mass-market fit: broad fit across both segments because processing and recycling logic remain unchanged. Limitation notes: distinguish drop-ins from other innovative bioplastics; do not overextend to PLA/PHA or non-drop-in resins.
Source name: Mechanical Recycling — European Bioplastics — https://docs.european-bioplastics.org/publications/bp/EUBP_BP_Mechanical_recycling.pdf

Summary of what was found: RecyClass’s guideline for clear PET bottles lists PLA, PVC, PS, PETG, PC, and PBT in the main body as non-recyclable/high-risk for PET recycling, making them clear caution materials for beauty packs intended for the PET stream.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: design-for-recycling constraints for PET bottles. Packaging format relevance: clear cosmetic and personal-care PET bottles. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: no weight evidence; critical filter so non-drop-in or incompatible resins are not mistakenly shortlisted just because they seem sustainable or premium. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: directly relevant to clear skincare, haircare, and body-care bottles. Premium versus mass-market fit: especially important for premium clear packs where PETG or specialty transparent materials are often considered. Limitation notes: recyclability screen, not formula-compatibility assessment.
Source name: Transparent Clear & Light-blue PET Bottles — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/guidelines/clear-pet-bottles

Summary of what was found: RecyClass’s guideline for natural and white HDPE containers and tubes lists multilayer HDPE with PLA, PET, PETG, PVC, or PS as non-recyclable, while allowing defined barrier options such as EVOH at or below 6% under conditions.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: design-for-recycling constraints for HDPE/PE bottles and tubes. Packaging format relevance: PE/HDPE cosmetic bottles and tubes. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: no weight claim; prevents false-positive recommendations for bio-based or specialty multilayers that would damage PE-stream recyclability. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: directly relevant to shampoo, lotion, cream, and tube formats. Premium versus mass-market fit: especially relevant to mass-market PE packaging where recyclability at scale is a core filter. Limitation notes: recyclability permissions are conditional and do not replace product-compatibility and shelf-life testing.
Source name: Natural and White HDPE Containers and Tubes — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/guidelines/natural-white-pe-hd-containers-and-tubes/

Summary of what was found: The EU PPWR requires packaging to be recyclable, sets minimum recycled-content rules for plastic packaging, and says packaging must be designed to minimise weight and volume while maintaining functionality.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: regulatory screen affecting all recycled/bio-based packaging materials. Packaging format relevance: primary, secondary, tertiary, industrial, retail, and household packaging. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: directly relevant because it ties future material choices to recyclability, recycled content, and minimisation rules. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: covers cosmetic packaging as part of all packaging placed on the EU market and restricts some small single-use cosmetic/toiletry packs in hotels. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both, especially premium packs that may be over-built or use hard-to-recycle decoration/material combinations. Limitation notes: regulatory context, not evidence that any specific material is technically compatible with a cosmetic formula.
Source name: Packaging and packaging waste from 2026 — EUR-Lex / European Union — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/packaging-and-packaging-waste-from-2026.html

Summary of what was found: FDA’s recycled-plastics guidance says sensitive-contact recycled plastics require evidence on feedstock source controls, contamination prevention, decontamination efficiency, and migration, offering a conservative benchmark for screening PCR in cosmetic primary packs.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: regulatory/safety screening for recycled plastic. Packaging format relevance: direct-contact plastic bottles, jars, closures, and primary components using PCR. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: no direct weight claim; included because lightweighting recommendations should not advance recycled-content routes that cannot demonstrate adequate contamination control. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: food-contact guidance used as conservative benchmark for sensitive cosmetic primary-pack applications where odor, impurity, and migration matter. Premium versus mass-market fit: important for both, especially leave-on skincare, fragrance-adjacent packs, baby care, and sensitive formulations. Limitation notes: inferential benchmark rather than a cosmetics-specific statutory rule.
Source name: Recycled Plastics in Food Packaging — U.S. Food and Drug Administration — https://www.fda.gov/food/packaging-food-contact-substances-fcs/recycled-plastics-food-packaging

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging’s PaceSetter is a lightweight recycled paperboard sheet made from 100% recycled fiber and intended for folding-carton applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recycled paperboard / CRB. Packaging format relevance: secondary folding cartons for beauty and personal care. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: combines recycled content and lightweight sheet positioning. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: consumer-product folding cartons are directly relevant to cosmetics and personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest current fit is mass-market secondary packaging; premium use depends on print/finish requirements. Limitation notes: supports secondary-pack substitution and virgin-fiber reduction, not primary-pack formula compatibility.
Source name: PaceSetter Recycled Paperboard — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/products/pacesetter-recycled-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki states that molded fiber packaging’s main material is recycled fiber from recycled newspaper/used paper and that its molded fiber products are 100% recycled and recyclable back into the waste-paper stream.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recycled molded fiber / recycled fiber protective packaging. Packaging format relevance: inserts, trays, dividers, and secondary/tertiary protective formats. Weight/material/virgin-material reduction evidence: strong route for replacing virgin plastic, foam, or heavier multi-material protective elements in secondary/tertiary packaging where function is protection or presentation rather than direct formula contact. Cosmetic/personal-care relevance: transferable to beauty gift sets, transport inserts, and e-commerce protection. Premium versus mass-market fit: broad in mass-market protective packaging and can be upgraded for premium if finish quality is acceptable. Limitation notes: source is about molded fiber generally, not cosmetics-specific; premium-surface expectations and moisture/oil limitations still need screening.
Source name: Why choose molded fiber? — Huhtamaki — https://www.huhtamaki.com/en-za/fiber-packaging/sustainability/why-choose-molded-fiber/

Summary of what was found: Pregis frames packaging decisions around total cost of ownership rather than material purchase price alone.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: cost/commercial scoring logic. Packaging route relevance: applies across all lightweighting routes because a packaging TCO approach should optimize costs across materials, labor, operations, and supply chain using data-driven analysis. Cost implication: supports a cost per functional pack lens instead of raw resin or board price/kg. Cosmetic relevance: beauty lightweighting concepts should include material grams, labor, conversion, warehousing, and freight effects. How to use in the matrix: make raw material cost only one sub-factor inside a broader commercial score. Limitation: supplier-authored TCO framing, so use as decision logic rather than a quantified cross-market benchmark.
Source name: Maximize Efficiency with Total Cost of Ownership Insights — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/knowledge-hub/total-cost-of-ownership-unlocking-hidden-savings-with-packaging/

Summary of what was found: Pregis shows that under-protective packaging creates hidden costs through freight, replacement product, customer-service labor, and warehouse rework.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: damage/return-risk gate. Packaging route relevance: especially relevant when evaluating downgauging, lighter refills, thinner tubes, lighter shippers, or lower-mass inserts. Cost implication: even when a lightweight route saves pack grams, damage can erase savings because cost buckets include return freight, replacement product, customer-service labor, and warehouse labor. Cosmetic relevance: fragile beauty packs, pumps, and leak-prone liquids should not advance without protection assurance. How to use in the matrix: include a hard or heavily weighted penalty for routes that increase breakage, leakage, scuffing, or reship risk. Limitation: qualitative rather than quantified by format.
Source name: Impact of Damaged Goods on Profitability & Brand Image — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/knowledge-hub/putting-a-dent-in-profitability-the-real-impact-of-ineffective-packaging-and-damaged-products/

Summary of what was found: ISTA’s 3-Series protocols simulate the damage-producing motions, forces, and conditions of transport environments.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: performance-risk and validation gate. Packaging route relevance: necessary for lighter primary packs, lighter secondary cartons, molded-fiber inserts, pouches, and e-commerce tertiary packs. Cost implication: routes should not be scored as implementation-ready unless they can pass appropriate transport simulation. Cosmetic relevance: useful for evaluating whether reduced-weight packs remain robust during parcel/distribution handling. How to use in the matrix: add a validation requirement that any route affecting pack robustness must pass relevant ISTA-based transit testing before move-forward-now status. Limitation: protocol source explains testing framework, not beauty-specific pass/fail data.
Source name: Test Procedures — International Safe Transit Association — https://ista.org/test_procedures.php

Summary of what was found: The European Commission states that PPWR entered into force on 11 February 2025 and will generally apply from 12 August 2026.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: regulatory/compliance hard gate. Packaging route relevance: all packaging routes sold into the EU. Cost implication: routes that are hard to recycle, overly complex, or poorly aligned with future packaging-waste rules carry compliance risk that should be treated as cost/risk, not optional sustainability benefit. Cosmetic relevance: beauty packs often use mixed materials, decorative elements, and complex dispensers that may face higher compliance scrutiny. How to use in the matrix: create a PPWR-readiness gate before commercial scoring for EU-facing portfolios. Limitation: regulatory overview page; route-specific interpretation still needs material-by-material design checks.
Source name: Packaging waste — European Commission — https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/waste-and-recycling/packaging-waste_en

Summary of what was found: The EU Cosmetics Regulation states cosmetic products must be safe under normal or reasonably foreseeable conditions of use.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: primary-pack safety and compatibility hard gate. Packaging route relevance: all primary-pack lightweighting routes including thin-wall plastics, PCR plastics, mono-material pouches, tubes, pumps, and barrier-paper concepts. Cost implication: any route requiring deeper toxicology, migration, or compatibility work should carry higher implementation cost and longer qualification timing. Cosmetic relevance: safety is non-negotiable for formulas containing oils, fragrances, alcohols, actives, and other challenging ingredients. How to use in the matrix: no primary-pack route should move into scoring unless it can plausibly meet product safety obligations. Limitation: regulation sets the legal principle; detailed testing methods must be sourced separately.
Source name: Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 November 2009 on cosmetic products — European Union — https://health.ec.europa.eu/system/files/2016-11/cosmetic_1223_2009_regulation_en_0.pdf

Summary of what was found: Intertek says cosmetic packaging testing is vital because packaging can release harmful substances into product.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: compatibility/testing cost gate. Packaging route relevance: especially important for PCR plastics, new coatings, barrier papers, adhesives, inks, recycled inputs, and novel mono-material structures. Cost implication: routes that introduce packaging-interaction risk should carry extra test cost and longer qualification in the matrix. Cosmetic relevance: directly relevant to migration, leachables, and safety substantiation for primary packaging. How to use in the matrix: create a compatibility-risk multiplier or qualification-cost uplift for any route with higher extractables/leachables uncertainty. Limitation: service-provider source; explains why testing is needed but does not provide route-specific cost data.
Source name: Cosmetics Packaging Testing — Intertek — https://www.intertek.com/beauty-personal-care/cosmetics-packaging-testing/

Summary of what was found: Intertek explains that stability and compatibility studies should use final marketed packaging where possible and may include compatibility, leak, pump, drop, and transport-related tests.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: performance-risk scoring and approval-workload estimate. Packaging route relevance: primary packs, pumps, caps, tubes, pouches, and e-commerce-capable formats. Cost implication: testing workload can materially change route economics because compatibility studies, migration screening, climatic cycling, active-ingredient checks, leak testing, pump priming, dose delivery, cap opening force, thread integrity, and drop tests may be required. Cosmetic relevance: strongly relevant to formula-pack interaction and consumer-use performance. How to use in the matrix: score routes on expected qualification burden: low for near-drop-in substitutions, high for new materials or high-risk direct-contact systems. Limitation: testing menu is broad and indicative; actual program depends on formula and format.
Source name: Cosmetic Stability Testing — Intertek — https://www.intertek.com/beauty-personal-care/cosmetic-stability-testing/

Summary of what was found: 4evergreen’s 2026 design guideline says paper-based packs should still be tested for actual recyclability because behavior depends on constituents, integrated components, and final composition.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: design-for-recycling hard gate for fiber-based routes. Packaging route relevance: paperboard cartons, barrier-coated paper, composite paper packs, molded-fiber solutions, and paper-based hybrids. Cost implication: decorative layers, coatings, laminates, windows, and multi-material elements can create compliance or qualification risk and should be inside the matrix. Cosmetic relevance: beauty secondary packs often use coatings, foils, tactile finishes, and inserts that complicate paper-stream recyclability. How to use in the matrix: require evidence of recyclability compatibility for final converted pack construction, not just base substrate. Limitation: 4evergreen notes parts of the guidance remain based on expert opinion and ongoing test development.
Source name: Circularity by Design Guideline for Paper-Based Packaging — 4evergreen — https://4evergreenforum.eu/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/4evergreen-Circularity-by-Design-Version-4-February-2026.pdf

Summary of what was found: The 2026 APR–RecyClass cooperation report confirms foamed olefinic parts are compatible with HDPE and PP recycling, and EVA in closures is considered fully compatible with HDPE and PP recycling.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: recyclability gate for lightweight plastics and closures. Packaging route relevance: foamed PP/PE components, lightweight closures, liners, and rigid polyolefin packs. Cost implication: supports some lightweighting routes that might otherwise be filtered out as non-recyclable, improving their score when the base pack is HDPE or PP. Cosmetic relevance: useful for lighter overcaps, trays, inserts, or closure subcomponents in polyolefin systems. How to use in the matrix: do not reject foamed olefinic routes automatically; classify them as conditional on stream and format compatibility. Limitation: compatibility is recycling-stream specific and does not remove product/function testing.
Source name: APR-RecyClass Cooperation Report — RecyClass / Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://recyclass.eu/wp-content/uploads/2026/05/APR-RecyClass-Cooperation-Report-May-2026_Final-version.pdf

Summary of what was found: SABIC links thin-wall polypropylene packaging to shorter cycle times, higher productivity, top-load performance, and downgauging possibilities.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: cost/commercial scoring for thin-wall plastics. Packaging route relevance: thin-wall PP packs, caps, closures, tubs, jars, and similar rigid plastic parts. Cost implication: good flow and fast crystallization are linked to shorter cycle times and higher productivity, while top-load performance supports stacking and can lower transport/storage costs. Cosmetic relevance: makes thin-wall PP a strong candidate for high-volume mass-market packs where small resin reductions scale materially. How to use in the matrix: give thin-wall polyolefin routes a high near-term economic score when technically compatible with formula and pack architecture. Limitation: supplier solution page, not pack-specific cosmetic case study.
Source name: Thin Wall Packaging & Pails — SABIC — https://www.sabic.com/en/industries/packaging/industrial/thin-wall-packaging-and-pails

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board says folding boxboard can reduce packaging weight while keeping stiffness high.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: cost/commercial scoring for paperboard lightweighting. Packaging route relevance: secondary cartons, sleeves, inserts, and fiber-based beauty packs. Cost implication: lower basis weight at equivalent stiffness supports material reduction without necessarily compromising carton function. Cosmetic relevance: relevant for mass-market folding cartons and premium packs where board feel must remain acceptable. How to use in the matrix: high-bulk/high-stiffness-to-weight boards should score well for secondary-pack move-forward-now screening, subject to print/decor performance checks. Limitation: broad board-level evidence rather than beauty-specific carton qualification dataset.
Source name: Future-ready packaging — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/globalassets/metsa-board/documents/campaigns/metsa-board---future-ready-packaging-guide.pdf

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging explicitly separates consumer beauty from luxury beauty and links luxury packs to premium print finishes, structural design, and brand-elevating enhancements.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: segment-fit scoring. Packaging route relevance: secondary packaging in folding cartons, gift boxes, inserts, and decorative paperboard solutions. Cost implication: premium/luxe packs may accept higher decoration or material cost if they reinforce brand impact, while mass-market beauty is more shelf-impact and value-driven. Cosmetic relevance: directly supports different score weightings for premium/luxe versus mass-market tracks. How to use in the matrix: for luxury beauty, weight aesthetics, tactility, anti-counterfeit, and premium finish retention higher; for mass market, weight cost, scalability, and recyclability higher. Limitation: supplier portfolio framing rather than quantified consumer willingness-to-pay.
Source name: Beauty Packaging — Graphic Packaging International — https://www.graphicpkg.com/markets/beauty/

Summary of what was found: Amcor states its AmPrima refill pouches run at the same machine speeds as non-recyclable materials and can cut packaging weight by up to 90% versus rigid-container refill systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: commercial readiness and pack-level weight reduction for flexible refill routes. Packaging route relevance: mono-material PE refill pouches for home/personal care, with strong transfer relevance to selected cosmetic and personal-care refills. Cost implication: major weight reduction plus no machine-speed penalty makes the route commercially attractive in high-volume mass-market categories. Cosmetic relevance: strongest for shampoos, body wash, liquid soap, and refill-friendly formulations; weaker for prestige formats requiring rigid premium cues. How to use in the matrix: score mono-material refill pouches as a leading move-forward-now route where product and brand fit exist. Limitation: up-to-90% claim is route-specific and should not be generalized to every beauty application.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for Home and Personal Care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/emea/homeandpersonalcare

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s 2026 mono-PP wet-wipe flow wrap is designed to replace non-recyclable laminates, uses 35% PCR, showed comparable sealing/barrier/machinability in testing, and is compatible with existing packaging lines.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: implementation readiness for lightweight recyclable flexibles. Packaging route relevance: mono-PP recyclable flow-wrap structures for wipes and similar personal-care formats; conceptually useful for beauty sheet masks/wipes and selected flow-wrap applications. Cost implication: line compatibility lowers changeover risk and comparable machinability reduces disruption cost. Cosmetic relevance: strongest for personal-care wipes rather than broad liquid-beauty packaging. How to use in the matrix: classify mono-PP/mono-PE flexible structures as strong candidates when route has proven machinability and application does not require excessive premium aesthetics. Limitation: application-specific packaging; not a universal substitute for all laminate or rigid formats.
Source name: Mondi introduces 35% PCR re/loop FlowWrap for wet wipes — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2026/mondi-introduces-reloop-flowwrap-with-35-percent-post-consumer-recycled-content-for-wet-wipes-packaging/

Summary of what was found: Hoffmann Neopac’s mono-material barrier tube with HDPE cap is described as providing product protection while remaining compatible with existing HDPE recycling processes.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: conditional route screening for tubes. Packaging route relevance: lightweight mono-material barrier tubes and recyclable cap/tube systems. Cost implication: improves recyclability without abandoning tube functionality, which can make it commercially practical where laminate-to-mono-material conversion is feasible. Cosmetic relevance: relevant for creams, lotions, gels, and selected personal-care tubes. How to use in the matrix: tube conversions should score higher when they preserve barrier performance and use existing recycling streams. Limitation: tube/body/cap solution still requires formula compatibility and line qualification for each product.
Source name: Neopac Introduces Europe’s First Mono-Material Barrier Tubes with HDPE Caps to Receive Full RecyClass Technology & Product Approval — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.hoffmann-neopac.com/en/news-events/neopac-introduces-europes-first-mono-material-barrier-tubes-with-hdpe-caps-to-receive-full-recyclass-technology-product-approval

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s Advance collection uses full-plastic PP/PE pumps, is e-commerce capable, supports premium decoration, and is fully interchangeable with existing GSA finishing lines.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: conversion/tooling/line-change scoring plus premium-fit scoring. Packaging route relevance: lightweight pumps and sprayers for personal care and selected beauty applications. Cost implication: interchangeability with existing finishing lines reduces implementation burden; retained performance and decoration options reduce consumer-experience risk. Cosmetic relevance: important because pumps are often a recyclability and material-complexity bottleneck. How to use in the matrix: score all-plastic pump routes favorably when line-compatible, highly recyclable with common bottle resins, and performance-equivalent. Limitation: route is still more complex and likely more expensive than simple caps/closures.
Source name: Aptar Beauty Introduces Advance, A New Full-Plastic Pump Collection — Aptar Beauty — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/aptar-beauty-introduces-advance-a-new-full-plastic-pump-collection

Summary of what was found: PulPac says Dry Molded Fiber enables high-speed, low-cost production with close to zero material waste while also supporting premium finishes and textures.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: commercial readiness and segment fit for molded-fiber alternatives. Packaging route relevance: fiber inserts, trays, secondary protective components, and premium presentation elements. Cost implication: dry molded fiber is positioned as production-efficient and aesthetically acceptable, improving its score versus niche eco concepts that are slow or expensive. Cosmetic relevance: relevant for replacing heavier or less recyclable trays/inserts in secondary packaging. How to use in the matrix: classify molded-fiber insert/tray substitution as a strong secondary-pack candidate when surface-quality requirements are achievable. Limitation: source emphasizes technology potential and brand finish possibilities, not quantified cosmetic pack cost.
Source name: Sustainability doesn’t have to compromise beauty — PulPac — https://www.pulpac.com/story/sustainability-should-be-beautiful/

Summary of what was found: EPL management stated that FDA-approved PCR can cost about 2x virgin in its experience and that recycled materials are not automatically cheaper.
Detailed description of what was found: Decision factor covered: price-first filter caution for PCR/recycled routes. Packaging route relevance: PCR tubes and other contact-sensitive plastic components. Cost implication: recycled content should not be assumed to improve economics; PCR may raise cost even when it improves circularity. Cosmetic relevance: especially important for direct-contact beauty packaging where quality, odor, regulatory quality, and traceability requirements are tighter. How to use in the matrix: score PCR/bio-based routes on total functional-pack economics and qualification risk, not on a sustainable-therefore-viable assumption. Limitation: management commentary from one tube supplier, so treat as directional evidence rather than a universal market price benchmark.
Source name: EPL Limited Investor Meeting Transcript, 28 June 2022 — EPL Limited — https://www.eplglobal.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/EPL-Final-Transcript-28-06-2022.pdf
